**Balance Condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$

### Step 1 — Substitute into the Balance Equation

$$\left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right) \cdot R_4 = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}$$

**Logic:** The bridge is at null when the cross-product of impedances of opposite arms is equal.

### Step 2 — Multiply Both Sides by the Complex Conjugate of the Denominator

Multiply throughout by $(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$:

$$R_4 \left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)\left(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2\right) = R_2 R_3$$

**Logic:** This eliminates the complex denominator, leaving an explicit real-plus-imaginary expansion.

### Step 3 — Expand the Left-Hand Side

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(R_1 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}\right)\left(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2\right) &= R_1 (1) + R_1 (j\omega R_2 C_2) - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}(1) - \frac{j}{\omega C_1}(j\omega R_2 C_2) \\
&= R_1 + j\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{j}{\omega C_1} + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1} \\
&= \left(R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}\right) + j\left(\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Recall that $j \cdot j = j^2 = -1$, which converts the product of the two imaginary terms into a positive real number.

### Step 4 — Multiply by R₄ and Equate to R₂R₃

$$R_4 \left[\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}\right) + j\left(\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1}\right)\right] = R_2 R_3$$

### Step 5 — Separate Real and Imaginary Parts

For a complex number to equal a **purely real** quantity (R₂R₃), the **imaginary part must vanish**, and the **real parts must be equal**.

#### Real Part Equation:

$$\frac{R_3}{R_4} = \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

#### Imaginary Part Equation (Setting to zero):

$$\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1} = 0$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Imaginary Equation for ω

$$\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 = \frac{1}{\omega C_1}$$

**Logic:** Cross-multiply to bring the ω terms together.

$$\omega^2 R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2 = 1$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}}$$

### Step 7 — Derive Frequency f

Since $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}}$$

### Step 8 — Special Symmetric Case

When $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

- **Frequency simplifies to:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$
- **Resistance ratio simplifies to:** $\dfrac{R_3}{R_4} = 1 + 1 = 2$, hence $R_3 = 2R_4$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase of the RC arms at balance:** When $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ and $\omega = 1/(RC)$:
>
> $$Z_1 = R - \frac{j}{\omega C} = R(1 - j), \quad \phi_1 = -45^\circ$$
>
> $$Z_2 = \frac{R}{1 + j} = \frac{R(1-j)}{2}, \quad \phi_2 = -45^\circ$$
>
> **Both arms have the SAME phase angle of −45° at balance.** This is the key phasor insight.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example Walkthrough (Model Problem)

**Problem:** A Wien's Bridge has $R_1 = R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = 1\,\text{nF}$. Find the frequency of balance and the value of R₃ if R₄ = 5 kΩ.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify symmetric case:** $R_1 = R_2$ and $C_1 = C_2$, so use the simplified formula.

**Step 2 — Compute f:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 3 — Arithmetic evaluation:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi} = 15915.49\,\text{Hz} \approx 15.92\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute R₃:**

$$R_3 = 2 R_4 = 2 \times 5\,\text{k}\Omega = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $f \approx 15.92\,\text{kHz}$ and $R_3 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wien's Bridge Circuit Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC["AC Source"]:::source
    NODE_A["Node A"]:::junction
    NODE_B["Node B"]:::junction
    NODE_C["Node C"]:::junction
    NODE_D["Node D"]:::junction
    DET["Null Detector D"]:::detector
    R1["R1"]:::resistor
    C1["C1"]:::capacitor
    R2["R2"]:::resistor
    C2["C2"]:::capacitor
    R3["R3"]:::resistor
    R4["R4"]:::resistor

    SRC --- NODE_A
    SRC --- NODE_C
    NODE_A --- R1
    R1 --- C1
    C1 --- NODE_B
    NODE_B --- DET
    DET --- NODE_D
    NODE_D --- R3
    R3 --- NODE_A
    NODE_C --- R2
    R2 --- C2
    C2 --- NODE_B
    NODE_C --- R4
    R4 --- NODE_D

    classDef source fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detector fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resistor fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef capacitor fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Node A → Node B (Arm 1):** R₁ in series with C₁
- **Node C → Node B (Arm 2):** R₂ in parallel with C₂
- **Node A → Node D (Arm 3):** Pure R₃
- **Node C → Node D (Arm 4):** Pure R₄
- **Source** between A and C; **Detector** between B and D

---

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram at Balance (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VREF["Vs - Reference Phasor along +X axis"]:::ref
    I1["I1 - Current through Arm 1 Series RC"]:::current
    I3["I3 - Current through Arm 3 Pure R3 - In phase with Vs"]:::current
    V1["V1 - Voltage across Z1 Leads I1 by 45 deg"]:::voltage
    V3["V3 - Voltage across Z3 In phase with I3"]:::voltage
    V2["V2 - V1 equal V2 at BALANCE"]:::voltage
    V4["V4 - V3 equal V4 at BALANCE"]:::voltage
    PHASE["Both Z1 and Z2 share 45 deg phase lag at balance"]:::highlight

    VREF --> I3
    VREF --> V3
    I3 --> V3
    I3 --> I1
    I1 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V3 --> V4
    V2 --> PHASE
    V4 --> PHASE

    classDef ref fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef current fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef voltage fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef highlight fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the Phasor Diagram at Balance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Source voltage V_s** is taken as the reference phasor along the horizontal (+X) axis.
> 2. **I₃** (current through R₃) is **in phase** with V_s since R₃ is purely resistive.
> 3. **I₁** (current through R₁–C₁ series arm) leads V_s by some phase angle θ₁.
> 4. **V₁ = I₁ · Z₁** leads I₁ by 45° (at balance frequency).
> 5. **V₂ = I₂ · Z₂** also has the same 45° phase as Z₁ (key insight).
> 6. **V₃ = V₄** at balance, ensuring zero potential difference across the detector.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (Bridge Operation Sequence)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1 Apply AC Source Vs between nodes A and C"]:::step
    S2["Step 2 Current splits into two parallel branches Branch AB and Branch AD"]:::step
    S3["Step 3 Vary R3 until Detector reads ZERO"]:::step
    S4["Step 4 At null Vb equals Vd in both magnitude and phase"]:::step
    S5["Step 5 Two balance conditions get satisfied simultaneously"]:::step
    S6["Step 6 Read frequency from f = 1 over 2 pi RC"]:::step
    S7["Step 7 Read capacitance from C2 over C1 if C1 is known"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef step fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

This functional flow captures the **operational sequence** of the Wien's Bridge as it would be used in a laboratory setup for frequency measurement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Wien's Bridge. State any two of its applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Wien's Bridge** is an AC bridge used for the precise measurement of an unknown **frequency**, particularly in the audio frequency range (20 Hz to 20 kHz). It consists of a series RC arm, a parallel RC arm, and two pure resistive ratio arms.
>
> **Applications:**
> 1. **Frequency measurement** in audio communication systems and laboratories.
> 2. **Capacitance measurement** when the source frequency is precisely known.
> *(Alternative: Used as the frequency-selective feedback network in Wien Bridge Oscillators.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Write down the two balance conditions for a Wien's Bridge.**

**Model Answer:**

The two balance conditions are:

**1. Real Balance Condition (Resistance Ratio):**

$$\frac{R_3}{R_4} = \frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

**2. Imaginary Balance Condition (Frequency Equation):**

$$\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

For the symmetric case $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$, these reduce to $R_3 = 2R_4$ and $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the balance conditions for a Wien's Bridge, clearly showing the real and imaginary parts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the phasor diagram of a Wien's Bridge at balance and explain the significance of the −45° phase angle for both Z₁ and Z₂. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — State the general balance condition:**

For any four-arm AC bridge, the balance (null) condition is:

$$Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$$

**Step 2 — Write impedances of the four arms:**

$$Z_1 = R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}, \quad Z_2 = \frac{R_2}{1 + j\omega R_2 C_2}, \quad Z_3 = R_3, \quad Z_4 = R_4$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and cross-multiply by $(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2)$:**

$$R_4 \left(R_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}\right)(1 + j\omega R_2 C_2) = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 4 — Expand using $j^2 = -1$:**

$$R_4 \left[\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2 C_2}{C_1}\right) + j\left(\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 - \frac{1}{\omega C_1}\right)\right] = R_2 R_3$$

**Step 5 — Equate real and imaginary parts:**

- **Real part:** $\dfrac{R_3}{R_4} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} + \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$
- **Imaginary part:** $\omega R_1 R_2 C_2 = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_1}$

**Step 6 — Solve imaginary part:**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{1}{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2} \implies \omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

**Final Answer:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ and $R_3/R_4 = R_1/R_2 + C_2/C_1$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Phasor Diagram Description:**

Take the **source voltage V_s** as the reference phasor along the positive X-axis.

**Construction Steps:**

1. Draw V_s horizontally → reference axis.
2. Draw I₃ along V_s (in phase, since R₃ is purely resistive).
3. Draw V₃ = I₃R₃ along V_s (in phase with I₃).
4. The current I₁ in the series RC arm leads V_s by some angle.
5. The voltage V₁ across Z₁ leads I₁ by 45° at the balance frequency.
6. The voltage V₂ across Z₂ also makes a 45° angle with V_s.
7. At balance: V₁ = V₂ in magnitude and phase; V₃ = V₄ in magnitude and phase.
8. The detector voltage V_BD = 0.

**Significance of −45° Phase Angle:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At balance frequency $\omega = 1/(RC)$, both the series RC arm and the parallel RC arm exhibit **identical phase angle of −45°**. This is the **hallmark of Wien's Bridge balance** and is why the bridge is so elegantly frequency-selective. The phasor diagram visually confirms that V₁ and V₂ are co-phasor at null, satisfying the requirement that the bridge be in **phase balance** simultaneously with **magnitude balance**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the operation of the Wien's Bridge for the measurement of an unknown frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Wien's Bridge, $R_1 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$, and $C_2 = 1\,\text{nF}$. The bridge is balanced at a particular frequency. Find: (i) the balance frequency, and (ii) the value of R₃ if R₄ = 2 kΩ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Circuit Operation:**

1. The unknown frequency AC source is connected between nodes A and C.
2. The unknown frequency is $f_x$ Hz.
3. R₁ = R₂ = R are set to convenient values, and C₁ = C₂ = C are precision capacitors.
4. R₃ is varied (or R₄ is varied) until the detector reads zero.
5. At null, the unknown frequency is computed as $f_x = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
6. **Note:** In the practical version, R₁ and R₂ are often **ganged** together so that they change simultaneously, ensuring that $R_1 = R_2$ always holds.

**Sketch:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid circuit diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given:** $R_1 = R_2 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 1\,\text{nF} = 10^{-9}\,\text{F}$, $R_4 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**(i) Balance Frequency:**

Since $R_1 = R_2$ and $C_1 = C_2$, use the symmetric formula:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \frac{10^6}{2\pi \times 4.7} = \frac{10^6}{29.53}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 33{,}863\,\text{Hz} \approx 33.86\,\text{kHz}}$$

**(ii) Value of R₃:**

For the symmetric case, $R_3 = 2R_4$:

$$R_3 = 2 \times 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_3 = 4\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up ω and f:** Many students write the final answer in rad/s when the question asks for Hz. Always convert using $f = \omega / (2\pi)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the real balance condition:** The resistance ratio $R_3/R_4 = R_1/R_2 + C_2/C_1$ is **equally important** as the frequency equation. Examiners allocate separate marks for both.
> 3. **Sign errors with j:** The substitution $1/(j\omega C) = -j/(\omega C)$ is the most common source of mistakes. Practice it repeatedly.
> 4. **Drawing the phasor diagram without labels:** Every phasor must be clearly labeled with its symbol (V_s, I₁, V₁, V₂, etc.). Unlabeled diagrams fetch zero marks.
> 5. **Using Hay Bridge formula by mistake:** Hay and Wien bridges look similar but have **different** RC arm arrangements. Always verify the topology.
> 6. **Not stating units:** Writing "f = 1/(2πRC)" without specifying Hz is incomplete.
> 7. **Missing the −45° phase angle explanation:** Examiners love to ask for the significance of the phasor angle. A simple "both arms have the same phase" without explaining **why** (i.e., the equal RC time constants at the balance frequency) costs you 2–3 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Wien's Bridge** = AC bridge for **frequency measurement** in the **audio range**.
- [x] **Four arms:** Series-RC (Z₁), Parallel-RC (Z₂), Pure R₃, Pure R₄.
- [x] **Source** between A and C; **Detector** between B and D.
- [x] **General balance condition:** $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ — this is the **starting point** for every derivation.
- [x] **Two balance conditions** (memorize verbatim!):
  - $R_3/R_4 = R_1/R_2 + C_2/C_1$ (real)
  - $f = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$ (imaginary)
- [x] **Symmetric case** ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$): $R_3 = 2R_4$ and $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
- [x] **At balance, both Z₁ and Z₂ have phase angle = −45°** (key phasor insight).
- [x] **The bridge is frequency-sensitive** — null occurs at only one specific frequency.
- [x] **Convergence** to balance is slower than DC bridges; **ganged resistors** are used in practice.
- [x] **Applications:** Frequency measurement, capacitance measurement, Wien Bridge Oscillator, harmonic distortion analyzer.
- [x] **Compare with Hay Bridge** — Hay has a series R-L arm in series with a resistor, and is used for high-Q inductance measurement. Wien is purely RC and is for frequency.
- [x] **Inventor:** Max Wien, 1891.
- [x] **Always** show the phasor diagram for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- [x] **Units, units, units!** — Don't forget Ω, F, Hz, rad/s annotations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistive Transducers - Potentiometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistive Transducers - Potentiometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Resistive Transducer** is a primary sensing element that converts a physical measurand (most commonly linear or angular displacement) into a proportional change in electrical resistance, which can then be measured using suitable auxiliary circuitry.

A **Potentiometer** (also called a **Potentiometric Transducer** or simply a **"Pot"**) is a passive, variable-resistance, voltage-dividing type of resistive transducer. It consists of a uniformly wound resistive element (wire-wound, carbon-film, or conductive plastic) with a sliding contact (**wiper**) that moves along its surface in response to the mechanical displacement being measured. The displacement physically varies the effective length of the resistive path between the wiper terminal and one of the end terminals, producing a change in resistance and a corresponding change in the output voltage taken across the wiper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Potentiometers belong to the family of **Passive Transducers** because they require an external excitation voltage (typically a stable DC source) to produce an output signal. They cannot generate electrical energy on their own (unlike active transducers such as thermocouples or piezoelectric sensors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **volume-control knob on an old-fashioned stereo amplifier** or the **fuel-level indicator dial inside a car dashboard**.

- When you turn the knob or as the float rises with fuel, a small mechanical sliding contact (the wiper) physically moves along a long strip of resistive material.
- The further the wiper travels along the strip, the greater the resistance "sensed" between the wiper and one fixed end — and the higher the output voltage tapped from the wiper.
- So, a tiny mechanical motion (your finger turning the knob) is faithfully translated into a smoothly varying electrical signal.

In engineering language, the **knob / float** = the **displacement input**, the **resistive strip** = the **transducer element**, and the **voltage measured across the wiper** = the **electrical output**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Reference:**
> - SI unit of resistance: **Ohm (Ω)**
> - SI unit of displacement: **metre (m)**
> - Standard excitation voltage for laboratory potentiometers: **5 V DC to 15 V DC**
> - Typical resistance values: **100 Ω to 100 kΩ**

### 1.3 Classification of Potentiometers

| S.No | Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|------|----------------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Motion Pattern | **Linear Potentiometer** | Wiper travels along a straight resistive track; used for linear displacement. |
| 2 | Motion Pattern | **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometer** | Wiper rotates over a circular track; used for angular displacement (typically up to 360°). |
| 3 | Construction | **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | Made by winding resistance wire on a former; high resolution depends on the number of turns. |
| 4 | Construction | **Carbon-Film / Cermet Potentiometer** | A thin resistive film is deposited on a substrate; offers effectively infinite resolution. |
| 5 | Construction | **Conductive-Plastic Potentiometer** | Uses a plastic track loaded with conductive particles; long life, low noise. |
| 6 | Taper | **Linear Taper (Type A)** | Resistance change is proportional to displacement. |
| 7 | Taper | **Logarithmic / Audio Taper (Type B)** | Resistance change is logarithmic; used in audio volume controls. |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Linear Potentiometer — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Resistive Element:** A uniform resistance wire (e.g., constantan, nichrome, or karma alloy) is wound on a rectangular or cylindrical insulated former. The total resistance is denoted as $R_T$.
2. **End Terminals (T1 and T2):** The two ends of the resistive element are brought out as fixed terminals across which the **excitation voltage $V_s$** is applied.
3. **Wiper (Slider):** A movable contact slides over the resistance wire as the mechanical input (displacement $x$) is applied to it.
4. **Output Terminal (T3):** The wiper is connected to a third terminal from which the **output voltage $V_o$** is tapped.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The Voltage Divider Concept

A potentiometer operates on the **principle of the voltage divider** (also called the *Kelvin-Varley principle* in its multi-stage precision form).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the operation:** Ohm's Law states that $V = I R$. If a constant excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across the entire resistance $R_T$, a constant current flows through it. As the wiper moves, the resistance between the wiper and the lower terminal changes linearly with displacement. By Ohm's Law, the voltage drop across this portion is therefore directly proportional to the displacement.

### 2.3 Mathematical Formulation

Let:
- Total length of resistive track = $L$ (metres)
- Total resistance of track = $R_T$ (Ω)
- Resistance per unit length = $R_T / L$ (Ω/m)
- Displacement of wiper from one end = $x$ (metres)
- Excitation voltage applied across the full track = $V_s$ (V)
- Output voltage measured at the wiper = $V_o$ (V)

The resistance offered up to the wiper position is:

$$R_x = \frac{R_T}{L} \cdot x$$

By the voltage divider rule, the output voltage is:

$$V_o = \frac{R_x}{R_T} \cdot V_s = \frac{(R_T \cdot x / L)}{R_T} \cdot V_s = \frac{x}{L} \cdot V_s$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula that may appear in a KTU University Exam question from this topic. Memorise these with units and boundary conditions.

| S.No | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|------|--------------------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Resistance per unit length | $r = R_T / L$ | Ω/m | Assumes uniform winding |
| 2 | Output voltage (no-load) | $V_o = (x / L) \cdot V_s$ | V | Ideal voltage divider |
| 3 | Output voltage (loaded, with $R_L$) | $V_{o,L} = \dfrac{V_s \cdot (R_x \parallel R_L)}{R_T - R_x + (R_x \parallel R_L)}$ | V | Voltage divider with load |
| 4 | Sensitivity | $K = dV_o / dx = V_s / L$ | V/m | Constant for linear taper |
| 5 | Loading error (absolute) | $\varepsilon = V_o - V_{o,L}$ | V | Difference between ideal and loaded output |
| 6 | Loading error (% of FS) | $e_{\%} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{V_s} \times 100$ | % | Used in design specifications |
| 7 | Maximum loading error (at $x = L/2$) | $e_{max} = \dfrac{V_s}{4} \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_L + R_T}$ | V | Occurs at mid-stroke |
| 8 | Resolution (wire-wound) | $\text{Res} = 1/N$ | — | $N$ = total number of turns |
| 9 | Resolution (film type) | Theoretically **infinite** | — | Smooth, continuous resistance track |
| 10 | Power dissipated in element | $P = V_s^{\,2} / R_T$ | W | Must be less than rated wattage |
| 11 | Shaft rotation to voltage (rotary type) | $V_o = (\theta / \theta_{max}) \cdot V_s$ | V | $\theta$ = angular displacement |
| 12 | Sensitivity (rotary type) | $K_\theta = V_s / \theta_{max}$ | V/deg or V/rad | Constant for linear taper |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Application Example |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Automotive** | Throttle position sensor, accelerator pedal sensor, fuel level gauge. |
| **Industrial Automation** | Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position feedback, valve position indicator. |
| **Aerospace** | Flight-control surface position feedback, flap and rudder angle sensing. |
| **Robotics** | Joint-angle encoders (low-cost versions), gripper position feedback. |
| **Medical Instruments** | Infusion pump plunger position, ventilator bellows position. |
| **Audio Engineering** | Faders and volume knobs in mixing consoles. |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** | Crack-width and beam-deflection measurement using long-stroke linear pots. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of an Ideal (No-Load) Linear Potentiometer

**Statement:** Show that the output voltage of a linear potentiometer is directly proportional to the wiper displacement.

**Given:** A linear resistive track of length $L$, total resistance $R_T$, excited by a stable DC source $V_s$. The wiper is displaced by a distance $x$ from the reference (lower) terminal.

**Step 1 — Express the resistance up to the wiper:**

Since the resistance is uniformly distributed along the length $L$, the resistance per unit length is:

$$\frac{R_T}{L} \quad \text{(Ohms per metre)}$$

For a wiper displacement of $x$, the resistance between the lower terminal and the wiper is:

$$R_x = \frac{R_T}{L} \cdot x$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule:**

Because the resistance element is purely ohmic and the wiper draws no current (ideal no-load condition), the current through the track is constant:

$$I = \frac{V_s}{R_T}$$

The voltage drop across the portion $R_x$ is, by Ohm's Law:

$$V_o = I \cdot R_x = \left(\frac{V_s}{R_T}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{R_T}{L} \cdot x\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the expression:**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

$$V_o \propto x \quad \text{(linear relationship)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental transfer function** of an ideal linear potentiometer. It states that the output voltage is **linearly proportional to the mechanical displacement**, which is exactly the property that makes the device a useful displacement-to-voltage transducer.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity of a Linear Potentiometer

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the sensitivity of a linear potentiometer and state its physical significance.

**Given:** From the previous derivation, $V_o = (V_s / L) \cdot x$.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of output per unit change of input:**

$$K = \frac{dV_o}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the transfer function with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dx} = \frac{V_s}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the sensitivity is:**

$$\boxed{K = \frac{V_s}{L}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical significance:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sensitivity $K$ is the **slope of the straight-line calibration curve** ($V_o$ versus $x$). It tells us *how many volts of output we obtain for every metre of displacement*. To increase sensitivity, either increase the excitation voltage $V_s$ or reduce the active track length $L$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loading Error in a Linear Potentiometer

**Statement:** A real measurement system always draws some current from the wiper. Derive the expression for the resulting loading error.

**Given:** A load resistance $R_L$ is connected between the wiper terminal and the lower (ground) terminal. The excitation voltage $V_s$ is applied across $R_T$.

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit topology:**

The circuit consists of two series arms:
- Lower arm resistance: $R_x = (R_T / L) \cdot x$
- Upper arm resistance: $R_T - R_x = R_T(1 - x/L)$
- A load $R_L$ is connected in parallel with the lower arm $R_x$.

**Step 2 — Calculate the equivalent resistance of the lower section:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_x \cdot R_L}{R_x + R_L} = \frac{R_x \, R_L}{R_x + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider rule across the loaded network:**

$$V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_{eq}}{R_T - R_x + R_{eq}}$$

Substituting $R_{eq}$ and simplifying:

$$V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x \, R_L}{R_T \, R_L - R_x \, R_L + R_x \, R_L + R_x \, (R_T - R_x)}$$

$$V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x \, R_L}{R_T \, R_L + R_x \, (R_T - R_x)}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of the displacement ratio $\alpha = x/L$:**

Let $\alpha = x / L$, so $R_x = \alpha R_T$. Then:

$$V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{\alpha \, R_T \, R_L}{R_T \, R_L + \alpha R_T \cdot (R_T - \alpha R_T)}$$

$$V_{o,L} = V_s \cdot \frac{\alpha \, R_L}{R_L + \alpha R_T (1 - \alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — The loading error is the difference between ideal and loaded output:**

$$\varepsilon = V_o - V_{o,L} = \frac{\alpha \, V_s \, R_T (1 - \alpha)}{R_L + \alpha R_T (1 - \alpha)}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum loading error occurs when $\alpha = 1/2$ (mid-stroke):**

$$e_{max} = \varepsilon \bigg|_{\alpha = 1/2} = \frac{V_s \cdot R_T}{4 R_L + 2 R_T} \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 2 R_L / R_T}$$

If $R_L \gg R_T$, this simplifies to:

$$e_{max} \approx \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** To *minimise loading error*, the load resistance $R_L$ (input impedance of the next stage, e.g., voltmeter or ADC) must be at least **10 to 100 times larger** than the potentiometer's total resistance $R_T$. This is why potentiometers are always paired with high-impedance measuring instruments.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resolution of a Wire-Wound Potentiometer

**Statement:** Derive the resolution of a wire-wound potentiometer having $N$ turns of resistance wire.

**Given:** A wire-wound potentiometer has $N$ discrete turns of resistance wire. The total resistance is $R_T$.

**Step 1 — Resistance change per step:**

As the wiper moves from one turn to the next, the resistance changes by:

$$\Delta R = \frac{R_T}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Corresponding voltage step:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_s}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Resolution (the smallest measurable fractional input):**

$$\boxed{\text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Example:** If a wire-wound pot has $N = 1000$ turns, its resolution is $0.1\%$ of full scale — meaning the smallest displacement it can resolve is $L/1000$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference:** The resolution of a wire-wound pot is **finite and discrete**, whereas a film-type pot has a **theoretically infinite resolution** because its resistance varies smoothly without discrete steps. This is a high-yield exam comparison point.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — At-a-Glance Comparison

| Property | Wire-Wound Pot | Carbon-Film / Cermet Pot | Conductive-Plastic Pot |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Resolution | Finite ($1/N$) | Effectively infinite | Infinite |
| Life (cycles) | $\sim 10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$ | $\sim 10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ | $\sim 10^{7}$ to $10^{8}$ |
| Power rating | High (up to several watts) | Low (typically < 2 W) | Medium |
| Temperature coefficient | ±50 ppm/°C | ±200 to ±500 ppm/°C | ±400 ppm/°C |
| Cost | Low to Medium | Very Low | High |
| Noise | Higher (contact noise) | Low | Very Low |
| Best suited for | Heavy-duty industrial use | Consumer electronics | Precision instrumentation |

**Reading the diagram:**
- A non-electrical quantity (e.g., pressure) is first converted to mechanical displacement by a primary sensing element.

- The signal conditioner amplifies and filters the signal.
