## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*Define a capacitive transducer. List the three primary methods by which capacitance can be varied for measurement purposes.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A capacitive transducer is a passive transducer that converts a non-electrical physical quantity (such as displacement, pressure, or level) into a measurable change in electrical capacitance, based on the relation $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.

> **Three methods of varying capacitance:**
> 1. **Variation of plate separation (air gap) $d$** — used for small displacement/pressure measurement.
> 2. **Variation of effective overlapping area $A$** — used for linear and angular displacement.
> 3. **Variation of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$** — used for liquid-level and humidity measurement.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*State the general expression for the sensitivity of a single-plate variable air-gap capacitive transducer and explain why it is non-linear.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Sensitivity expression:** $S = dC/dd = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$

> **Reason for non-linearity:** Capacitance is *inversely proportional* to the gap $d$, giving a hyperbolic relationship $C \propto 1/d$. The second-order dependence on $d$ in the denominator causes the rate of change (sensitivity) to vary with operating point, producing non-linear output. Hence, the transducer is restricted to small displacements ($\Delta d \ll d$) where linearisation is valid.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the sensitivity of a **differential-type variable air-gap capacitive transducer**. Show that the differential configuration has twice the sensitivity of a single-plate design.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A differential capacitive transducer, used in a pressure gauge, has plates of area $A = 250 \text{ mm}^2$ and nominal air gap $d_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm}$. Calculate:
  (i) The nominal capacitance.
  (ii) The sensitivity of the differential arrangement.
  (iii) The change in differential capacitance for a plate displacement of $\Delta d = 0.05 \text{ mm}$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> Consider three parallel plates: two fixed outer plates and one movable central plate, with initial air gaps $d_0$ on both sides. After a small displacement $\Delta d$ of the central plate, the capacitances become:
>
> $$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - \Delta d}, \quad C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + \Delta d}$$
>
> The differential output is:
>
> $$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \dfrac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 A \, \Delta d}{d_0^2 - (\Delta d)^2}$$
>
> For $\Delta d \ll d_0$:
>
> $$\Delta C \approx \dfrac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} \Delta d$$
>
> Hence, **differential sensitivity:**
>
> $$S_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} = 2 \times S_{\text{single}}$$
>
> This proves the differential design has **twice the sensitivity** of the single-plate design, and the output is *linear* in $\Delta d$ for small displacements.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> **Given:** $A = 250 \text{ mm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $d_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\Delta d = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
>
> **(i) Nominal capacitance:**
>
> $$C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} = \dfrac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$
>
> $$C_0 = \dfrac{2.2135 \times 10^{-15}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = \mathbf{4.427 \text{ pF}}$$
>
> **(ii) Differential sensitivity:**
>
> $$S_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 250 \times 10^{-6}}{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$
>
> $$S_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{4.427 \times 10^{-15}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = \mathbf{1.771 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m} = 17.71 \text{ pF/mm}}$$
>
> **(iii) Change in differential capacitance:**
>
> $$\Delta C \approx S_{\text{diff}} \times \Delta d = 1.771 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$$
>
> $$\Delta C = \mathbf{8.85 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} \approx 0.885 \text{ pF}}$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the capacitance of a **variable-area capacitive transducer** with linear motion, and show that its sensitivity is constant. Mention two advantages over the air-gap type.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with derivation the working of a **variable-permittivity capacitive transducer** used for measurement of liquid level in a tank. State two practical limitations.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> Consider two parallel plates of width $w$ and length $l$, separated by a fixed gap $d$. The initial overlap area is $A = w l$, giving:
>
> $$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, w \, l}{d}$$
>
> When the moving plate translates by $\Delta l$, the new overlap becomes $l - \Delta l$, so:
>
> $$C' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, w \, (l - \Delta l)}{d}$$
>
> Change in capacitance:
>
> $$\Delta C = C - C' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, w \, \Delta l}{d}$$
>
> **Sensitivity:**
>
> $$S = \dfrac{dC}{dl} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, w}{d} = \text{constant}$$
>
> Since $S$ is independent of $l$, the response is **perfectly linear** over the entire range of motion.
>
> **Advantages over air-gap type:**
> 1. **Linear output** over a much larger range of displacement (cm-scale vs µm-scale).
> 2. **Larger measurable range** — limited only by the plate dimensions, not by mechanical instability at small gaps.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

> Consider a cylindrical tank with concentric inner and outer plates forming a capacitor. Let total plate height be $H$, radial gap be $d$, and liquid of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ rise to height $h$.
>
> The capacitor has two parallel sections:
> - Lower section (filled with liquid, height $h$): $C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (2\pi r h)}{d}$
> - Upper section (filled with air, height $H - h$): $C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 (2\pi r (H-h))}{d}$
>
> Total capacitance (parallel combination):
>
> $$C = C_1 + C_2 = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 r}{d}\Big[\varepsilon_r h + (H - h)\Big] = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 r}{d}\Big[H + (\varepsilon_r - 1)h\Big]$$
>
> Hence, $C$ is **linear in $h$** with sensitivity $S = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 r (\varepsilon_r - 1)}{d}$.
>
> **Two practical limitations:**
> 1. **Temperature dependence:** $\varepsilon_r$ of the liquid varies with temperature, introducing drift.
> 2. **Liquid creepage / coating errors:** Thin films of liquid on the upper plates alter the effective dielectric, causing non-linearity near the meniscus.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Sensitivity:** Forgetting the negative sign for the air-gap type — capacitance *decreases* as gap *increases*. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Skipping the small-displacement approximation:** When asked to "derive and linearise", you must explicitly state $\Delta d \ll d_0$ and show the simplification step. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Wrong area conversion:** Plate area must be in **m²** and gap in **m** before substituting into $C = \varepsilon_0 A/d$ — examiners penalise unit errors heavily. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing series vs parallel combination in the level sensor:** The liquid and air sections are in **parallel**, not series — this is a frequent conceptual error. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Omitting the sketch:** KTU Part B questions carrying 7 marks typically allocate **1–2 marks for a neat labelled diagram**. Always include one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Capacitive Transducers**

- **Master equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
- **Three types:** vary $d$ (air gap), vary $A$ (area), vary $\varepsilon_r$ (permittivity).
- **Air-gap transducer sensitivity:** $S = -\varepsilon_0 A / d_0^2$ (negative, non-linear, use only for small $\Delta d$).
- **Differential air-gap sensitivity:** $S_{\text{diff}} = 2\varepsilon_0 A / d_0^2$ — *twice* the single-plate value, linearised.
- **Variable area (linear) sensitivity:** $S = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r w / d$ — *constant*, hence perfectly linear.
- **Variable permittivity (level) sensitivity:** $S = 2\pi \varepsilon_0 r (\varepsilon_r - 1)/d$ — linear in $h$.
- **Differential configurations are always preferred** for air-gap types — they double sensitivity and linearise output.
- **For level sensing**, water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) gives far larger signals than oils ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2$–4).
- **Always convert units to SI (m, m², F/m)** before substituting into any formula.
- **For board answers**: include a labelled diagram, state the governing equation, perform the small-displacement approximation if applicable, and present the final sensitivity in a boxed equation.
- **Common applications:**
  - Variable air gap → pressure gauges, accelerometers, microphones.
  - Variable area → displacement transducers, thickness gauges.
  - Variable permittivity → liquid-level sensors, humidity sensors, composition analysers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitor Microphone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitor Microphone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Capacitor Microphone** (historically called a *condenser microphone*) is a passive electrostatic transducer that converts acoustic pressure variations in the air into corresponding electrical signals by exploiting the principle of a **variable capacitance** formed between a fixed back-plate and a flexible, mass-loaded diaphragm. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, it belongs to the family of *passive transducers* because it cannot generate electrical energy on its own and requires an external polarization voltage (DC bias) or a permanently charged dielectric (electret) to function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The capacitor microphone is the canonical example of an *electrostatic* (capacitive) type acoustic sensor and is universally used in high-fidelity audio capture, precision sound-level meters, and laboratory-grade measurement microphones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Soda Can" Model

Imagine two flat aluminum soda cans lying parallel to each other, separated by a thin invisible rubber spacer. If you blow air between them, the air pressure pushes one of the cans slightly closer to the other. The distance between them changes. Now imagine this distance is part of an electrical circuit that stores charge — the smaller the gap, the more charge it can hold at a given voltage. That is exactly how a capacitor microphone works: **sound is a pressure wave, the diaphragm moves, the air gap changes, and the capacitance changes accordingly.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** *Sound pushes a thin metal membrane closer to (or further from) a fixed plate, changing the electrical "storage capacity" of the gap. A downstream circuit detects this change and produces a voltage proportional to the sound.*

### 1.3 Classification within Transducer Taxonomy

| Parameter | Classification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Energy Conversion | Acoustic → Electrical (Passive) |
| Principle | Electrostatic / Variable Capacitance |
| Primary Signal Conditioning | External DC bias / Electret |
| Output | Modulated voltage or current |
| Application | Studio recording, SLMs, reference mics |

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Referenced

- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- **Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C):** $c \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$
- **Standard reference acoustic pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (threshold of hearing)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Step-by-Step Logic)

A capacitor microphone is built from three functional layers, each playing a precise role:

1. **Diaphragm (Movable Plate):** A thin (typically 2–6 µm), highly tensioned metallic or metallized polymer membrane. It is mass-controlled so that acoustic pressure can displace it linearly. Its low mass gives the mic a flat, extended high-frequency response.
2. **Back-plate (Fixed Plate):** A rigid, perforated metal disc held at a microscopic standoff (typically 20–50 µm) from the diaphragm. The perforations allow air to move freely, which damps diaphragm resonance and prevents pressure build-up.
3. **Insulating Spacer / Dielectric Body:** A precision-machined non-conductive ring that holds the air gap constant in the rest state. In an **electret** design, a permanently polarized fluoropolymer (e.g., PTFE) replaces or supplements the air gap with a quasi-permanent charge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why perforations?** A solid back-plate would trap air and create a stiff air-spring that limits low-frequency response. Perforations restore acoustic compliance across the audio band (20 Hz – 20 kHz).

### 2.2 The Operating Principle — Variable Capacitance

The fundamental capacitance of a parallel-plate geometry is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

When an acoustic pressure wave $p(t)$ impinges on the diaphragm, the plate separation becomes $d - \Delta d(t)$, so the instantaneous capacitance is:

$$C(t) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta d(t)}$$

For small diaphragm displacements ($\Delta d \ll d$), applying a first-order Taylor expansion yields the linearized sensitivity expression, which is derived in Section 3.

### 2.3 Why External Biasing Is Required

A pure variable capacitor cannot, by itself, produce an output voltage or current under open-circuit conditions. It needs to be embedded in one of two ways:

- **Classic (Externally Biased) Design:** A high DC polarization voltage $V_p$ (typically **200 V**) is placed across the capsule in series with a very high load resistor $R_L$. The change in capacitance modulates the charge, producing a small AC voltage $v_o(t)$ across $R_L$.
- **Electret (Self-Biased) Design:** A permanently charged dielectric (electret) provides the equivalent of the polarization voltage internally. Only a low-voltage FET impedance converter inside the mic body is needed. This is the form found in **99 % of consumer and most professional mics today.**

### 2.4 Equivalent Circuit Model

The mechanical diaphragm assembly can be modeled as a second-order mass-spring-damper, while the electrical side consists of the time-varying capacitor in series with a bias resistor feeding into a high-impedance pre-amplifier (FET or tube). The KTU-recommended small-signal electrical equivalent is a **voltage source $e(t) = V_p\,\dfrac{\Delta d(t)}{d}$ in series with the capsule capacitance $C_0$ and the load resistor $R_L$.**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Use Case |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Static capacitance of capsule | Farads (F) | Calculate resting capacitance |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1-\Delta d/d_0}$ | Exact fractional change | Dimensionless | Exact sensitivity analysis |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | Linearized small-signal change | Dimensionless | KTU board exam approximation |
| 4 | $e(t) = V_p \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d(t)}{d_0}$ | Open-circuit output EMF | Volts (V) | Sensitivity calculation |
| 5 | $v_o(t) = \dfrac{e(t)}{1 + j\omega R_L C_0}$ | Loaded output voltage | Volts (V) | Frequency response derivation |
| 6 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_L C_0}$ | Lower cutoff (-3 dB) | Hertz (Hz) | Low-frequency roll-off |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{\vert e(t) \vert}{p(t)}\ [\text{V/Pa}]$ | Pressure sensitivity | V/Pa | Microphone rating |
| 8 | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_0}$ | Capacitive reactance | Ohms (Ω) | Pre-amp impedance matching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** For board questions, always linearize the capacitance equation first ($\Delta C/C_0 \approx \Delta d/d_0$). Failing to use the small-signal approximation when the question states *“small displacement”* costs full marks.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Studio & Broadcast Audio:** Cardioid-pattern condenser microphones for vocals, piano, overhead drums.
- **Sound Level Meters (IEC 61672):** Class-1 reference measurement microphones are almost exclusively ½-inch prepolarized condenser capsules.
- **Acoustic Sensing in Industry:** Used in noise surveys of factories, HVAC, and aircraft.
- **Ultrasonic & Infra-sonic Research:** Modified condenser capsules serve hydrophones and ultrasonic probes.
- **Mobile Devices:** MEMS condenser microphones dominate smartphones, leveraging the same capacitance principle at silicon scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Linearized Capacitance Sensitivity

**Goal:** Derive the relationship $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ from the static capacitance formula.

**Step 1 — Start with the static capacitance formula.**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

Here, $d_0$ is the rest-state air gap. $\varepsilon_0$, $\varepsilon_r$, and $A$ are constant.

**Step 2 — Apply a small displacement $\Delta d$ to the diaphragm.** The new plate separation is $d_0 - \Delta d$, so the new capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the denominator to expose the perturbation.**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $C_0$.**

$$C = \frac{C_0 \cdot d_0}{d_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)} = \frac{C_0}{1 - \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Use the geometric series expansion for $\vert \Delta d/d_0 \vert \ll 1$.** Recall that $\dfrac{1}{1 - x} = 1 + x + x^2 + x^3 + \ldots$, so:

$$C \approx C_0 \left(1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} + \left(\frac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)^2 + \ldots\right)$$

**Step 6 — Retain only the first-order term (small-signal linearization).**

$$C \approx C_0 \left(1 + \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Subtract $C_0$ from both sides to isolate the change.**

$$\Delta C = C - C_0 \approx C_0 \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

**Step 8 — Final linearized sensitivity expression.**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A fractional change in the air gap produces an *equal* fractional change in capacitance. This symmetry is what makes the capacitor microphone inherently linear over a wide dynamic range.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Open-Circuit Output EMF

**Goal:** Show that the generated voltage across the capsule is $e(t) = V_p \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d(t)}{d_0}$.

**Step 1 — Charge conservation.** The polarization voltage $V_p$ is applied across the series combination of the capsule $C(t)$ and a much larger blocking capacitor (or appears directly across the capsule in the electret equivalent). The charge stored is:

$$Q(t) = C(t) \cdot V_p$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find the signal current.**

$$i(t) = \frac{dQ}{dt} = V_p \cdot \frac{dC}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Express $dC/dt$ in terms of $\Delta d(t)$.** From the linearized result, $C(t) = C_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta d(t)}{d_0}\right)$, so:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{C_0}{d_0} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta d)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the mechanical velocity of the diaphragm.** Let $v_m(t) = \dfrac{d(\Delta d)}{dt}$ be the diaphragm velocity. Therefore:

$$i(t) = V_p \cdot \frac{C_0}{d_0} \cdot v_m(t)$$

**Step 5 — The open-circuit EMF (Thevenin equivalent).** For a capacitive source feeding a high-impedance load $R_L$, the loaded output voltage is:

$$v_o(t) = i(t) \cdot Z_{eq} = V_p \cdot \frac{\Delta d(t)}{d_0} \cdot \frac{j\omega R_L C_0}{1 + j\omega R_L C_0}$$

**Step 6 — In the midband region, where $\omega R_L C_0 \gg 1$, the transfer function approaches unity, giving the open-circuit EMF:**

$$\boxed{e(t) = V_p \cdot \frac{\Delta d(t)}{d_0}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Pressure Sensitivity & Frequency Response

**Goal:** Express the open-circuit sensitivity $S$ in V/Pa, and identify the low-frequency cutoff.

**Step 1 — Mechanical model of the diaphragm.** The diaphragm is treated as a mass-spring-damper driven by acoustic pressure $p(t)$. For a rigid, mass-controlled membrane of effective area $A_d$, the displacement is:

$$\Delta d(t) = \frac{p(t)}{k_m} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (\omega/\omega_0)^2 + j(\omega/\omega_0 Q)}$$

where $k_m$ is the mechanical stiffness, $\omega_0$ is the resonance, and $Q$ is the quality factor.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the EMF expression.**

$$e(t) = \frac{V_p}{d_0} \cdot \frac{p(t)}{k_m} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (\omega/\omega_0)^2 + j(\omega/\omega_0 Q)}$$

**Step 3 — Define the pressure sensitivity in the midband** ($\omega \ll \omega_0$):

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\vert e(t) \vert}{p(t)} = \frac{V_p}{k_m \, d_0}}\ [\text{V/Pa}]$$

**Step 4 — Loaded response including $R_L C_0$ high-pass.** The complete transfer function is:

$$H(\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_L C_0}{1 + j\omega R_L C_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (\omega/\omega_0)^2 + j(\omega/\omega_0 Q)}$$

**Step 5 — Lower cutoff frequency.** The electrical high-pass corner is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_0}$$

**Step 6 — Typical numerical values used in KTU problems.** For a ½-inch capsule: $C_0 \approx 20$ pF, $V_p = 200$ V, $d_0 = 20$ µm, $A_d = 1.27$ cm². Plugging into the formula:

$$S \approx \frac{200}{(20 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot k_m}$$

The exact $k_m$ depends on diaphragm tension; for a typical $k_m = 10^4$ N/m, the sensitivity is approximately **50 mV/Pa**, in line with real measurement microphones.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Increases $S$ | Drawback |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Larger polarization $V_p$ | $\propto V_p$ | Requires HV supply; risk of arcing |
| Smaller air gap $d_0$ | $\propto 1/d_0$ | Reduces diaphragm travel; distortion |
| Lower stiffness $k_m$ | $\propto 1/k_m$ | Lowers resonance, narrows bandwidth |
| Larger diaphragm area $A_d$ | Indirect (via $k_m$) | Increases mass, raises noise floor |
| Higher $R_L$ | Lowers $f_c$ (extends LF) | Raises thermal (Johnson) noise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Flow)

Because a literal physical drawing of the mic capsule is impractical in Mermaid, the following block-level functional architecture shows how acoustic, mechanical, electrical, and signal-conditioning domains are coupled inside the device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ACOUSTIC DOMAIN] -->|Sound Pressure p t| B1[MECHANICAL DOMAIN]
    B1 -->|Diaphragm Displacement delta d| C1[ELECTRICAL DOMAIN]
    C1 -->|Modulated Charge Q t| D1[BIAS NETWORK]
    D1 -->|High DC Vp| C1
    C1 -->|Signal Current i t| E1[PRE AMPLIFIER]
    E1 -->|Voltage Vo t| F1[OUTPUT]

    subgraph ACOUSTIC_DOMAIN[ACOUSTIC]
        A1
    end
    subgraph MECHANICAL_DOMAIN[MECHANICAL]
        B1
    end
    subgraph ELECTRICAL_DOMAIN[ELECTRICAL]
        C1
    end
```

### 4.2 Signal-Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Sound Source 20 Hz to 20 kHz] --> DIA[Thin Metallized Diaphragm]
    DIA -->|Vibrates| GAP[Variable Air Gap d minus delta d]
    GAP --> CAP[Time Varying Capacitor C t]
    BIAS[DC Polarization Source Vp] --> CAP
    CAP --> RES[High Load Resistor RL]
    RES --> FET[JFET Impedance Converter]
    FET --> OUT[Balanced Audio Output]
```

### 4.3 Electrical Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VP[(DC Bias Vp)] --- CAP[/C0 Capacitor/]
    CAP --- RL[R_L Load Resistor]
    RL --- AMP[Pre-amp High Z]
    AMP --- VOUT[Output Vout]
    NOISE[Johnson Noise sqrt 4 k T R df] -.-> RL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Follow the arrows from left to right. The leftmost block is the *physical stimulus* (sound pressure), and the rightmost block is the *electrical output* delivered to the recorder or mixer. The three feedback/feed-forward links (V_p, R_L, noise) reflect real engineering constraints in the design.

### 4.4 Frequency-Response Plot (Schematic Description)

- **Region 1 — Low Frequency (f < f_c):** Roll-off at -20 dB/decade due to $R_L C_0$ high-pass.
- **Region 2 — Midband (f_c < f < f_0):** Flat response, sensitivity = $S$ V/Pa.
- **Region 3 — Resonance (f ≈ f_0):** Peak of magnitude $Q$, typically damped to < 4 dB.
- **Region 4 — High Frequency (f > f_0):** Roll-off at -40 dB/decade due to mass loading.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a capacitor microphone. State any two advantages and one disadvantage of a capacitor microphone.*

**Model Answer:**
A capacitor microphone is an electrostatic transducer that converts sound pressure variations into a corresponding change in electrical capacitance between a fixed back-plate and a movable diaphragm. The varying capacitance modulates a DC bias voltage to produce an AC output signal proportional to the incident acoustic pressure.

*Advantages:*
1. Excellent frequency response (20 Hz – 20 kHz, extendable higher) due to the low mass of the diaphragm.
2. High sensitivity and low self-noise, making it ideal for studio recording and measurement.

*Disadvantage:*
1. Requires an external polarization supply (or electret + internal FET), making it more complex and fragile than a dynamic microphone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is an external DC bias required in a classic capacitor microphone? What happens in the absence of it?*

**Model Answer:**
A varying capacitor cannot, by itself, deliver power to a load — it only stores charge. The external DC bias $V_p$ establishes a baseline charge $Q_0 = C_0 V_p$ on the capsule. When sound modulates $C$ by $\Delta C$, the resulting $\Delta Q = V_p \Delta C$ flows through the load and produces an output voltage. Without $V_p$, there is no baseline charge, so the microphone produces no output signal — it becomes electrically silent. In electret versions, the dielectric itself holds the equivalent bias permanently, eliminating the need for an external supply.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) and (b). Students answer either the whole of *Question A* or the whole of *Question B*.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a capacitor microphone. Derive the expression for the change in capacitance for a small displacement of the diaphragm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capacitor microphone has a static capacitance of **50 pF**, an air gap of **25 µm**, and a diaphragm area of **1.0 cm²**. It is polarized by **150 V**. The diaphragm experiences a sinusoidal displacement of peak amplitude **0.1 µm** at **1 kHz**. Calculate:
    (i) The peak AC output voltage across the capsule (midband approximation).
    (ii) The required load resistance $R_L$ such that the lower cutoff is **10 Hz**.
    (iii) The open-circuit sensitivity in V/Pa, given the acoustic pressure amplitude is **1 Pa**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A capacitor microphone consists of a thin, highly tensioned metallic diaphragm (2–6 µm thick) mounted parallel to a rigid, perforated back-plate. The two are separated by an insulating spacer ring that maintains an air gap of 20–50 µm. The diaphragm and back-plate form the two plates of a parallel-plate capacitor. A DC polarization voltage $V_p$ (typically 200 V) is applied across the capsule via a high-value load resistor $R_L$ in series.

**Working Principle:** When an acoustic pressure wave strikes the diaphragm, it vibrates, modulating the air gap $d$. Because the capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ depends inversely on the gap, the capacitance varies in proportion to the sound. The change in capacitance, in turn, modulates the charge $Q = C V_p$, producing an AC current that develops a measurable output voltage $v_o(t)$ across $R_L$.

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_0 = 50$ pF $= 50 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $d_0 = 25$ µm $= 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $A = 1.0$ cm² $= 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $V_p = 150$ V, $\Delta d_{\text{peak}} = 0.1$ µm $= 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $f = 1$ kHz, $f_c = 10$ Hz, $p = 1$ Pa.

**(i) Peak output voltage:**

Using the linearized EMF formula $e_{\text{peak}} = V_p \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d_{\text{peak}}}{d_0}$:

$$e_{\text{peak}} = 150 \times \frac{0.1 \times 10^{-6}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 150 \times 0.004 = 0.6\ \text{V}$$

**$\boxed{e_{\text{peak}} = 0.6\ \text{V} = 600\ \text{mV}}$**

**(ii) Required load resistance for 10 Hz cutoff:**

The lower cutoff is given by $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_L C_0}$, so:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_0} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 50 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_L = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{8}\ \Omega$$

**$\boxed{R_L \approx 318\ \text{M}\Omega}$**

**(iii) Open-circuit sensitivity in V/Pa:**

For a 1 Pa pressure producing a 0.1 µm displacement, the sensitivity is:

$$S = \frac{e_{\text{peak}}}{p} = \frac{0.6\ \text{V}}{1\ \text{Pa}} = 0.6\ \text{V/Pa}$$

**$\boxed{S = 0.6\ \text{V/Pa}\ (= 600\ \text{mV/Pa})}$**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare capacitor (condenser) microphones with moving-coil (dynamic) microphones under the following heads: principle of operation, sensitivity, frequency response, power requirement, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the frequency response of a capacitor microphone has a low-frequency roll-off and a high-frequency resonance. Derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency. A measurement microphone has $C_0 = 18$ pF. Determine the value of $R_L$ required for a lower cutoff of **5 Hz**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Capacitor Microphone | Moving-Coil (Dynamic) Microphone |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Principle | Variable capacitance | Electromagnetic induction |
| Sensitivity | High (10–50 mV/Pa) | Moderate (1–5 mV/Pa) |
| Frequency Response | Excellent, 20 Hz – 20 kHz (and beyond) | Good, 50 Hz – 15 kHz typical |
| Power Requirement | Requires DC bias / battery / phantom power | Self-powered, no external supply |
| Construction | Delicate diaphragm, fixed back-plate | Robust coil + magnet assembly |
| Output Impedance | High (needs impedance converter) | Low (direct connection) |
| Typical Application | Studio recording, SLMs, reference mics | Live performance, broadcasting |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Self-Noise | Very low | Higher (coil thermal + hum) |

---

**(b) Frequency-Response Explanation and Cutoff Derivation (7 marks):**

**Low-frequency roll-off:** The series combination of the capsule capacitance $C_0$ and the load resistor $R_L$ forms a **first-order high-pass filter**. At very low frequencies, the impedance $1/(\omega C_0)$ becomes so large that the voltage divider heavily attenuates the signal. The transfer function is:

$$H(\omega) = \frac{j\omega R_L C_0}{1 + j\omega R_L C_0}$$

At $\omega = 1/(R_L C_0)$, the magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of the midband value, defining the **-3 dB cutoff frequency**:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_0}$$

**High-frequency resonance:** The diaphragm has a finite mass $m$ and is held under tension, behaving as a mass-spring system with natural frequency $\omega_0 = \sqrt{k_m / m}$. At and above this frequency, mechanical resonance causes a peak in response, after which the mass-controlled region rolls off at -40 dB/decade.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $C_0 = 18$ pF $= 18 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $f_c = 5$ Hz:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_0} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 18 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_L = \frac{1}{5.6549 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{9}\ \Omega$$

**$\boxed{R_L \approx 1.77\ \text{G}\Omega}$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Converting µm, pF, and cm² correctly is the single biggest reason students lose 2–3 marks per numerical. Always show the unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Linearization Oversight:** Using the *exact* formula $\Delta C/C_0 = \Delta d/(d_0 - \Delta d)$ when the question says *“small displacement”* is wrong — you must linearize.
> 3. **Phantom Power vs Polarization Voltage:** Do not confuse the +48 V phantom supply (which powers the internal pre-amp) with the 200 V capsule polarization. They are entirely different.
> 4. **Sign of $\Delta d$:** A pressure increase reduces $d$, so $\Delta d$ is *negative* for compression. The sign cancels out in the magnitude of output voltage, but examiners may deduct marks if you do not mention it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Principle:** A capacitor microphone is a **passive, variable-capacitance acoustic transducer** requiring an external bias (or electret).
- **Governing Formula:** $C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d_0$ and the linearized sensitivity $\Delta C / C_0 \approx \Delta d / d_0$.
- **Output EMF:** $e(t) = V_p \cdot \Delta d(t) / d_0$ — sensitivity is directly proportional to polarization voltage.
- **Frequency Response:** High-pass corner at $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_L C_0)$; mechanical resonance at $\omega_0 = \sqrt{k_m/m}$.
- **Sensitivity Units:** Always expressed in **mV/Pa** or **V/Pa** in KTU problems.
- **Two Bias Variants:** *Externally polarized* (200 V) vs *electret* (self-biased dielectric). Both obey the same equation.
- **Typical Component Values:** $C_0 \sim 10$–$60$ pF, $d_0 \sim 20$–$50$ µm, $R_L \sim 100$ M$\Omega$ – $10$ G$\Omega$.
- **Typical Sensitivity:** $5$–$50$ mV/Pa for studio and measurement mics.
- **Block-Diagram Recognition:** Be ready to draw or identify the four domains — *Acoustic → Mechanical → Electrical → Output* — and the role of the pre-amp.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  1. Phantom power ($\approx +48$ V) ≠ Polarization voltage ($\approx 200$ V).
  2. Variable capacitance ≠ Variable inductance (that would be an inductive microphone, a different topic).
  3. Moving-coil mics do *not* need bias; capacitor mics *do*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Advice:** When asked to "explain the working," always start with the *static* capacitance formula, then show the *modulation* by $\Delta d$, derive the *linearized* sensitivity, and finish with the *output EMF*. This four-step structure is the exact pattern KTU examiners use as the marking key.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tacho Generators and Stroboscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tacho Generators and Stroboscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tacho Generator — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Tacho Generator** (short for *Tachometer Generator*) is a precision electromechanical transducer whose sole purpose is to convert the **rotational speed** (angular velocity) of a mechanical shaft into a **proportional electrical output signal** — either a DC voltage or an AC voltage — that can be easily measured, recorded, or fed into a closed-loop control system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Transducers and Measurements*, the tacho generator is classified under **velocity-type motion transducers**, sitting alongside electromagnetic pickups and Hall-effect sensors. It is essentially the electrical *speedometer* of industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCAET303 / Module 2**  
> The official syllabus descriptor reads: *"Tacho Generators — DC and AC types, construction, working principle, EMF equation, and characteristics."*  
> A tacho generator is **not** a power-generating device — it is a **measurement instrument**, optimised for accuracy, linearity, and low ripple, *not* for delivering large currents.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Dynamo Idea 🚲

Imagine the small dynamo (generator) that you press against the side wheel of a bicycle to power its headlamp. As you pedal faster, the dynamo spins faster and the lamp burns brighter. Now strip away the lamp, put a sensitive voltmeter across the dynamo's terminals, and place a calibration sticker on it that says *"1 V = 1000 RPM"*.

You have just invented a **DC Tacho Generator**.  
The faster the shaft rotates → the higher the induced EMF → the voltmeter reads a higher voltage → you read off the corresponding speed.

The only difference: a *real* industrial tacho generator has been engineered for **incredibly linear output, low ripple, low temperature drift, and stable permanent-magnet flux** — none of which your bicycle dynamo cares about.

---

## 1.2 Stroboscope — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Stroboscope** is a non-contact optical instrument used to measure the **rotational speed (RPM)** or to *visually freeze* the motion of a rapidly rotating or vibrating object. It does this by emitting **intense, brief, precisely-timed flashes of light** at an adjustable rate. When the flash frequency is synchronised with the rotational frequency of the object (or an integer fraction/multiple of it), the object appears to stand still.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCAET303 / Module 2**  
> The official syllabus descriptor reads: *"Stroboscope — principle of operation, formula for speed measurement, types (mechanical disc type and electronic xenon-flash type), and applications."*  
> The key insight: **the stroboscope does NOT need to touch the rotating shaft** — it measures speed optically, making it ideal for inaccessible or hazardous machinery.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Western Spokes Illusion 🎬

You have surely seen a movie scene where a stagecoach is rolling along, and the spoked wheels appear to rotate **backwards**, or **freeze**, or even **stand completely still** while the coach moves forward. This is **stroboscopic illusion**.

Why does it happen? Because the camera captures frames at a fixed rate (**24 frames/second**). If a wheel spoke is moving into the exact position of the previous spoke between two frames, your brain registers *"the wheel has not moved."*

A stroboscope is simply a camera with **adjustable frame rate**. Set the flash rate to exactly match the wheel's rotation, and the wheel appears frozen in mid-air. Read the flash rate → divide by the number of visible marks on the wheel → and you have the **exact RPM**. Magic, but pure physics.

---

## 1.3 Classification at a Glance

> [!NOTE]  
> **Two Broad Families — Both Covered in KTU Module 2**

| Family | Type | Output Signal | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tacho Generator** | DC Tacho Generator | DC Voltage $V \propto N$ | Electromechanical velocity transducer |
| **Tacho Generator** | AC Tacho Generator (Drag-Cup type) | AC Voltage $V_{ac} \propto \omega$ | Electromechanical velocity transducer |
| **Stroboscope** | Mechanical Stroboscope | Visual frozen image | Non-contact tachometer |
| **Stroboscope** | Electronic (Xenon-flash) Stroboscope | Visual frozen image + digital RPM readout | Non-contact tachometer |

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Two Topics Grouped Together in KTU Module 2?

Because they **both measure rotational speed** — but via fundamentally different physical principles:

- A **tacho generator** converts rotation *electrically* (Faraday's law of induction).
- A **stroboscope** measures rotation *optically* (visual persistence of the human eye, approximately **1/16 second = 62.5 ms**).

In real KTU exam questions, students are often asked to **compare** their advantages, limitations, and choose the right instrument for a given industrial scenario.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DC Tacho Generator — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Construction (Permanent-Magnet Type — Most Common in KTU Exams)

- A **stator** housing **permanent magnets** (typically Alnico or modern rare-earth materials such as **Samarium-Cobalt** for stable flux).
- A **rotating armature** (drum-wound) mounted on the shaft whose speed is to be measured.
- A **commutator** and **brush assembly** to convert the internally generated AC into DC at the output terminals.
- The armature is wound with many turns of fine wire to maximise the induced EMF.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why Permanent Magnets?**  
> Field excitation is taken care of by permanent magnets. Hence there is no need for an external DC field supply — this makes the device compact, reliable, and drift-free. This is the dominant construction asked about in KTU 2024 questions.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Faraday's Law)

When the shaft rotates at $N$ RPM, the armature conductors cut the magnetic flux $\Phi$ produced by the permanent magnets. According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in each conductor.

The induced EMF per conductor = $B \cdot l \cdot v$, where $v$ is the linear velocity of the conductor.

Since $v \propto N$, the total generated EMF is directly proportional to $N$:

$$E_g \propto N$$

That is the heart of the device — the **linear relationship between shaft speed and output voltage**.

### 2.1.3 The KTU Master Formula for DC Tacho Generator

$$\boxed{\,E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}\,}$$

where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_g$ | Generated EMF at output terminals | **Volts (V)** |
| $P$ | Number of poles | dimensionless |
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole | **Weber (Wb)** |
| $Z$ | Total number of armature conductors | dimensionless |
| $N$ | Speed of rotation | **RPM** |
| $A$ | Number of parallel paths in armature winding | dimensionless |
| **60** | Conversion factor (seconds per minute) | — |

For the **common armature windings**:

$$A_{\text{wave}} = 2$$

$$A_{\text{lap}} = P$$

### 2.1.4 Sensitivity — The Figure of Merit

The **sensitivity** $K_t$ of a DC tacho generator is defined as the EMF produced per 1000 RPM:

$$\boxed{\,K_t = \dfrac{E_g}{N} \times 1000 = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot 1000}{60 \cdot A} = \dfrac{50 \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{3 \cdot A}\, \;\; \text{(V/kRPM)}\,}$$

Typical industrial DC tacho generators offer sensitivities in the range of **5 V / 1000 RPM to 100 V / 1000 RPM**.

### 2.1.5 Characteristics — The Ideal vs. Reality Curve

**Ideal characteristic**: A perfectly straight line passing through the origin ($E_g$ vs $N$).

**Real characteristic**: Slightly deviates due to:
1. **Armature reaction** — armature current distorts the main field flux $\Phi$.
2. **Brush contact drop** — a small voltage drop (~1 V) at the brushes, causing the line to miss the origin.
3. **Magnetic saturation** — at high speeds, $\Phi$ saturates and the curve bends.
4. **Temperature drift** — resistance of armature winding changes with temperature.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often forget that DC tacho generators have **commutator ripples** in the output — a small AC ripple superimposed on the DC output due to the finite number of commutator segments. This is a frequent short-answer question.

---

## 2.2 AC Tacho Generator (Drag-Cup Type)

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Stator**: Has two windings placed at **90° electrical** to each other in the stator slots.
  - **Reference winding** (primary, fixed-axis winding) — connected to an AC excitation supply of fixed amplitude and frequency (typically **50 Hz** or **400 Hz**).
  - **Quadrature winding** (secondary, output-axis winding) — the winding from which the output voltage is taken.
- **Rotor**: A thin, hollow **drag cup** (usually made of aluminium or copper) mounted on the shaft whose speed is to be measured. The cup rotates in the air gap between the inner and outer stator iron.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle

When AC excitation is applied to the reference winding, an alternating flux is set up along its axis. With the rotor stationary, this flux does not link the quadrature winding — so the **output is zero**.

When the rotor rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, eddy currents are induced in the drag cup. These eddy currents produce a flux component **along the quadrature axis**, which links the output winding and induces a voltage:

$$E_{out} = K_a \cdot \omega$$

where $K_a$ is the sensitivity constant of the AC tacho generator.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Property**: The output of an AC tacho generator is an **AC voltage whose amplitude is proportional to speed**, but whose **frequency is fixed** (equal to the excitation/reference frequency). This is what distinguishes it from a plain alternator — the frequency is decoupled from the speed.

### 2.2.3 Sensitivity Equation

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = K \cdot \omega = K \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \approx \dfrac{K \cdot N}{9.55}\,}$$

For a 2-pole drag-cup tacho generator, the sensitivity constant $K$ depends on the reference voltage $V_{ref}$, the reference frequency $f$, and the rotor-cup geometry:

$$K \approx \dfrac{V_{ref} \cdot C}{f}$$

where $C$ is a construction constant depending on the number of turns and rotor resistance.

---

## 2.3 Stroboscope — Operational Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Working Principle — Persistence of Vision

The human eye retains an image for approximately **1/16 second ≈ 62.5 ms** after the stimulus is removed. This is called **persistence of vision**. A stroboscope exploits this beautifully.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The rotating object has **$n$** identical reference marks (slots, holes, vanes, or radial lines) equally spaced on its body.
2. The stroboscope emits flashes of light at a controllable frequency $f$ (in Hz).
3. If, between two successive flashes, the object rotates through an angle $\theta = 360°/n$, the $n$-th mark moves into the position previously occupied by mark #1.
4. To the observer, every flash lights up **the same mark in the same position** — the object appears **frozen**.
5. The flash frequency $f$ is then read off the calibrated dial and the RPM is calculated.

### 2.3.2 The KTU Master Formula for Stroboscope

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}\,}$$

where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N_{\text{RPM}}$ | Rotational speed of the object | **RPM** |
| $f$ | Flash frequency of the stroboscope | **Hz** (flashes per second) |
| $n$ | Number of reference marks/slots/vanes on the rotating object | dimensionless |
| **60** | Conversion from per-second to per-minute | — |

**Special case** — when the object has only **one** reference mark ($n = 1$):

$$N_{\text{RPM}} = 60 \cdot f$$

### 2.3.3 The Two Operating Modes

**Mode 1 — Single-Speed Mode (Direct Reading):**
When the flash rate is exactly equal to the rotation rate, a single stationary image is seen.

$$N_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}$$

**Mode 2 — Multiplier Mode (for very high speeds):**
To measure RPM beyond the maximum flash rate of the instrument, use a disc with multiple slots. The measured $N$ is then *divided* by $n$.

**Mode 3 — Multiple Stationary Images:**
Sometimes two or more stationary images appear — one at $f$, one at $2f$, one at $3f$, etc. The *lowest* frequency that produces a stationary image is the **true RPM**.

---

| Device | Real-World Applications |
|---|---|
| **DC Tacho Generator** | Servo-motor speed feedback, paper-machine speed control, CNC spindle RPM, gun-turret rotation, anti-lock braking feedback (older vehicles) |
| **AC Tacho Generator** | Aircraft gyro instruments, military servo systems, inertial navigation platforms, where AC excitation is already available onboard |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of a DC Tacho Generator

This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU question**. We derive the EMF equation from **fundamental Faraday's law** of electromagnetic induction.

### Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Revolution

Consider one armature conductor. In one complete revolution, it sweeps through an area under **$P$ poles**, each carrying a flux $\Phi$ Webers.

Therefore, the flux cut by **one conductor in one revolution** is:

$$\Phi_{\text{cut per conductor per rev}} = P \cdot \Phi$$

### Step 2 — Flux Cut Per Second (for Speed in RPS)

If the armature rotates at $N$ RPM, then the revolutions per second (RPS) is:

$$n_{\text{RPS}} = \dfrac{N}{60}$$

Hence the flux cut by one conductor per second is:

$$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$$

### Step 3 — Average EMF Induced in One Conductor

By **Faraday's Law**, the average EMF induced in one conductor is:

$$e_{\text{avg per conductor}} = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \;\; \text{Volts}$$

### Step 4 — Total EMF for $Z$ Conductors in Series

The $Z$ armature conductors are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. The number of conductors **in series per parallel path** is:

$$Z_{\text{series}} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

Since conductors in series add their EMFs, the total EMF generated across the brushes is:

$$\boxed{\,E_g = e_{\text{avg per conductor}} \cdot Z_{\text{series}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}\,}$$

This is the **canonical DC tacho generator EMF equation** required for KTU exams. ✅

### Step 5 — Substituting Winding Topology

| Winding Type | Parallel Paths $A$ | Resulting EMF Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Lap Wound** | $A = P$ | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60}$ |
| **Wave Wound** | $A = 2$ | $E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{120}$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Quick Numerical Example (for clarity, not a sample question):**  
> A lap-wound 4-pole DC tacho generator has 480 conductors, flux per pole = 5 mWb, running at 1500 RPM.  
> $E_g = \dfrac{4 \times 5\times10^{-3} \times 480 \times 1500}{60 \times 4} = \dfrac{14400}{240} = 60$ Volts. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output of an AC Tacho Generator

### Step 1 — Reference Winding Sets Up an Alternating Flux

When an AC voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$ of frequency $f$ is applied to the reference (primary) winding, it produces a sinusoidally varying flux along its axis:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_{\max} \sin(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 2 — Rotor (Drag Cup) Stationary → Output = 0

With the rotor stationary, this alternating flux does **not** induce any voltage in the **quadrature-axis** winding, because the two stator windings are physically at 90° and magnetically decoupled.

$$V_{out}\Big\vert_{\omega = 0} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Rotor Rotating → Eddy Currents in the Drag Cup

When the rotor rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, the relative motion between the alternating reference flux and the conducting drag cup induces **eddy currents** in the cup.

These eddy currents, by **Lenz's Law**, oppose the relative motion and produce a **secondary flux component along the quadrature axis**.

### Step 4 — Quadrature Flux is Proportional to Speed

The amplitude of the quadrature-axis flux $\Phi_q$ is proportional to the rotor speed:

$$\Phi_q \propto \omega$$

### Step 5 — Output Voltage from Quadrature Winding

By Faraday's Law, the quadrature-axis flux induces an AC voltage in the quadrature winding at the **same reference frequency** $f$:

$$V_{out}(t) = K_a \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The **RMS amplitude** is:

$$\boxed{\,V_{out,\text{RMS}} = K_a \cdot \omega = K_a \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\,}$$

where $K_a$ is a constant that depends on the construction (turns, rotor resistance, reference voltage amplitude).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stroboscope Speed Equation

This is the **single most important formula** for KTU Module 2 stroboscope problems.

### Step 1 — Object Rotates at $N$ RPM

Let the rotating object have $n$ equally spaced reference marks (e.g., holes, slots, radial lines) and rotate at $N$ revolutions per minute.

### Step 2 — Time Per Revolution

The time taken for **one complete revolution** is:

$$T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{60}{N} \;\; \text{seconds}$$

### Step 3 — Time Between Two Adjacent Marks

The $n$ marks divide one revolution into $n$ equal angular segments of $360°/n$ each. The time for the rotor to advance by **one such segment** (i.e., the time between two consecutive marks passing a fixed point) is:

$$T_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{rev}}}{n} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot n} \;\; \text{seconds}$$

### Step 4 — Condition for a Stationary Image

The stroboscope emits a flash every $T_{\text{flash}} = 1/f$ seconds. For the image to appear stationary, the object must rotate by exactly **one segment** between two successive flashes:

$$T_{\text{flash}} = T_{\text{segment}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot n}$$

### Step 5 — Solving for $N$

Cross-multiplying:

$$N \cdot n = 60 \cdot f$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental stroboscope equation** that KTU expects in every Module 2 problem.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]  
> **Worked Example — Stroboscope Calibration**  
> A stroboscope disc has **12 slots**. When the flash frequency is set to **25 Hz**, the rotating shaft appears stationary. Find the shaft RPM.

**Solution using the boxed formula:**

$$N = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n} = \dfrac{60 \times 25}{12} = \dfrac{1500}{12} = 125 \;\; \text{RPM}$$

**Verification by reverse engineering:**

- Shaft RPM = 125 → RPS = 125/60 = 2.083 rev/s
- Marks passing a fixed point per second = 2.083 × 12 = 25 marks/s
- So flashes at 25 Hz = exactly 1 flash per mark passing → image frozen ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tacho Generator vs Stroboscope

| Parameter | DC Tacho Generator | AC Tacho Generator | Stroboscope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measured quantity** | Rotational speed | Rotational speed | Rotational speed |
| **Output signal** | DC voltage | AC voltage (fixed freq.) | Visual (no electrical output) |
| **Contact with shaft?** | Yes — coupled to shaft | Yes — coupled to shaft | **No — non-contact** |
| **Speed range** | Up to ~10,000 RPM | Up to ~10,000 RPM | Up to ~100,000 RPM (electronic) |
| **Loading effect** | Yes (back-torque) | Yes (back-torque) | **None (non-contact)** |
| **Output linearity** | Good (with compensation) | Excellent | Depends on operator skill |
| **Suitable for** | Servo feedback, industrial | Aerospace, instruments | Inspection, balancing |
| **Major limitation** | Brush wear, ripple | Requires AC excitation | Visual interpretation needed |
| **Typical accuracy** | ±0.5% to ±1% | ±0.5% to ±2% | ±1% to ±5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite Question Pattern**: *"Compare DC tacho generator and stroboscope as speed measuring devices."* — Memorise this comparison matrix!
