# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder**: Part A questions are short, definition or short-explanation type, carrying **3 marks** each. They expect crisp answers of 4–5 lines with a labelled diagram where applicable.

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a tacho generator. List the two main types of tacho generators.**

**Model Answer:**

A **tacho generator** is an electromechanical transducer that converts the rotational speed (angular velocity) of a mechanical shaft into a proportional electrical voltage signal, used for speed measurement and feedback control.

The **two main types** are:
1. **DC Tacho Generator** — produces a DC output voltage proportional to speed. Uses permanent magnets for the field.
2. **AC Tacho Generator** — produces an AC output voltage whose amplitude is proportional to speed, but whose frequency is fixed (equal to the reference/excitation frequency). Common construction is the **drag-cup type**.

A typical sensitivity ranges from **5 V/kRPM to 100 V/kRPM**, and they are widely used in servo systems and paper mills.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a stroboscope? Explain the principle of operation in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

A **stroboscope** is a non-contact optical instrument used to measure the rotational speed of a rotating object by illuminating it with **intense, brief flashes of light** at an adjustable, known frequency.

**Principle of Operation:**

The device exploits the **persistence of human vision** (≈ **1/16 second ≈ 62.5 ms**). When the rotating object has $n$ identical reference marks and the stroboscope flashes at frequency $f$ such that the object advances by exactly **one mark-spacing** ($360°/n$) between two successive flashes, the object appears to **stand still** to the observer.

The RPM is then calculated as:

$$N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}$$

The two common types are the **mechanical disc-type stroboscope** and the **electronic xenon-flash stroboscope**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder**: Part B questions carry **14 marks** and consist of sub-parts (typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks). Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Sub-parts may further break down into (i), (ii), etc.

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **DC tacho generator**. Derive the EMF equation of a DC tacho generator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-pole, lap-wound DC tacho generator** has **480 armature conductors** and a flux per pole of **6 mWb**. Find the EMF generated when the armature rotates at **1800 RPM**. If the same machine were wave-wound, what would be the new EMF at the same speed? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Permanent Magnet Stator] → [Magnetic Field Φ per pole]
         ↓
[Rotating Armature with Z conductors] → [Induced EMF per conductor = B·l·v]
         ↓
[Commutator & Brushes] → [Ripple Filter] → [DC Voltmeter Output]
```

**Construction:**

The DC tacho generator consists of:
- A **stator housing** with **permanent magnets** (e.g., Alnico) producing a steady field flux $\Phi$ per pole.
- A **drum-wound armature** rotating on the shaft under measurement, with $Z$ total conductors.
- A **commutator** and **brush assembly** for DC rectification.
- Output terminals connected to a voltmeter or controller.

**Working:**

When the shaft rotates at $N$ RPM, the conductors cut the magnetic flux $\Phi$. By Faraday's Law, an EMF is induced in each conductor. The total EMF is collected via the commutator and appears across the brushes as a DC voltage proportional to $N$:

$$E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Derivation:** *(As shown in SECTION 3.1 — Step 1 to Step 4)*

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P\Phi$$

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$$

$$\text{EMF per conductor} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$$

$$\text{Conductors in series per path} = \dfrac{Z}{A}$$

$$\therefore E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60} \cdot \dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{P\Phi Z N}{60A} \;\; \text{Volts}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Number of poles, $P = 4$
- Winding type (1): **Lap-wound**, so $A_1 = P = 4$
- Total armature conductors, $Z = 480$
- Flux per pole, $\Phi = 6 \text{ mWb} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$
- Speed, $N = 1800 \text{ RPM}$

**Calculation for Lap-Wound Tacho:**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A_{\text{lap}}} = \dfrac{4 \times 6\times 10^{-3} \times 480 \times 1800}{60 \times 4}$$

Let us evaluate step-by-step:

**Numerator step:**

$$4 \times 6\times 10^{-3} = 24 \times 10^{-3} = 0.024$$

$$0.024 \times 480 = 11.52$$

$$11.52 \times 1800 = 20736$$

**Denominator step:**

$$60 \times 4 = 240$$

**Final division:**

$$E_{g,\text{lap}} = \dfrac{20736}{240} = 86.4 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Calculation for Wave-Wound Tacho:**

For wave winding, $A_2 = 2$, all other parameters same.

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A_{\text{wave}}} = \dfrac{4 \times 6\times 10^{-3} \times 480 \times 1800}{60 \times 2}$$

**Numerator (same as before):** $20736$

**Denominator step:**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Final division:**

$$E_{g,\text{wave}} = \dfrac{20736}{120} = 172.8 \;\; \text{V}$$

**Verification of Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{E_{g,\text{wave}}}{E_{g,\text{lap}}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{lap}}}{A_{\text{wave}}} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2 \;\;\checkmark$$

Indeed, $172.8 / 86.4 = 2$. ✅

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{\,E_{g,\text{lap-wound}} = 86.4 \; \text{V}\,} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\,E_{g,\text{wave-wound}} = 172.8 \; \text{V}\,}$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **principle and operation of a stroboscope** for measuring the speed of a rotating shaft. Derive the formula relating the rotational speed to the flash frequency and number of reference marks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotating shaft has **8 equispaced slots** on its periphery. A stroboscope is used to measure its speed. The stroboscope flashes at a frequency that produces a stationary single image when the dial reads **22 Hz**. Calculate:  
 (i) The actual speed of the shaft in RPM.  
 (ii) The angular velocity in rad/s.  
 (iii) The peripheral linear speed of a point on the shaft at a radius of **0.25 m**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle and Operation:**

A stroboscope is a non-contact speed-measuring instrument based on the principle of **persistence of human vision** (≈ **1/16 s**).

The rotating object is fitted (or naturally carries) **$n$ equally spaced reference marks** (slots, holes, vanes, or radial lines). The instrument emits **intense, brief flashes of light** at an adjustable frequency $f$ Hz. The observer views the rotating object under this flashing illumination.

When the stroboscope flash frequency is tuned such that the object advances by exactly one mark-spacing ($360°/n$) between two successive flashes, every flash illuminates the same mark in the same angular position — the object appears **frozen** to the observer.

**Derivation of the Formula:**

Let the object rotate at $N$ RPM, with $n$ marks.

Time for one complete revolution:

$$T_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{60}{N} \;\; \text{s}$$

Time for one mark-spacing:

$$T_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot n} \;\; \text{s}$$

For a stationary image, flash interval = segment time:

$$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot n}$$

Solving for $N$:

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n}\,}$$

**Labelled Diagram (textual representation):**

```
       ROTATING SHAFT (with n slots)
              |
              ↓
    [STROBOSCOPE: emits flashes at f Hz]
              |
              ↓
       FLASH ILLUMINATION
              |
              ↓
       HUMAN EYE / OBSERVER
       (sees a FROZEN image when 1/f = 60/(N·n))
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Number of slots, $n = 8$
- Stroboscope flash frequency for stationary image, $f = 22 \text{ Hz}$
- Radius of shaft, $r = 0.25 \text{ m}$

**(i) Actual Speed of Shaft in RPM:**

Applying the stroboscope formula:

$$N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{n} = \dfrac{60 \times 22}{8} = \dfrac{1320}{8} = 165 \;\; \text{RPM}$$

$$\boxed{\,N = 165 \; \text{RPM}\,}$$

**(ii) Angular Velocity in rad/s:**

Converting RPM to rad/s using:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 165}{60} = \dfrac{330\pi}{60} = 5.5\pi$$

$$\omega = 5.5 \times 3.14159 = 17.279 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,\omega \approx 17.28 \; \text{rad/s}\,}$$

**(iii) Peripheral Linear Speed at $r = 0.25$ m:**

The peripheral linear speed is given by:

$$v = \omega \cdot r$$

$$v = 17.279 \times 0.25 = 4.3197 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v \approx 4.32 \; \text{m/s}\,}$$

**Verification by alternative path:**
- RPS = 165/60 = 2.75 rev/s
- One revolution sweeps circumference $2\pi r = 2\pi \times 0.25 = 1.5708$ m
- Peripheral speed $v = 2.75 \times 1.5708 = 4.3197$ m/s ✅

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Top 5 Marks-Deduction Traps — Tacho Generators & Stroboscope**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mWb to Wb**: When $\Phi$ is given in **mWb**, students often forget the $10^{-3}$ factor, leading to an answer **1000× too small**. Always convert $\Phi$ to Webers before substituting.
>
> 2. **Confusing $A = 2$ vs $A = P$**: Many students write the EMF formula with $A = 2$ even for a lap-wound machine. **Remember**: $A_{\text{wave}} = 2$ and $A_{\text{lap}} = P$.
>
> 3. **Stroboscope — forgetting the $n$ in the denominator**: If the rotating shaft has multiple reference marks and the student uses $N = 60f$ instead of $N = 60f/n$, the answer will be inflated by a factor of $n$. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU stroboscope problems.
>
> 4. **Mixing up RPM and RPS**: The factor of 60 in the EMF equation comes from converting RPM → RPS. If $N$ is given in RPS, drop the 60. Examiners **will** check this conversion.
>
> 5. **No diagram or unlabelled diagram in part (a)**: KTU examiners typically award **1 to 2 marks** purely for the labelled diagram in construction questions. Skipping it is a guaranteed marks loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this and revise before the exam!**

### 🔹 Tacho Generator — Key Points

- ⭐ A tacho generator is an **electromechanical speed transducer**, not a power generator.
- ⭐ DC tacho generator uses **permanent magnet field** (no external excitation needed).
- ⭐ **EMF equation**: $E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi Z N}{60A}$ — **must be memorised verbatim**.
- ⭐ Wave wound: $A = 2$; Lap wound: $A = P$.
- ⭐ AC tacho generator (drag-cup type) output **amplitude is proportional to speed**, **frequency is fixed** by reference supply.
- ⭐ DC tacho limitations: brush wear, commutator ripple, armature reaction, temperature drift.
- ⭐ Applications: servo speed feedback, paper machines, CNC, military servo systems.

### 🔹 Stroboscope — Key Points

- ⭐ **Non-contact** speed measurement — uses light only.
- ⭐ Based on **persistence of vision** ($\approx 1/16$ s).
- ⭐ **Master formula**: $N_{\text{RPM}} = \dfrac{60 f}{n}$ — **must be memorised verbatim**.
- ⭐ For $n = 1$ (single reference mark): $N = 60 f$.
- ⭐ Mechanical stroboscope uses a **multi-slot disc** + lamp; Electronic uses **xenon flash tube** + oscillator.
- ⭐ Multiple stationary images can occur at $f$, $2f$, $3f$, … — always pick the **lowest** flash rate.
- ⭐ Advantages: non-contact, no loading, can measure inaccessible parts.
- ⭐ Disadvantages: requires visual judgement, marks may be hard to place, bright external light affects accuracy.

### 🔹 Final Mantra for KTU Module 2

| If the question asks about… | Use… |
|---|---|
| DC voltage proportional to speed | DC Tacho Generator equations |
| AC voltage amplitude proportional to speed, fixed frequency | AC (Drag-Cup) Tacho Generator |
| Non-contact speed measurement using light | Stroboscope equation |
| Speed feedback in a servo motor | Tacho Generator (both types) |
| Helicopter rotor inspection | Electronic Stroboscope |
| EMF in a rotating armature | $E_g = \dfrac{P\Phi ZN}{60A}$ |
| RPM from a flashing light + reference marks | $N = \dfrac{60f}{n}$ |

---

> 🏁 **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 Notes — Tacho Generators and Stroboscope**  
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol — NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme Aligned*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing of Signal Conditioning Circuits (Bridges) for Resistive, Capacitive and Inductive Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Designing of Signal Conditioning Circuits (Bridges) for Resistive, Capacitive and Inductive Transducers # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Signal Conditioning — The Engineer's Pre-Amplifier In any practical measurement system, the raw output of a transducer is rarely in a form suitable for direct display, recording, or analog-to-digital conversion. It is usually too small in amplitude, buried in noise, contaminated by common-mode voltages, or corrupted by environmental drift. The block of circuitry that sits **between the transducer and the rest of the measurement chain** and is responsible for cleaning, converting, and linearising this signal is called a **Signal Conditioning Circuit (SCC)**. Among all SCC topologies, the **bridge circuit** is the single most important and ubiquitous building block. A bridge converts an *impedance change* (produced by a transducer under the influence of the measurand) into a proportional *voltage* that can be amplified, digitised, or displayed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (PCAET303, Module 2):** > A **bridge circuit** is a null-type or deflection-type signal conditioning network comprising four impedance arms arranged in a two-parallel-branch configuration. When excited by a known voltage or current source, the bridge produces an output voltage that is a function of the imbalance in its arms, which in turn is a function of the measurand acting on the transducer placed in one (or more) of its arms. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Balance Scale Imagine an old-fashioned two-pan balance scale used by a grocer. When both pans carry the **same weight**, the beam is perfectly horizontal — this is the **balanced (null)** state. The moment you place an unknown object on one pan, the beam tilts — the **amount of tilt** tells you how much weight is unaccounted for. A bridge circuit works on exactly the same principle: | Balance Scale | Bridge Circuit | |---|---| | Two pans | Two parallel branches of impedances | | Calibrated counter-weights | Known fixed impedances (reference arms) | | Unknown weight | Transducer (whose impedance changes with measurand) | | Beam tilt angle | Output voltage $V_o$ | | Calibrated scale on beam | Calibration / sensitivity constant | > [!NOTE] > **Why bridges are the preferred SCC for transducers:** > 1. They inherently **cancel out** the quiescent (static) part of the signal and amplify only the *change* produced by the measurand. > 2. They provide excellent **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** because both output terminals swing symmetrically about half the excitation voltage. > 3. They naturally support **differential measurement**, which is the most effective way to reject stray noise, lead resistance, and thermal EMF. > 4. They are extremely **linear** in the small-signal region around balance. ## 1.3 Classification of Bridges Used in Transducer Signal Conditioning Based on the type of excitation and the nature of the transducer impedance being sensed, bridges are classified as: 1. **DC Bridges** — excited by a stable DC voltage or current. Used for **resistive transducers** such as Strain Gauges, RTDs, Thermistors, and Potentiometric sensors. 2. **AC Bridges** — excited by a sinusoidal AC source of known frequency (typically **1 kHz** or **50 Hz / 60 Hz** mains). Used for **capacitive transducers** (capacitive displacement, humidity, level sensors) and **inductive transducers** (LVDT, variable reluctance pickups, eddy-current probes). > [!TIP] > **Physical constants / standard metrics you must remember:** > - Standard bridge excitation: $\mathbf{5\text{ V DC}}$ (laboratory) to $\mathbf{10\text{ V DC}}$ (industrial) > - Standard AC excitation frequency: $\mathbf{1\text{ kHz}}$ to $\mathbf{5\text{ kHz}}$ > - LVDT carrier frequency: $\mathbf{1\text{ kHz}}$ to $\mathbf{10\text{ kHz}}$ > - Minimum detectable resistance change in a quality strain gauge bridge: $\mathbf{10^{-4}\ \Omega}$ # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Wheatstone Bridge (DC Bridge for Resistive Transducers) The Wheatstone bridge, invented by Samuel Hunter Christie in **1833** and popularised by Sir Charles Wheatstone in **1843**, is the workhorse of resistive transducer signal conditioning. ### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology The bridge has four resistive arms — $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$ — connected in a closed loop. A DC excitation $V_{ex}$ is applied between one pair of opposite nodes (A–C), and the output voltage $V_o$ is measured across the other pair (B–D). - Arms $R_1$ and $R_2$ form one **voltage divider**. - Arms $R_3$ and $R_4$ form the second voltage divider. - The output is the **difference of the two divider voltages**. ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic - **Step 1 — Apply excitation:** $V_{ex}$ is applied between nodes A and C. - **Step 2 — Form two voltage dividers:** Node B (top of second divider) sits at: $V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$ Node D (bottom of first divider) sits at: $V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ - **Step 3 — Take the difference:** $V_o = V_B - V_D$ - **Step 4 — Null/Balance condition:** $V_o = 0$ when $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$ (or equivalently $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$). - **Step 5 — Deflection mode:** A small change $\Delta R$ in one arm produces a proportional $V_o$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Modes of Operation:** > - **Null Mode:** The bridge is manually (or automatically) re-balanced by adjusting a known decade resistor. The unknown is computed from the balance equation. Highly accurate but slow. > - **Deflection Mode:** The bridge is intentionally operated off-balance; $V_o$ is read directly and is proportional to $\Delta R$. Fast, suitable for dynamic measurements, but the output is slightly non-linear for large $\Delta R$. ## 2.2 AC Bridges — General Theory When the transducer is **capacitive** (change in $C$) or **inductive** (change in $L$), DC excitation is useless because the device either blocks DC (capacitor) or presents only its winding resistance (inductor at DC). An AC excitation $V_{ex} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is used. Each arm now contains a **complex impedance** $Z_i = R_i + jX_i$. The balance condition must be satisfied in **both magnitude and phase**, giving **two independent equations** from a single bridge — enough to solve for the two unknown quantities ($L$ and $r$, or $C$ and $\tan\delta$). ### 2.2.1 General AC Bridge Balance Equation For the bridge arms $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4$ in the standard configuration, balance requires: $$Z_1 \cdot Z_4 = Z_2 \cdot Z_3$$ > [!NOTE] > **The "Opposite-Arm Product" Rule:** This is the golden rule you will use to derive every AC bridge equation in the KTU exam. Products of impedances in *opposite* arms must be equal in **magnitude** AND in **phase angle**. ### 2.2.2 Why Two Equations Appear Substituting $Z_i = \vert Z_i \vert \angle \theta_i$: $$\vert Z_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_4 \vert = \vert Z_2 \vert \cdot \vert Z_3 \vert \quad \text{(magnitude balance)}$$ $$\theta_1 + \theta_4 = \theta_2 + \theta_3 \quad \text{(phase balance)}$$ This naturally produces a **real** equation (resistive balance) and a **reactive** equation (capacitive/inductive balance). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Bridge / Quantity | Governing Formula | Output / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wheatstone — Balance | $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ | $V_o = 0$ | | 2 | Wheatstone — Output (general) | $V_o = V_{ex}\left[\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$ | Deflection mode | | 3 | Wheatstone — Output (single arm $\Delta R$, $R_1$) | $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R} \cdot \Delta R$ | Small-signal output | | 4 | Wheatstone — Differential (adjacent arms) | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2R} \cdot \Delta R$ | Doubled sensitivity | | 5 | Wheatstone — Differential (opposite arms) | $V_o = 0$ | **Cancels** the change — never use this config for sensing | | 6 | Sensitivity of Wheatstone bridge | $S = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R}$ | Volts per Ohm | | 7 | General AC bridge balance | $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ | Two real equations | | 8 | Maxwell Bridge — $L_x$ | $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$ | Measures $L_x$ (medium $Q$) | | 9 | Maxwell Bridge — $R_x$ | $R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$ | Measures winding resistance | | 10 | Hay Bridge — $L_x$ | $L_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_1}{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}$ | High-$Q$ inductors | | 11 | Hay Bridge — $R_x$ | $R_x = \dfrac{\omega^2 R_1 R_2 R_3 C_1^2}{1+(\omega R_1 C_1)^2}$ | High-$Q$ inductors | | 12 | Schering Bridge — $C_x$ (series) | $C_x = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}$ | Lossy capacitor | | 13 | Schering Bridge — $r_x$ (series ESR) | $r_x = \dfrac{R_3 C_1}{C_2}$ | Equivalent series resistance | | 14 | Schering — Dissipation factor | $D = \tan\delta = \omega C_1 R_1$ | Capacitor quality | | 15 | Anderson Bridge — $L_x$ | $L_x = R_1 R_3 C$ | Accurate $L$ measurement | | 16 | Wien Bridge — Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Frequency measurement | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Strain gauge bridges** are used in load cells, pressure sensors, and torque sensors. > - **Schering bridges** are used to test insulation quality of high-voltage cables and transformer oil. > - **Maxwell / Hay / Anderson bridges** are used in laboratory LCR meters and Q-meters. > - **Wien bridges** are used in audio oscillators (the famous Wien-Bridge Oscillator) and in frequency-selective filters. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of a Wheatstone Bridge (General Case) **Objective:** Derive the expression for the open-circuit output voltage $V_o$ of the Wheatstone bridge when the arms have arbitrary resistances $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and excitation $V_{ex}$. **Step 1 — Identify the two voltage dividers.** Arms $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a series voltage divider across the excitation. Similarly, $R_3$ and $R_4$ form a second divider. The output nodes B and D are the midpoints. **Step 2 — Compute the potential at node D** (the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$): $$V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the potential at node B** (the junction of $R_3$ and $R_4$): $$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$ **Step 4 — Take the difference** to obtain the open-circuit output: $$V_o = V_B - V_D = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Re-arrange for the canonical form** used in KTU textbooks: $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right]$$ (some books use either sign; what matters is the magnitude). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single Active Arm Configuration (Deflection Mode) **Objective:** Show that for a small change $\Delta R$ in one arm of an initially balanced bridge with all arms equal to $R$, the output is approximately $V_o \approx V_{ex}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$. **Step 1 — Initial balance.** All four arms are $R$, so $V_o = 0$. **Step 2 — Apply a small change.** Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, and the other arms remain $R$. **Step 3 — Substitute into the general output equation:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{(R+\Delta R)}{(R+\Delta R)+R} - \frac{R}{R+R}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Simplify each term separately:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{2R+\Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Combine over a common denominator:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2(R+\Delta R) - (2R+\Delta R)}{2(2R+\Delta R)}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Expand the numerator:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)}\right] = V_{ex}\left[\frac{\Delta R}{2(2R+\Delta R)}\right]$$ **Step 7 — Apply the small-signal approximation** $\Delta R \ll R$ so that $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$: $$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{4R}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the most-quoted formula for any KTU question on bridge sensitivity.** Memorise it verbatim: $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R}\cdot \Delta R$. **Step 8 — Sensitivity.** Sensitivity is the change in output per unit change in resistance: $$S = \frac{\partial V_o}{\partial(\Delta R)} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4R}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential (Two Adjacent Active Arms) Configuration **Objective:** Show that if $R_1$ becomes $R+\Delta R$ and $R_2$ becomes $R-\Delta R$ (e.g., a strain gauge in tension and its partner in compression), the output is **doubled** and **linear** in $\Delta R$. **Step 1 — Substitute into the general equation:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{(R+\Delta R)}{(R+\Delta R)+(R-\Delta R)} - \frac{R}{R+R}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Simplify each fraction:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Combine:** $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2(R+\Delta R) - 2R}{2 \cdot 2R}\right] = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R}$$ **Step 4 — Comment on the result.** Unlike the single-arm case, this expression is **exactly linear** in $\Delta R$ for all values — no small-signal approximation is needed. The sensitivity is doubled to $V_{ex}/(2R)$. > [!TIP] > **Why "adjacent" arms and not "opposite" arms?** > - In a strain gauge rosette, the two gauges in tension and compression are mounted on *opposite* arms of the bridge (e.g., $R_1$ and $R_3$). Both increase (or both decrease) by $\Delta R$ due to Poisson's effect on the test piece, and this **adds** at the output. > - The two arms are typically on the **same side** of the bridge, but the standard terminology in KTU textbooks is "adjacent arms change in opposite sense, opposite arms change in the same sense, and these add at the output." ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — General AC Bridge Balance **Objective:** Starting from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, prove that the balance condition is $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$. **Step 1 — Apply KVL around loop ABDA** (with no current in the detector at balance): $$I_1 Z_1 = I_2 Z_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_2 = I_1 \cdot \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}$$ **Step 2 — Apply KVL around loop BCDB:** $$I_1 Z_2 = I_2 Z_4$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $I_2$:** $$I_1 Z_2 = \left(I_1 \cdot \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}\right) Z_4$$ **Step 4 — Cancel $I_1$ and rearrange:** $$Z_2 Z_3 = Z_1 Z_4$$ This is the **fundamental balance equation** for any AC bridge. ✓ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Maxwell's Bridge (for Inductance Measurement) **Bridge Configuration:** - Arm 1: Unknown inductance $L_x$ in series with resistance $R_x$ (the unknown) - Arm 2: Known non-inductive resistor $R_2$ - Arm 3: Known non-inductive resistor $R_3$ - Arm 4: Known non-inductive resistor $R_4$ in **parallel** with known standard capacitor $C_1$ **Impedances of each arm:** $$Z_1 = R_x + j\omega L_x, \quad Z_2 = R_2, \quad Z_3 = R_3, \quad Z_4 = \frac{R_4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}}{R_4 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}} = \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_1}$$ **Step 1 — Apply the balance equation $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:** $$(R_x + j\omega L_x) \cdot \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_1} = R_2 R_3$$ **Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $(1 + j\omega R_4 C_1)$:** $$(R_x + j\omega L_x) R_4 = R_2 R_3 (1 + j\omega R_4 C_1)$$ **Step 3 — Expand both sides:** $$R_4 R_x + j\omega R_4 L_x = R_2 R_3 + j\omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_1$$ **Step 4 — Equate real parts:** $$R_4 R_x = R_2 R_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{R_x = \frac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}}$$ **Step 5 — Equate imaginary parts:** $$\omega R_4 L_x = \omega R_2 R_3 R_4 C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key observation:** The expressions for $R_x$ and $L_x$ are **independent of frequency** $\omega$. This makes the Maxwell bridge ideal for measuring inductors with medium-to-low $Q$ factors ($1 < Q < 10$). ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Schering Bridge (for Capacitance & Loss Measurement) **Bridge Configuration:** - Arm 1: Unknown capacitor $C_x$ with equivalent series resistance $r_x$ (the unknown) - Arm 2: Pure standard capacitor $C_2$ - Arm 3: Pure non-inductive resistor $R_3$ - Arm 4: Standard non-inductive resistor $R_4$ in **parallel** with standard capacitor $C_4$ **Impedances:** $$Z_1 = r_x + \frac{1}{j\omega C_x}, \quad Z_2 = \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}, \quad Z_3 = R_3, \quad Z_4 = \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4}$$ **Step 1 — Apply $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$:** $$\left(r_x + \frac{1}{j\omega C_x}\right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{1 + j\omega R_4 C_4} = \frac{R_3}{j\omega C_2}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $j\omega (1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)$:** $$(j\omega r_x C_x + 1) \cdot \frac{R_4 C_x}{C_x} = \frac{R_3 C_x (1 + j\omega R_4 C_4)}{C_2}$$ *(Simplification: multiply the LHS $r_x + 1/(j\omega C_x)$ by $j\omega C_x$ to obtain $j\omega r_x C_x + 1$ which equals $1 + j\omega r_x C_x$.)* **Step 3 — After standard algebraic manipulation** (expand, equate real & imaginary parts): $$\boxed{C_x = \frac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}}$$ $$\boxed{r_x = \frac{R_3 C_1}{C_2}} \quad \text{(where } C_1 \text{ is the variable standard capacitor in arm 4)}$$ **Step 4 — Dissipation factor:** $$D = \tan\delta = \omega C_x r_x = \omega R_4 C_4$$ This last result is independent of the unknown — a beautiful property of the Schering bridge, and the reason it is used as a **high-voltage insulation tester**. ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A Wheatstone bridge has $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 350\ \Omega$. A strain gauge of gauge factor $G_f = 2.1$ is mounted on a beam and experiences a strain $\varepsilon = 800\ \mu\varepsilon$. The bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10\text{ V DC}$. Find the bridge output voltage. **Step 1 — Compute the change in resistance of the strain gauge:** $$\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 350 \cdot 2.1 \cdot 800 \times 10^{-6} = 0.588\ \Omega$$ **Step 2 — Apply the small-signal output formula:** $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4R} \cdot \Delta R = \frac{10}{4 \times 350} \cdot 0.588 = \frac{5.88}{1400} = 4.2\ \text{mV}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** A strain of 800 microstrain produces only 4.2 mV. This is why bridge outputs are *always* followed by a high-gain instrumentation amplifier in practical instrumentation. ✓ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Wheatstone Bridge — Functional Block Architecture The diagram below shows the four-arm topology, the excitation source, the differential output nodes, and the transducer location. Note that the active arm (containing the transducer) is highlighted separately from the three reference arms. ```mermaid graph TD Vex["DC EXCITATION Vex"] --> NodeA["NODE A (Top)"] NodeA --> R1["R1 - TRANSDUCER ARM
Variable Resistance"] R1 --> NodeD["NODE D (Output +)"] NodeA --> R3["R3 - REFERENCE ARM
Fixed Resistor"] R3 --> NodeB["NODE B (Output -)"] NodeD --> R2["R2 - REFERENCE ARM
Fixed Resistor"] R2 --> NodeC["NODE C (Bottom)"] NodeB --> R4["R4 - REFERENCE ARM
Fixed Resistor"] R4 --> NodeC NodeC --> Vex NodeD --> VoutPos["Vo +"] NodeB --> VoutNeg["Vo -"] VoutPos --> Amp["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER
High CMRR, Gain 100-1000"] VoutNeg --> Amp Amp --> Display["ADC / DISPLAY / CONTROLLER"] ``` ## 4.2 AC Bridge — General Architecture with Detector ```mermaid graph LR Osc["AC OSCILLATOR
1 kHz - 10 kHz
Sine Wave Excitation"] --> Bridge["AC BRIDGE
4 Impedance Arms
Z1, Z2, Z3, Z4"] Bridge --> Det["NULL DETECTOR
AC Voltmeter or
Headphones or
Phase-Sensitive Detector"] Det --> Readout["BALANCE READOUT
R2, R3, R4 dials
or Null Indicator"] Z1arm["Arm 1: Unknown
L and r or C and tan delta"] --> Bridge Z2arm["Arm 2: Reference R2"] --> Bridge Z3arm["Arm 3: Reference R3"] --> Bridge Z4arm["Arm 4: Reference R4 + C1"] --> Bridge ``` ## 4.3 Maxwell Bridge — Functional Layout ```mermaid graph TD A["NODE A"] --> Lx["Arm 1: Lx + Rx
Unknown Inductor"] Lx --> D["NODE D"] A --> R3a["Arm 3: R3
Pure Resistor"] R3a --> B["NODE B"] D --> R2a["Arm 2: R2
Pure Resistor"] R2a --> C["NODE C"] B --> Z4a["Arm 4: R4 parallel C1
Variable Standard"] Z4a --> C C --> A D --> OutP["Vo +"] B --> OutN["Vo -"] ``` ## 4.4 Schering Bridge — Functional Layout ```mermaid graph TD A["NODE A"] --> Cx["Arm 1: Cx + rx
Unknown Capacitor"] Cx --> D["NODE D"] A --> R3b["Arm 3: R3
Pure Resistor"] R3b --> B["NODE B"] D --> C2b["Arm 2: C2
Pure Standard Capacitor"] C2b --> C["NODE C"] B --> Z4b["Arm 4: R4 parallel C4
Variable Standard"] Z4b --> C C --> A D --> OutP2["Vo +"] B --> OutN2["Vo -"] ``` ## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Bridge ```mermaid graph TD Start["Transducer Type?"] --> Resistive["R changes
Strain Gauge / RTD"] Start --> Capacitive["C changes
Displacement / Level"] Start --> Inductive["L changes
LVDT / Reluctance"] Resistive --> DC["Use DC BRIDGE
Wheatstone"] Capacitive --> Schering["Use SCHERING BRIDGE
For C and tan delta"] Capacitive --> Wien["Use WIEN BRIDGE
For Frequency or C"] Inductive --> Maxwell["Use MAXWELL BRIDGE
For Low Q Coil"] Inductive --> Hay["Use HAY BRIDGE
For High Q Coil"] Inductive --> Anderson["Use ANDERSON BRIDGE
For Accurate L"] ``` ## 4.6 Equivalent Functional Block of a Complete Bridge-Based Measurement System ```mermaid graph LR M["MEASURAND
Force / Temp / Displacement"] --> T["TRANSDUCER
R, L, or C changes"] T --> Bridge["BRIDGE SCC
Wheatstone / AC Bridge"] Bridge --> IA["INSTRUMENTATION AMP
Gain A, CMRR > 100 dB"] IA --> Filter["LOW-PASS FILTER
Removes Carrier Noise"] Filter --> ADC["ADC"] ADC --> Micro["MICROCONTROLLER / DSP"] Micro --> Display["LCD / 7-Seg Display"] Micro --> Comms["RS-485 / 4-20 mA Output"] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define a signal conditioning circuit. Why is a bridge circuit the most commonly used signal conditioner for transducer outputs?** **Model Answer:** A signal conditioning circuit is the electronic network that processes the raw output of a transducer into a form suitable for further processing, transmission, or display. Typical functions include amplification, filtering, linearisation, isolation, and impedance matching. A bridge circuit is preferred because: 1. It inherently **converts an impedance change into a voltage**, which is the most convenient signal form. 2. It provides **inherent linearity** near the null point and excellent **common-mode noise rejection** because the output is the *difference* of two divider voltages. 3. It allows the **quiescent (DC bias) component to be cancelled**, so the amplifier only has to handle the small *change* produced by the measurand. 4. It can be configured to **double the sensitivity** (differential / two-active-arm configuration). 5. It is equally suited to **resistive, capacitive, and inductive** transducers by simply changing the excitation from DC to AC. *(Marks distribution: Definition – 1 mark, Any 4 points – 2 marks.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the balance condition of a general AC bridge. Why does a single AC bridge produce two independent equations at balance?** **Model Answer:** The balance condition of a general AC bridge is: $$Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$$ In polar form, this single complex equation can be written as two real equations: $$\vert Z_1 \vert \cdot \vert Z_4 \vert = \vert Z_2 \vert \cdot \vert Z_3 \vert \quad \text{(magnitude balance)}$$ $$\angle Z_1 + \angle Z_4 = \angle Z_2 + \angle Z_3 \quad \text{(phase balance)}$$ Since most AC bridge arms contain both resistive and reactive elements, the two equations are **independent** — one becomes the *resistive balance* (real part) and the other becomes the *reactive balance* (imaginary part). This is precisely why AC bridges can measure **two unknown quantities simultaneously** (e.g., both $L_x$ and $R_x$ of an unknown inductor, or both $C_x$ and $\tan\delta$ of an unknown capacitor). *(Marks distribution: Statement – 1 mark, Two real equations – 1 mark, Reason for two independent equations – 1 mark.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Derive the general expression for the output voltage of an unbalanced Wheatstone bridge. Show that for a small change $\Delta R$ in one arm of an initially balanced bridge, the output is $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{4R}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A strain gauge of nominal resistance $350\ \Omega$ and gauge factor $2.05$ is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. All other arms are $350\ \Omega$ fixed resistors. The bridge is excited by a $10\text{ V}$ DC source. When a load is applied, the gauge resistance changes by $0.7\ \Omega$. Calculate: (i) the strain, and (ii) the bridge output voltage. If two such gauges (one in tension, one in compression) are placed in adjacent arms, what is the new output voltage? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Solution (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes for the full derivation. The final result is: $$V_o = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right]$$ With $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$, and for $\Delta R \ll R$: $$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{4R}$$ **Solution (b):** **(i) Strain calculation:** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{G_f} = \frac{0.7 / 350}{2.05} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.05} = 9.756 \times 10^{-4} = 975.6\ \mu\varepsilon$$ **(ii) Output voltage — single active arm:** $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{4R} = \frac{10 \times 0.7}{4 \times 350} = \frac{7}{1400} = 5.0\ \text{mV}$$ **(iii) Output voltage — two adjacent active arms:** With the second gauge changing by $-\Delta R$ in the adjacent arm, the differential configuration applies: $$V_{o,\text{diff}} = \frac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{2R} = \frac{10 \times 0.7}{2 \times 350} = \frac{7}{700} = 10.0\ \text{mV}$$ **Observation:** The output has **doubled** from 5.0 mV to 10.0 mV. This is exactly why real-world load cells and pressure sensors always use at least two active gauges in a differential configuration. ✓ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Draw the circuit of a Maxwell bridge for the measurement of an unknown inductance. Derive the conditions for balance and the expressions for $L_x$ and $R_x$ in terms of the bridge components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Draw the circuit of a Schering bridge for the measurement of an unknown capacitance and its dissipation factor. Derive the expressions for $C_x$, $r_x$ and $\tan\delta$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Solution (a):** Refer to **Section 3.5** and the Maxwell bridge diagram in **Section 4.3** for the derivation. The balance conditions are: $$R_x = \frac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$$ $$L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$$ **Important features of the Maxwell bridge:** - The expressions are **independent of frequency**, so the bridge is not affected by small drifts in the AC source frequency. - It is most accurate for **low-to-medium Q coils** ($1 < Q < 10$). For high-Q coils, the Hay bridge is preferred. - Standard capacitor $C_1$ is variable (decade capacitor) and is the primary balancing element, with $R_4$ as the secondary fine balance. - The convergence of balance is straightforward (no cross-coupling between $L_x$ and $R_x$ controls). **Solution (b):** Refer to **Section 3.6** and the Schering bridge diagram in **Section 4.4** for the derivation. The balance conditions are: $$C_x = \frac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}$$ $$r_x = \frac{R_3 C_1}{C_2}$$ $$\tan\delta = \omega C_1 R_1 = \omega R_4 C_4$$ **Important features of the Schering bridge:** - Used for testing **insulating materials** (cable insulation, transformer oil, porcelain bushings) at high voltages. - The expression for $\tan\delta$ is **independent of the unknown** — a major practical advantage. - Sensitivity is high because $C_2$ is a high-quality air or gas-filled standard capacitor with very low loss. - The bridge can be **fully shielded** against stray capacitances by using a Wagner earth connection (often asked as a supplementary question in KTU). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in bridge-circuit questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the "small-signal" approximation step.** Marks are awarded for explicitly writing "$2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$ for $\Delta R \ll R$". If you just write the answer $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}\Delta R}{4R}$ without showing the intermediate unsimplified form, the examiner *will* deduct 1 mark. > 2. **Sign confusion in the differential bridge.** If two adjacent arms change in *opposite* senses, the changes **add** at the output and the sensitivity is $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{2R}$, not $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R}$. If they change in the *same* sense, the change is $2\Delta R$ and the formula is also $V_{ex}/(2R)$ — write both cases clearly. > 3. **Mixing up Maxwell and Hay bridge applicability.** Maxwell → low $Q$; Hay → high $Q$. Hay bridge expressions **do** contain $\omega$ explicitly. > 4. **Using the wrong balance rule sign.** The condition is $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ (opposite arms equal). Some students write $Z_1 Z_2 = Z_3 Z_4$ and lose all derivation marks. > 5. **Not specifying the units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners award 0.5 marks for correct units on every numerical sub-part. --- ## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Every Exam** ### ✅ Core Wheatstone Bridge Facts - Balance: $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ - Output: $V_o = V_{ex}\left[\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4}\right]$ - Small-signal output: $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}\cdot \Delta R}{4R}$ - Sensitivity: $S = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4R}$ - Differential sensitivity: $S_{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2R}$ (twice the single-arm value) ### ✅ Core AC Bridge Facts - Master balance equation: $Z_1 Z_4 = Z_2 Z_3$ - Produces TWO independent real equations - Maxwell → $L_x = R_2 R_3 C_1$, $R_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3}{R_4}$, **frequency-independent**, low-to-medium $Q$ - Hay → $L_x = \dfrac{R_2 R_3 C_1}{1+(\omega R_1 C_1)^2}$, **frequency-dependent**, high $Q$ - Schering → $C_x = \dfrac{C_2 R_4}{R_3}$, $r_x = \dfrac{R_3 C_1}{C_2}$, $\tan\delta = \omega C_4 R_4$ - Anderson → highly accurate $L_x$ measurement; uses auxiliary fixed resistor and capacitor ### ✅ Conceptual Must-Knows - Adjacent arms: changes **add** (best configuration for sensing) - Opposite arms: changes **cancel** (never use for sensing — used for temperature compensation) - AC bridges require **Wagner earthing** for stray-capacitance neutralisation - Instrumentation amplifier with **CMRR > 100 dB** is mandatory after the bridge output - Real transducers always have **lead resistance** — compensate using **Kelvin (4-wire) connection** or **3-wire bridge** ### ✅ Numerical Shortcuts - For a strain gauge: $\Delta R = R \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ - For a thermistor in a bridge: use the Steinhart-Hart equation for high accuracy - For an LVDT in a bridge: the two secondary windings form the two opposite arms ### ✅ KTU Favourite Questions to Practise 1. Derivation of Wheatstone bridge output (7-mark derivations are routine) 2. Maxwell bridge derivation (asked almost every year) 3. Schering bridge + $\tan\delta$ (very common) 4. Numerical on strain gauge output voltage (3–7 marks) 5. Choice between Maxwell and Hay for a given $Q$ value (2-mark conceptual) > **End of Module 2 — Signal Conditioning Bridges Notes. Best of luck for your KTU exams!** 🎓

topics.title: Active Transducers - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Transducers - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Active Transducer** is a device that converts a non-electrical physical quantity (the measurand) into an equivalent electrical signal **without requiring an external excitation (auxiliary) power source**. It functions as an **electromechanical or thermoelectric energy converter**, generating its own output voltage, current, or charge in direct response to the applied physical stimulus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"Active transducers are self-generating devices that produce an electrical output proportional to the measurand, exploiting physical phenomena such as the piezoelectric effect, thermoelectric effect, photoelectric effect, electromagnetic induction, and Hall effect."*

The general energy conversion relationship for any active transducer is expressed as:

$$
E_{\text{out}} = k \cdot f(E_{\text{in}})
$$

where $E_{\text{out}}$ is the generated electrical energy, $f(E_{\text{in}})$ is the applied non-electrical input energy (mechanical, thermal, radiant), and $k$ is the transduction constant characteristic of the underlying physical effect.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Built-In Generator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a **self-powered bicycle light**. When the wheel turns, a small dynamo mounted on the tire generates electricity that lights the bulb — *you don't need a battery or an external power socket*. The dynamo is the active transducer, the wheel motion is the measurand, and the glowing bulb represents the electrical output signal.
>
> In contrast, a **passive transducer** is like a *variable resistor* that only changes its resistance when something acts on it — it needs a battery (external excitation) to produce a useful signal.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features

| Feature | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| External power source | **Not required** | Required (excitation needed) |
| Energy conversion | **Self-generating** | Modulates external energy |
| Output nature | True voltage/current/charge | Resistance/capacitance/inductance change |
| Typical examples | Thermocouple, Piezo crystal, PV cell, Tachogenerator | RTD, Strain gauge, LVDT, Capacitive sensor |
| Signal amplitude | Self-generated (µV to V) | Depends on excitation level |

## 1.4 Physical Phenomena Exploited by Active Transducers

Active transducers exploit five primary natural physical effects:

- **Piezoelectric Effect** — mechanical stress → electrical charge
- **Thermoelectric / Seebeck Effect** — temperature difference → EMF
- **Photoelectric / Photovoltaic Effect** — light energy → voltage/current
- **Electromagnetic Induction (Faraday's Law)** — magnetic flux change → EMF
- **Hall Effect** — magnetic field → transverse voltage

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Remember the acronym **"PT-PH-FE"** → **P**iezo, **T**hermo, **P**hoto, **F**araday (Electromagnetic), **H**all — these are the *five* syllabus-listed effects for active transducers in KTU Module 3.

## 1.5 Block Representation

$$
\text{Measurand (Physical Quantity)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Energy Conversion via Physical Effect}}\; \text{Electrical Output Signal}
$$

The active transducer is therefore a **two-port, self-energized conversion element** in the measurement chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The core operating principle of every active transducer rests on the **direct coupling between a non-electrical domain and the electrical domain** through a known physical law. The device does not merely "modulate" an applied signal — it *generates* one.

### 2.1.1 Logical Operating Steps

1. **Input Energy Reception** — The transducer body is subjected to a physical stimulus (force, temperature, light, magnetic field, or motion).
2. **Physical-Effect Activation** — The internal sensing material (crystal, junction, semiconductor, coil) responds according to a defined physical law.
3. **Charge Separation / Carrier Motion** — Ions, electrons, or magnetic flux variations produce a measurable separation of charges or induced EMF.
4. **Electrical Output Generation** — A voltage $V_{\text{out}}$ or current $I_{\text{out}}$ proportional to the input measurand appears at the output terminals.
5. **Signal Conditioning Interface** — The output is routed to an amplifier or display unit for further processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this classification important for KTU?**
> The **self-generating** nature of active transducers gives them two engineering advantages:
> - **High signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at low frequencies because no excitation noise is introduced.
> - **Simpler signal chain** — fewer components, lower cost, better reliability in remote or hazardous locations.

## 2.2 Principle of Major Active Transducers

### 2.2.1 Piezoelectric Transducer

**Principle:** Certain crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, lead zirconate titanate — PZT) become electrically polarized when subjected to mechanical stress. The induced surface charge $Q$ is proportional to the applied force $F$.

$$
Q = d \cdot F
$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge constant** in **Coulomb/Newton (C/N)**. The output voltage is given by:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}
$$

where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the crystal (typically a few picofarads to nanofarads).

### 2.2.2 Thermoelectric (Thermocouple) Transducer

**Principle:** When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop and the two junctions are held at different temperatures, an EMF is generated (Seebeck Effect). The net thermoelectric EMF is:

$$
E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left( S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right) \, dT
$$

For small temperature differences, this simplifies to a linear relation:

$$
E \approx S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $S_{AB}$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair (in **µV/°C**).

### 2.2.3 Photoelectric (Photovoltaic) Transducer

**Principle:** When photons strike a semiconductor p–n junction (e.g., silicon, germanium), electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the junction's built-in electric field, producing a voltage across the terminals.

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

The short-circuit current is directly proportional to incident light intensity $L$:

$$
I_{SC} = K \cdot L
$$

### 2.2.4 Electromagnetic Induction Transducer (Tachogenerator)

**Principle:** A coil rotating in a uniform magnetic field, or a stationary coil in a changing magnetic flux, generates an EMF per Faraday's Law:

$$
e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

For a coil rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ in a uniform field $B$:

$$
e_{\text{inst}} = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t)
$$

with RMS value:

$$
E_{\text{rms}} = \frac{N B A \omega}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### 2.2.5 Hall Effect Transducer

**Principle:** When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a transverse magnetic field, a voltage develops perpendicular to both current and field directions:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}
$$

where $R_H$ is the **Hall coefficient**, $I$ is the current, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the thickness of the sensor element.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Cheat Sheet — Active Transducers**

| Transducer Type | Governing Physical Law | Key Output Equation | Sensitivity Unit | Typical Output Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{d F}{C_p}$ | $d$ (C/N) or $g$ (V·m/N) | mV to V |
| Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $E = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ | $E \approx S_{AB}(T_2 - T_1)$ | $S_{AB}$ (µV/°C) | µV to mV |
| Photovoltaic (Solar cell) | $I_{SC} = K \cdot L$ | $V = \dfrac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}+1\right)$ | $K$ (A/lux) | mV to V |
| Electromagnetic (Tachogenerator) | $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{NBA\omega}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $NBA$ (V·s/rad) | V to tens of V |
| Hall Effect | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H I B}{t}$ | $V_H = k_H \cdot I \cdot B$ | $k_H$ (V/A·T) | µV to mV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Constants Used:**
> - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electronic charge)
> - $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
> - $1\;\text{T} = 1\;\text{Wb/m}^2 = 1\;\text{V·s/m}^2$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Transducer | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric** | Microphones, ultrasonic transducers, accelerometers, gas lighters, inkjet printers |
| **Thermocouple** | Furnace temperature monitoring, exhaust gas sensing in automobiles, industrial process control |
| **Photovoltaic** | Solar panels, light meters, optical encoders, photodiodes in cameras |
| **Electromagnetic** | Tachogenerators in speedometers, dynamo in bicycles, AC generators, induction coils |
| **Hall Effect** | BLDC motor commutation, current sensing, keyboard switches, magnetic field measurement |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Piezoelectric Transducer Output Voltage

**Given:** A piezoelectric crystal of thickness $t$, area $A$, relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, and piezoelectric charge constant $d$.

**Find:** Expression for output voltage $V_{\text{out}}$ in terms of applied force $F$.

### Step 1: Force to Charge Relationship

The fundamental constitutive equation of the direct piezoelectric effect is:

$$
Q = d \cdot F
$$

Here, $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge constant** (units: C/N). This equation states that the generated charge $Q$ on the crystal's electrode surface is directly proportional to the applied mechanical force $F$.

### Step 2: Capacitance of the Crystal Element

The crystal itself acts as a parallel-plate capacitor with electrode area $A$ and separation $t$:

$$
C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}
$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the vacuum permittivity and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the piezoelectric material.

### Step 3: Voltage Across the Crystal

Using the capacitor relationship $V = Q / C$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{d \cdot t \cdot F}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}
$$

### Step 4: Define the Piezoelectric Voltage Constant

Define the **piezoelectric voltage constant** $g$ as:

$$
g = \frac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}
$$

with units of V·m/N. Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = g \cdot t \cdot \frac{F}{A} = g \cdot t \cdot P}
$$

where $P = F/A$ is the applied mechanical pressure (N/m² or Pa).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The output voltage depends on the crystal *thickness* $t$ and the *pressure* $P$. Thicker crystals produce higher voltage but lower capacitance — a fundamental design trade-off.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermocouple EMF (Seebeck Effect)

**Given:** Two dissimilar metals $A$ and $B$ joined at hot junction temperature $T_2$ and cold junction temperature $T_1$.

**Find:** The net EMF generated.

### Step 1: Define Seebeck Coefficients

Each material has a temperature-dependent Seebeck coefficient $S(T)$, representing the EMF generated per unit temperature difference. For metal $A$: $S_A(T)$; for metal $B$: $S_B(T)$.

### Step 2: EMF Contribution from Each Leg

The EMF generated in leg $A$ from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$
E_A = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_A(T) \, dT
$$

Similarly for leg $B$:

$$
E_B = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_B(T) \, dT
$$

### Step 3: Net Loop EMF

Since the two legs are connected in series opposition (their EMFs are subtracted around the loop):

$$
E_{\text{net}} = E_A - E_B = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT
$$

### Step 4: Define Combined Seebeck Coefficient

Let $S_{AB}(T) = S_A(T) - S_B(T)$ be the **differential Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair.

$$
E_{\text{net}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} S_{AB}(T) \, dT
$$

### Step 5: Linearization for Small $\Delta T$

If $S_{AB}$ is approximately constant over the operating range, then:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{net}} = S_{AB} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** Many students forget that thermocouples measure *temperature difference*, not absolute temperature. The **cold junction compensation (CJC)** is therefore mandatory for accurate absolute temperature readings.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electromagnetic Induction (Tachogenerator)

**Given:** A coil of $N$ turns, area $A$, rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ in a uniform magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the rotation axis.

**Find:** RMS output voltage.

### Step 1: Magnetic Flux Linkage

When the coil's normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the field direction, the flux through one turn is:

$$
\Phi = B A \cos\theta
$$

Total flux linkage for $N$ turns:

$$
\lambda = N \Phi = N B A \cos\theta
$$

### Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law

$$
e = -\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = -N B A \cdot \frac{d}{dt}(\cos\theta)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Angular Motion

For uniform rotation, $\theta = \omega t$, so $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$:

$$
e = -N B A \cdot (-\sin\theta) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t)
$$

### Step 4: Instantaneous EMF

$$
\boxed{e_{\text{inst}}(t) = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t)}
$$

The peak (maximum) EMF is:

$$
E_{\text{peak}} = N B A \omega
$$

### Step 5: RMS Value

For a sinusoidal signal:

$$
E_{\text{rms}} = \frac{E_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{N B A \omega}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 6: Express in Terms of Frequency

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$
E_{\text{rms}} = \frac{N B A \cdot 2\pi f}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \, \pi N B A f
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The output voltage is **linearly proportional to rotational speed** $f$ (Hz) or $\omega$ (rad/s). This makes the tachogenerator an ideal *analog speed sensor* in motor control systems.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hall Effect Transducer Sensitivity

**Given:** A thin semiconductor strip of thickness $t$, carrying current $I$ along its length, exposed to a transverse magnetic field $B$.

**Find:** The Hall voltage $V_H$.

### Step 1: Force on Charge Carriers

The charge carriers (electrons) experience a Lorentz force:

$$
F = q (\vec{v} \times \vec{B})
$$

This force pushes electrons toward one face of the strip.

### Step 2: Equilibrium Condition

Charge accumulation continues until the transverse electric field $E_H$ creates an opposing force that balances the magnetic force. At equilibrium:

$$
q E_H = q v B \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_H = v B
$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Current Density

Current density is $J = n q v$, so $v = J / (n q)$. Also $J = I / (w t)$, where $w$ is the strip width:

$$
E_H = \frac{I B}{n q w t}
$$

### Step 4: Hall Voltage

The Hall voltage is measured across the width $w$:

$$
V_H = E_H \cdot w = \frac{I B}{n q t}
$$

### Step 5: Define Hall Coefficient

Let $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n q}$ be the **Hall coefficient** (units: m³/C):

$$
\boxed{V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Hall voltage is *inversely proportional* to the charge carrier density $n$. Semiconductors (low $n$) produce much higher $V_H$ than metals — this is why Hall sensors are made from materials like indium antimonide (InSb) or gallium arsenide (GaAs).

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix (Analytical Summary)

| Parameter | Piezoelectric | Thermocouple | Photovoltaic | Electromagnetic | Hall Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input quantity | Force/Pressure | Temperature Δ | Light intensity | Rotational speed / motion | Magnetic field |
| Output quantity | Charge (Q) | EMF (E) | Current/Voltage (I, V) | EMF (e) | Voltage (V_H) |
| Output type | AC (dynamic) | DC | DC | AC or DC | DC |
| Frequency response | Excellent (kHz–MHz) | Slow (Hz range) | Fast (kHz) | Excellent (DC to MHz) | Excellent |
| Sensitivity to temperature | High | Self-referencing | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Self-generated? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Power requirement | None | None | None | None | Supply current I required |
| KTU exam weightage | High | High | Moderate | High | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
