# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define an active transducer. Give two examples and state the physical principle exploited by each.**

**Model Answer:**

An active transducer is a device that converts a non-electrical quantity into an electrical signal *without requiring any external excitation source*. It generates its own output voltage or current by exploiting fundamental physical effects in materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Examples:**
> 1. **Thermocouple** — exploits the **Seebeck Effect**, in which a temperature difference across the junction of two dissimilar metals produces a net EMF.
> 2. **Piezoelectric crystal** — exploits the **direct piezoelectric effect**, in which mechanical stress applied to certain crystals (e.g., quartz) produces surface electric charge.

*(Self-study keyword: "self-generating transducer" is a frequent board-expected synonym.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Differentiate between active and passive transducers with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generates its own electrical output | Requires external excitation source |
| 2 | Energy conversion device | Energy modulating device |
| 3 | Output: voltage, current, or charge | Output: change in R, L, or C |
| 4 | **Examples:** Thermocouple, Piezoelectric crystal, PV cell, Hall sensor | **Examples:** RTD, Strain gauge, LVDT, Capacitive sensor |
| 5 | Simpler signal chain | Requires excitation and bridge circuits |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with internal logical connection. Two alternative choices are provided below — answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **principle of operation of a piezoelectric transducer**. Derive the relationship between applied force and output voltage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric crystal has a charge sensitivity of $d = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N and a capacitance of $C_p = 500$ pF. If a force of $F = 0.8$ N is applied, calculate the output voltage.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **piezoelectric transducer** is an active transducer that converts mechanical force or pressure into a proportional electrical charge (or voltage). The principle is based on the **direct piezoelectric effect**, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880.

**Working Principle:**

1. The crystal is cut into a specific orientation (e.g., X-cut or Y-cut for quartz) and placed between two metallic electrodes.
2. When a mechanical force $F$ is applied along the sensitive axis, the crystal lattice deforms asymmetrically.
3. This deformation displaces positive and negative charge centers, producing a net **dipole moment** per unit volume.
4. The accumulated charge $Q$ appears on the electrode surfaces:

$$
Q = d \cdot F
$$

5. The voltage developed across the crystal is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Materials used:** Natural — Quartz, Rochelle salt; Synthetic — PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), PVDF polymer.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**
A rectangular block of piezoelectric material with electrodes on top and bottom, an arrow showing the applied force on the top face, and voltmeter leads connected to the electrodes.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given data:**
- $d = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $C_p = 500$ pF $= 500 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- $F = 0.8$ N

**Step 1: Calculate generated charge**

$$
Q = d \cdot F = (250 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.8
$$

$$
Q = 200 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{C} = 200 \;\text{pC}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate output voltage**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-12}}{500 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 0.4 \;\text{V}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = 0.4 \;\text{V}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is forgetting to convert pF to F or pC to C. Always express all quantities in **SI base units** before computing. (Loses ~1.5 marks if unit mismatch occurs.)

---

### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle of operation of a thermocouple** as an active transducer. Derive the expression for the thermoelectric EMF in terms of the Seebeck coefficient and temperature difference.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A K-type thermocouple has a Seebeck coefficient of $S_{AB} = 41\;\mu\text{V/°C}$. The hot junction is at $T_2 = 600°\text{C}$ and the cold junction is at $T_1 = 30°\text{C}$. Calculate the generated EMF. If the cold junction temperature rises by 10°C during operation, what is the new EMF? Comment on **cold junction compensation**.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **thermocouple** is formed by joining two dissimilar metals $A$ and $B$ at one end (hot junction) while the other ends are kept at a reference temperature (cold junction). The principle is the **Seebeck Effect** (1821).

**Working Principle:**

1. The hot junction absorbs thermal energy from the measurand.
2. Charge carriers (electrons) in metal $A$ and metal $B$ diffuse from hot to cold ends at *different rates* because each metal has a different Seebeck coefficient.
3. This differential diffusion creates a potential gradient along each leg.
4. Around the closed loop, the net EMF generated is:

$$
E_{\text{net}} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT
$$

5. For small temperature ranges where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are constants:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{net}} = S_{AB} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard thermocouple types (KTU syllabus list):** Type K (Chromel–Alumel), Type J (Iron–Constantan), Type T (Copper–Constantan), Type R/S (Platinum–Rhodium).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given data:**
- $S_{AB} = 41\;\mu\text{V/°C} = 41 \times 10^{-6}$ V/°C
- $T_2 = 600°\text{C}$
- $T_1 = 30°\text{C}$

**Step 1: Initial EMF calculation**

$$
\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 600 - 30 = 570\;\text{°C}
$$

$$
E_1 = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 570
$$

$$
E_1 = 23370 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{V} = 23.37\;\text{mV}
$$

**Step 2: New cold junction temperature**

$$
T_1' = 30 + 10 = 40°\text{C}
$$

$$
\Delta T' = 600 - 40 = 560°\text{C}
$$

$$
E_2 = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 560 = 22960 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{V} = 22.96\;\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3: Change in EMF**

$$
\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = 23.37 - 22.96 = 0.41\;\text{mV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) Discussion (3 marks weightage):**
> A thermocouple measures *temperature difference* between hot and cold junctions. If the cold junction temperature drifts (e.g., due to ambient heating at the terminal block), the reading becomes erroneous. **CJC techniques** include:
> - Hardware: Using an ice-bath reference ($T_1 = 0°\text{C}$).
> - Software: Measuring cold junction temperature with a separate sensor (e.g., silicon diode or RTD) and adding a compensation voltage in firmware.
> - Hardware ICs: Specialized CJC chips (e.g., AD594/AD595, MAX31855) integrated in modern instruments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In part (b), students often compute $E$ correctly but *omit the CJC discussion*, losing **2 to 3 marks**. The KTU 2024 rubric explicitly allocates marks for **practical application commentary**.
> - Do not write $S_{AB}$ in V/K without converting °C. Since temperature *differences* are involved, $\Delta T$ in °C equals $\Delta T$ in K numerically — but clearly state the assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Module 3 — Active Transducers Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Active transducers are **self-generating devices** that convert non-electrical energy into electrical energy *without external excitation*.

- **Five Principal Effects (PT-PH-FE mnemonic):**
  - **P**iezoelectric (mechanical → charge)
  - **T**hermoelectric/Seebeck (temperature difference → EMF)
  - **P**hotoelectric/Photovoltaic (light → voltage/current)
  - **F**araday Electromagnetic Induction (changing flux → EMF)
  - **H**all Effect (magnetic field → transverse voltage)

- **Key Formulae to Memorize:**
  - $Q = d F$ and $V = Q/C_p$ (piezoelectric)
  - $E = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ (thermocouple)
  - $I_{SC} = K \cdot L$ (photovoltaic)
  - $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ and $E_{\text{rms}} = \dfrac{NBA\omega}{\sqrt{2}}$ (electromagnetic)
  - $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ (Hall effect)

- **Active vs Passive Rule:** If a device *needs* a battery or excitation supply, it is **passive**. If it *generates* its own signal, it is **active**.

- **Engineering Trade-offs:**
  - **Piezoelectric:** Cannot measure static (DC) force — only dynamic changes.
  - **Thermocouple:** Low sensitivity (µV/°C) but extremely wide range and rugged.
  - **Photovoltaic:** Direct energy conversion; efficiency is limited by material bandgap.
  - **Electromagnetic:** Excellent for rotational speed; fails for stationary measurement.
  - **Hall Effect:** Requires a bias current but outputs a DC signal proportional to $B$.

- **Cold Junction Compensation (CJC):** Always mention CJC in thermocouple problems — it is a **repeated KTU examiner favourite**.

- **Unit Awareness:** Always use SI units in derivations; convert µV, pF, pC to base units before numerical substitution.

- **Exam Pattern Reminder:** Part A = 3 marks (definition + example); Part B = 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7, with internal choice between two fully independent questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Constructional Details" in Transducers?

In the context of **Transducers and Measurements (PCAET303)**, the term **"Constructional Details"** refers to the precise physical, mechanical, and material-level design architecture of a sensing element — i.e., *how* a transducer is actually *built*, *assembled*, and *packaged* to convert a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, force, displacement, etc.) into a measurable electrical signal.

Rather than focusing on the *mathematical model* or the *output equation*, this topic asks the student to identify:
- **What materials** are used for the active sensing element
- **How** the sensing wire/film/element is wound, deposited, or bonded
- **How** the element is protected (sheaths, insulation, encapsulation)
- **How** lead wires are taken out (cold-end compensation, lead-wire resistance)
- **What** the physical geometry looks like (probe-type, surface-mount, immersion-type)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> **Analogy — The Thermometer Family Reunion**
>
> Imagine three siblings who all tell you "how hot it is," but each is built very differently:
> - The **eldest (RTD)** is a tiny, pure platinum wire wound carefully on a ceramic/glass bobbin — a *precision watchmaker*.
> - The **middle child (Thermocouple)** is just two different metal wires *twisted* or *welded* at a junction — a *spontaneous spark*.
> - The **youngest (Thermistor)** is a sintered blob of metal-oxide semiconductor — a *chemistry-lab experiment compressed into a bead*.
>
> **Constructional Details** is the engineering biography of each sibling — what they are *made of*, *how* they are *shaped*, and *why* that specific construction gives them their unique personality (accuracy, range, speed, cost).

### 1.3 Why is this Topic Important for KTU Examinations?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Constructional Details is a *favourite* KTU 2024 question area. It is typically asked as:
> - **Part A (3 marks):** "With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an RTD / Thermocouple / Thermistor."
> - **Part B (14 marks):** "Discuss the construction, working principle, and characteristics of …" with internal choice between two transducers.

The construction directly dictates:
- **Accuracy class** of the instrument
- **Time constant** (thermal response)
- **Mechanical robustness**
- **Cost and commercial viability**

### 1.4 Scope of Module 3 — Constructional Details

The three primary transducers whose **constructional details** are mandated by the KTU 2024 PCAET303 syllabus are:

| Sl. No | Transducer | Primary Physical Quantity | Constructional Focus |
|:------:|:-----------|:--------------------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **RTD** (Resistance Temperature Detector) | Temperature | Platinum wire wound on a former |
| 2 | **Thermocouple** | Temperature | Two dissimilar metals joined at a hot junction |
| 3 | **Thermistor** (NTC / PTC) | Temperature | Sintered metal-oxide semiconductor bead |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Scales Used in Construction**
> - **IPTS-68 / ITS-90:** International Temperature Scale used to calibrate RTDs and thermocouples.
> - **DIN 43760 / IEC 60751:** Standards defining the nominal resistance (e.g., **100 Ω at 0 °C**) and tolerance classes for platinum RTDs.
> - **ISA / ANSI MC 96.1:** Colour-coding standard for thermocouple extension/compensating wires.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RTD — Constructional Details (Resistance Temperature Detector)

An **RTD** exploits the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal (most commonly **platinum**, sometimes nickel or copper) increases predictably with temperature.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Constructional Anatomy

- **Step 1 — Active Element Selection:** A high-purity platinum wire (typically **99.999 % pure**, with a nominal resistance of **100 Ω at 0 °C**, called the *PT100*) is chosen because of its near-linear, stable, and repeatable resistance-temperature relationship.
- **Step 2 — Wire Winding Geometry:** The platinum wire is wound in a **helical (spiral) fashion** on a *cross-shaped* or *cylindrical* ceramic former (made of alumina or steatite). The helical winding minimises stress and allows controlled expansion on heating.
- **Step 3 — Strain Relief:** The winding is coated with a thin layer of vitreous enamel/glass cement to *lock* the wire in place and prevent vibration-induced micro-strains that would alter resistance.
- **Step 4 — Bifilar Winding (in some designs):** To cancel the inductive effect of the helical coil, the wire is folded and wound back on itself (bifilar) — turning the RTD into a *purely resistive* element.
- **Step 5 — Encapsulation:** The wound element is slid into a **stainless-steel or Inconel sheath** filled with high-purity magnesium oxide (MgO) powder. The MgO provides:
  - Electrical insulation between wire and sheath
  - Excellent thermal coupling (high thermal conductivity)
  - Mechanical shock absorption
- **Step 6 — Lead Wire Termination:** Three- or four-wire lead configurations (Pt or nickel-clad copper) are welded to the element ends, sealed through a header, and brought out through a hermetic feedthrough.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Wire vs. Four-Wire RTD — Why the construction matters**
> The number of lead wires taken out from the construction determines the **lead-wire error compensation method**. A 2-wire RTD has the highest error; a 4-wire RTD (Kelvin connection) eliminates lead resistance completely.

### 2.1.2 Resistance–Temperature Relation (Theoretical Basis)

For an RTD, the resistance varies as a polynomial of temperature:

$$
R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \gamma (T - 100)\, T^3 \right)
$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (°C)
- $R_0$ = resistance at $0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typically **100 Ω** for PT100)
- $\alpha$ = linear temperature coefficient (for platinum, $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$)
- $\beta, \gamma$ = higher-order Callendar–Van Dusen coefficients

## 2.2 Thermocouple — Constructional Details

A **thermocouple** is the most widely used temperature sensor in industry. Its working principle is the **Seebeck Effect** — a continuous current flows in a circuit of two dissimilar metals when their junctions are held at different temperatures.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Constructional Anatomy

- **Step 1 — Thermoelement Selection:** Two wires of *dissimilar* metals/alloys (e.g., **Chromel–Alumel** for Type K, **Iron–Constantan** for Type J, **Platinum–Platinum/Rhodium** for Type R/S) are selected. Each wire is the **thermoelement** or **leg**.
- **Step 2 — Hot Junction Formation:** The two wires are joined at one end by one of three methods:
  - **Twisted & Welded** (most common — twisted then arc-welded or spot-welded)
  - **Butt-Welded** (parallel abutting ends fused)
  - **Brazed / Soldered** (using a brazing alloy)
  The junction is the *measuring* or *hot* junction.
- **Step 3 — Insulation of Individual Legs:** Each thermoelement leg is sleeved with a high-temperature insulating material:
  - **Fibreglass braid** (up to ~480 °C)
  - **Ceramic beads / tubes** of alumina (Al₂O₃) for higher temperatures
- **Step 4 — Sheath & Compaction:** The insulated pair is slid into a **metal sheath** (stainless steel, Inconel) and the annular space is filled with compacted **MgO powder** for vibration resistance and thermal coupling.
- **Step 5 — Cold End Termination:** The open ends (cold junction) terminate at a **terminal block** or **isothermal connecting head** where connection to extension/compensating wires is made.
- **Step 6 — Sealing & Housing:** A terminal head with weather-proof gland, or an *explosion-proof head* in hazardous areas, seals the assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Junction Geometries — Three Variants**
> - **Exposed (Bare) Junction:** Fastest response, but vulnerable to corrosion.
> - **Grounded Junction:** Welded to sheath tip → good thermal contact, faster than insulated, but electrical isolation is lost.
> - **Ungrounded (Insulated) Junction:** Welding is wrapped with MgO — *slowest* but electrically isolated, most common in industry.

## 2.3 Thermistor — Constructional Details

A **thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor device whose resistance changes *non-linearly* and *strongly* with temperature.

### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Constructional Anatomy

- **Step 1 — Material Synthesis:** Mixtures of **metal oxides** (manganese, nickel, cobalt, iron, copper oxides) are ground into fine powders, mixed with binders, and pressed into the desired shape.
- **Step 2 — Sintering:** The pressed oxide mixture is heated in a furnace to **1200–1400 °C** in a controlled atmosphere, fusing the grains into a *polycrystalline ceramic body*. This sintering step is the heart of thermistor construction.
- **Step 3 — Lead Attachment:** Platinum or *Dumet* lead wires are attached to the sintered body using:
  - **Alloyed (fired-on) silver contacts** baked at ~800 °C, OR
  - **Conductive epoxy / solder**
- **Step 4 — Encapsulation:** The thermistor is sealed inside a glass probe (for fast response) or coated with epoxy (for cost-sensitive applications), or mounted in a metal housing (for industrial use).
- **Steinhart–Hart Relation:**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^3
$$

where $A, B, C$ are device-specific calibration constants.

> [!NOTE]
> **NTC vs PTC — Constructional Implications**
> - **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* with temperature — used for inrush current limiting, temperature monitoring.
> - **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* sharply above a *Curie* or *switching* temperature — used as self-resetting fuses, motor protection.
> The difference is purely a *material* and *doping* level choice during construction.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicable to |
|:-:|:---------------------|:--------|:------|:--------------|
| 1 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \cdots)$ | $\Omega$ | RTD |
| 2 | Temperature from RTD | $t = \dfrac{R_T - R_0}{\alpha \, R_0}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Linear approx. |
| 3 | Thermocouple Seebeck EMF | $E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$ | Volts | Thermocouple |
| 4 | Linearised Thermocouple | $E \approx S\,(T_h - T_c)$ | Volts | Small $\Delta T$ |
| 5 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\,B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | $\Omega$ | NTC Thermistor |
| 6 | Steinhart–Hart | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | $\text{K}^{-1}$ | Thermistor (high accuracy) |
| 7 | Sensitivity (RTD) | $S = \dfrac{dR}{dT} = \alpha R_0$ | $\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | RTD |
| 8 | Seebeck Coefficient | $S_{AB} = \dfrac{dE}{dT}$ | $\mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Thermocouple |
| 9 | Time constant (1st order) | $\tau = \dfrac{m \, c}{h A}$ | s | All temperature sensors |
| 10 | Lead-wire error (2-wire) | $\Delta R = 2 \, R_{lead}$ | $\Omega$ | 2-wire RTD |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** Thermistor equations use **Kelvin (K)** for $T$, not Celsius. Convert carefully: $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RTDs:** Used in *laboratory-grade* precision thermometry, pharmaceutical industry, and calibration baths (PT100 with Class A or 1/3 DIN accuracy).
- **Thermocouples:** Dominate in *industrial furnaces*, gas turbines, and rocket engine test stands (Type R/S/B for ultra-high temperatures up to 1800 °C).
- **Thermistors:** Embedded in *consumer electronics* (mobile battery packs), automotive coolant sensors, and medical equipment (3-wire YSI-400 series).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — RTD Resistance as a Function of Temperature

Starting from the fundamental postulate of metallic conduction (free-electron density and lattice vibration model), the resistance of a pure metal is given by:

$$
R = \dfrac{\rho \, L}{A}
$$

where $\rho$ is the *temperature-dependent* resistivity. For a pure platinum wire:

$$
\rho(T) = \rho_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right]
$$

The Callendar–Van Dusen equation in expanded form for $T \ge 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ becomes:

$$
R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A T + B T^2 \right)
$$

with standard IEC 60751 values:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\,^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,^{\circ}\text{C}^{-2}$

### Numerical Worked Example (PT100 at 100 °C)

$$
R_{100} = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(100) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(100)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the linear term:**

$$
(3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \times 100 = 0.39083
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the quadratic term:**

$$
(-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 10000 = -5.775 \times 10^{-3} = -0.005775
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the bracket:**

$$
1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775 = 1.385055
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $R_0$:**

$$
R_{100} = 100 \times 1.385055 = 138.5055\;\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{100} \approx 138.51\;\Omega}
$$

This matches the standard **PT100** value at 100 °C within **±0.001 Ω**, confirming the validity of the construction.

## 3.2 Derivation — NTC Thermistor B-Parameter Equation

The conduction in a thermistor is governed by **semiconductor physics**. The number of charge carriers $n$ follows:

$$
n \propto T^{3/2} \, e^{-E_g / 2 k_B T}
$$

Resistivity $\rho \propto 1/n$, hence:

$$
\rho(T) = \rho_\infty \, e^{E_g / 2 k_B T}
$$

Defining the **B-parameter** as $B = E_g / 2k_B$ (in Kelvin), and normalising with respect to a reference $R_0$ at $T_0$:

$$
\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{e^{B/T}}{e^{B/T_0}} = e^{B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[ B \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]}
$$

### Numerical Example (NTC, $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $B = 3950\,\text{K}$)

Find $R$ at $T = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 373.15\,\text{K}$ and $T_0 = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $1/T$ and $1/T_0$:**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{373.15} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Difference:**

$$
\Delta = (2.680 - 3.354) \times 10^{-3} = -0.674 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $B$:**

$$
B \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-0.674 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.662
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate:**

$$
e^{-2.662} = 0.0699
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $R_0$:**

$$
R_{100} = 10000 \times 0.0699 \approx 699\;\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{100\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \approx 699\;\Omega}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The resistance dropped from 10 kΩ at 25 °C to ~700 Ω at 100 °C — a 14× decrease. This *enormous sensitivity* (much higher than an RTD) is why thermistors are favoured for narrow-range, high-resolution applications.

## 3.3 Derivation — Thermocouple Seebeck Voltage (Simplified)

The Seebeck EMF generated in a thermocouple with hot-junction $T_h$ and cold-junction $T_c$ is the integral of the *relative Seebeck coefficient* $S_{AB}(T)$ of the two metals:

$$
E_{AB} = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

Assuming $S_{AB}$ is approximately constant over the operating range, this collapses to the linearised form:

$$
E_{AB} \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_h - T_c)
$$

For a **Type K** thermocouple, $S_{AB} \approx 41\;\mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ at room temperature. Hence, for $T_h - T_c = 1000\,^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
E \approx (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1000 = 0.041\;\text{V} = 41\;\text{mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{Type K},\,1000^{\circ}\text{C}} \approx 41\;\text{mV}}
$$

In practice, $S_{AB}$ is *not* constant — KTU students should use the **NIST polynomial tables** (e.g., Type K polynomial coefficients valid from −200 °C to 0 °C and 0 °C to 1372 °C) for accurate voltage computation.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | RTD (PT100) | Thermocouple (Type K) | Thermistor (NTC) |
|:----------|:------------|:----------------------|:-----------------|
| Active Material | Pure Pt wire | Chromel + Alumel | Mn–Ni–Co oxides |
| Sensitivity | **0.385 Ω/°C** (linear) | **~41 µV/°C** (mildly non-linear) | **−3 to −5 %/°C** (highly non-linear) |
| Range | −200 to **+850 °C** | −200 to **+1370 °C** | −55 to **+150 °C** |
| Accuracy | **±0.1 °C** (Class A) | **±2.2 °C** (Standard limits) | **±0.1 °C** (narrow range) |
| Stability | Excellent | Moderate (drift) | Poor (over years) |
| Cost | High | Low | Very Low |
| Self-heating | Low (use low excitation) | Negligible | Critical concern |
| Lead-wire issue | Significant (use 3/4-wire) | Cold-junction comp. needed | Less critical |
| Construction complexity | High (precise winding) | Moderate (welding) | Low (sintered bead) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: RTD Construction  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise and KTU-scoring):**

An **RTD** is a temperature sensor whose resistance changes predictably with temperature.

**Construction (Sketch in words):**
1. A high-purity **platinum wire (PT100, 100 Ω at 0 °C)** is wound in a helical bifilar form on a **ceramic (alumina) cross-shaped former** to minimise inductance.
2. The winding is **coated with vitreous enamel** for strain relief.
3. The element is inserted into a **stainless-steel protective sheath**, and the annular gap is **packed with MgO powder** for insulation and thermal coupling.
4. **Three or four lead wires** (Pt or nickel) are brought out through a hermetic seal for connection to the measuring bridge.

**Working principle:** As temperature rises, lattice vibration of platinum increases, scattering more conduction electrons → resistance rises linearly.

**Key features:** High accuracy, excellent stability, range −200 to +850 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid**
> - Do **not** confuse RTD with a *thermistor* — RTD uses *pure metal* (Pt), thermistor uses *metal-oxide semiconductor*.
> - Always mention **PT100 and 100 Ω at 0 °C** in the answer — examiners award marks specifically for this numerical.

---

### Question 2: Thermocouple Construction  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. Explain with a diagram the constructional features of a thermocouple. List any two thermocouple types with their temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thermocouple** consists of two dissimilar metal wires joined at one end (hot junction).

**Construction:**
1. Two thermoelements (e.g., **Chromel** and **Alumel** for Type K) are joined at the *hot junction* by **arc/spot welding**.
2. Each leg is **insulated** with fibreglass braid or ceramic beads.
3. The insulated pair is enclosed in a **metal sheath** (stainless steel/Inconel) and **compacted with MgO powder**.
4. The cold-end is terminated in a **connecting head** with provisions for **cold-junction compensation**.

**Principle:** *Seebeck Effect* — a small EMF (microvolts to millivolts) is generated proportional to the temperature difference between hot and cold junctions.

**Two common types:**
| Type | Materials | Range |
|:-----|:----------|:------|
| **K** | Chromel–Alumel | 0 to 1260 °C |
| **R** | Pt–Pt/13 % Rh | 0 to 1480 °C |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Detailed RTD + Thermocouple Comparison  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working principle of an RTD**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **constructional details of a thermocouple** with a diagram. Discuss the **laws of thermocouples** used for cold-junction compensation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction of RTD (3 marks):**
- Pure platinum wire (99.999 % purity) is wound on a cross-shaped ceramic former in a bifilar helical pattern.
- Vitreous enamel coating locks the wire.
- Encapsulated in a stainless-steel sheath with compacted MgO powder insulation.
- Three or four lead wires are brought out for bridge connection.
- Standard: PT100 with $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$ at 0 °C.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Resistance of platinum increases linearly with temperature:

$$
R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)
$$

- For PT100, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, so a 1 °C rise gives a 0.385 Ω change.
- The resistance is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** or **4-wire Kelvin ohmmeter**.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High accuracy and repeatability (±0.1 °C)
- Excellent long-term stability
- Wide range (−200 to +850 °C)

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Low sensitivity (0.385 Ω/°C)
- Affected by lead-wire resistance
- Self-heating due to excitation current

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Construction of Thermocouple (3 marks):**
- Two dissimilar thermoelements (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for Type K) are **welded** at the hot junction.
- Each leg is insulated with high-temperature ceramic or fibreglass.
- Sheath with MgO packing provides mechanical and chemical protection.
- Cold junction terminates at a head with **CJC circuit** or **ice-bath reference**.

**Working (1 mark):**
- Seebeck Effect produces EMF:

$$
E = S_{AB}(T_h - T_c)
$$

**Laws of Thermocouples (3 marks):**
1. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Introducing a third metal in the circuit does not affect the EMF, provided its two junctions are at the same temperature. *This is why lead wires and connections can be added freely.*
2. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF generated with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs at $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_3)$. *This is the basis of cold-junction compensation using software reference.*
3. **Law of Additive/Substitute Materials:** When needed, a thermoelement may be replaced by a combination of others whose net Seebeck coefficient equals the original. *This is used in *extension* and *compensating* wires.*

---

### Question B — Detailed Thermistor + Constructional Comparison  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **construction of a thermistor** with a diagram. Differentiate between **NTC and PTC** types. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare the **constructional features, sensitivity, and applications of RTD, Thermocouple, and Thermistor** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction of Thermistor (4 marks):**
1. **Material preparation:** Metal oxides of Mn, Ni, Co, Fe, Cu are weighed, ground, and mixed with a ceramic binder.
2. **Forming:** The mixture is pressed into **bead, disc, rod, or chip** form using a die.
3. **Sintering:** The pressed body is fired at **1200–1400 °C** in a controlled atmosphere, fusing grains into a polycrystalline ceramic.
4. **Contacts:** Platinum or Dumet lead wires are attached using **fired silver paste** (sintered at ~800 °C).
5. **Encapsulation:** Bead type is sealed in **glass** for fast response; chip types are epoxy-coated or housed in metal probes.

**Working principle (1 mark):**
- Resistance follows:

$$
R_T = R_0 \, e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}
$$

- NTC: resistance **decreases** as T increases (more charge carriers thermally activated across band gap).
- PTC: resistance **increases** sharply near the *Curie temperature* (phase change in Barium Titanate ceramic).

**NTC vs PTC (2 marks):**
| Feature | NTC | PTC |
|:--------|:----|:----|
| Temperature coefficient | Negative | Positive |
| Material | Mn–Ni–Co oxides | BaTiO₃ doped with La/Sb |
| Use | Temperature sensing, inrush limit | Self-resetting fuse, motor protection |
| Response | Smooth exponential | Sharp transition at Curie point |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Parameter | RTD | Thermocouple | Thermistor |
|:----------|:----|:-------------|:-----------|
| **Active Element** | Pure Pt wire | Two dissimilar metals | Metal-oxide ceramic |
| **Construction** | Helical winding on ceramic former | Welded junction + sheath | Sintered bead/disc |
| **Output** | Resistance change | EMF (mV) | Resistance change (large) |
| **Sensitivity** | 0.385 Ω/°C | 41 µV/°C (Type K) | −3 to −5 %/°C |
| **Range** | −200 to 850 °C | −200 to 1370 °C | −55 to 150 °C |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 °C | ±2.2 °C | ±0.1 to ±0.5 °C |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Typical Applications** | Lab thermometry, calibration | Furnaces, turbines | Battery packs, HVAC |

(1 mark for proper tabular structure, 1 mark for units, 5 marks for content — examiners reward correctness of numerical values.)

> 1. **Skipping the diagram** in Part B costs **at least 2 marks** — always draw a *labelled* diagram.
> 2. **Mentioning numerical constants** (PT100, 100 Ω at 0 °C, $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}$, Type K → 41 µV/°C, B = 3950 K) shows exam-readiness and earns bonus marks.
> 3. **Vague descriptions** like "metal is used" are penalised — use precise terms: *99.999 % pure platinum, Chromel–Alumel, Manganese–Nickel oxide, MgO compaction, etc.*
> 4. **Mixing up the working principles** (e.g., writing "thermocouple works on resistance change") is the *single biggest* mark-loss reported in KTU valuation reports.
> 5. **Not stating the standard** (IEC 60751, ITS-90, ANSI MC 96.1) at least once is a missed opportunity — examiners explicitly allocate marks for "standard reference."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **RTD** → **P**ure **P**latinum **P**T100, helical winding on ceramic, MgO-packed sheath, **0.385 Ω/°C** sensitivity, **$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$**.
- [ ] **Thermocouple** → Two dissimilar metals, **Seebeck effect**, three junction types: **exposed / grounded / ungrounded**, needs **cold-junction compensation**, **$E = S_{AB}(T_h - T_c)$**.
- [ ] **Thermistor** → **Sintered metal-oxide ceramic**, **NTC** (resistance drops) and **PTC** (resistance jumps at Curie T), **$R_T = R_0 e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$**, range −55 to +150 °C.
- [ ] **Bifilar winding** in RTD → eliminates inductance.
- [ ] **Three laws of thermocouples** — Law of Intermediate Metals, Intermediate Temperatures, Additive/Substitute Materials.
- [ ] **4-wire RTD** (Kelvin connection) → best accuracy; **2-wire** → worst.
- [ ] **ITS-90** scale, **IEC 60751** for PT100, **ANSI MC 96.1** colour code for thermocouple wires.
- [ ] **Self-heating error** in thermistor: keep excitation current **< 1 mA** for small bead types.
- [ ] **Sensitivity ranking** (highest to lowest): **Thermistor ≫ RTD > Thermocouple** (in output change per °C).
- [ ] **Range ranking** (widest to narrowest): **Thermocouple > RTD > Thermistor**.
- [ ] **Cost ranking** (cheapest to costliest): **Thermistor < Thermocouple < RTD**.
- [ ] Always **label the diagram** (parts, materials, junction names) — KTU examiners allot specific marks for each label.
- [ ] Convert to **Kelvin** for thermistor equations; °C is fine for RTD/thermocouple.
- [ ] **Time constant** $\tau = mc/hA$ — minimised by small mass, high thermal conductivity, large surface area.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Transducers and Measurements**, the term **"Characteristics"** refers to the quantitative and qualitative properties that describe the **input-output behavior** of a measuring instrument or transducer under specified operating conditions. These characteristics are systematically classified into two fundamental categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Static Characteristics** are the set of performance attributes exhibited by a transducer when the measurand (input) is either constant or varies very slowly with respect to time, such that all transient (dynamic) effects have died out and the output has reached a **steady-state** condition.
>
> **Dynamic Characteristics** are the set of performance attributes exhibited by a transducer when the measurand is changing rapidly with time, necessitating the analysis of the **transient response**, **frequency response**, and **time-domain behavior** of the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Thermometer Story**
> Imagine you place a **mercury thermometer** in a cup of hot tea. The mercury does not jump instantly to the final temperature — it climbs slowly, overshoots a little, wobbles, and then settles. This **lag, overshoot, and final settling** is the *dynamic characteristic*.
>
> Now, suppose the tea temperature is held perfectly constant. The thermometer reading depends on how **close** it is to the true value (accuracy), how **close repeated readings** are to each other (precision), and how small a temperature change it can detect (resolution). These are *static characteristics*.
>
> In essence: **Static = How truthfully does the instrument speak at rest? Dynamic = How fluently does it speak in motion?**

## 1.3 Why These Characteristics Matter in KTU

Every KTU numerical problem and theoretical question on transducers ultimately reduces to evaluating one of these characteristics. The **accuracy** of a weighing scale in a shop, the **response time** of a thermocouple in a furnace, and the **drift** of a pressure sensor in an aircraft are all governed by these mathematical properties. The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and **International Organization of Standardization (ISO)** define standardized test conditions (e.g., **Reference Temperature of 20°C**, **Relative Humidity of 65%**, **Atmospheric Pressure of 101.325 kPa**) under which these characteristics are measured and reported.

## 1.4 The Two Broad Classes — At a Glance

| Aspect | Static Characteristics | Dynamic Characteristics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature of Input** | Constant or quasi-static | Rapidly time-varying |
| **System Behavior** | Steady-state equilibrium | Transient + Steady-state |
| **Mathematical Tools** | Algebra, polynomials, error curves | Differential equations, Laplace transforms, Bode plots |
| **Typical Parameters** | Accuracy, Linearity, Hysteresis, Drift | Time constant, Rise time, Damping ratio, Bandwidth |
| **Primary KTU Question Types** | Definitions, error analysis, % calculations | Step response, frequency response, 2nd-order analysis |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics — Operational Logic

Static characteristics define the **fidelity** of a measurement system when there is no time-dependence. Below is the structured logic for each parameter as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **What it is:** The closeness of a measured value ($\theta_m$) to the true value ($\theta_t$).
- **Why it matters:** A high-accuracy instrument minimizes systematic error.
- **KTU Formula:**
$$\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left| \frac{\theta_m - \theta_t}{\theta_t} \right|$$
- **In words:** Accuracy is often expressed as a **percentage of full-scale (%FS)** or **percentage of reading (%RD)**.

### 2.1.2 Precision
- **What it is:** The closeness of two or more measurements to each other under identical conditions (also called **repeatability** in some texts, though KTU treats them distinctly).
- **Key Insight:** An instrument can be **precise but not accurate** (e.g., a miscalibrated watch showing consistent wrong time).

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity
- **Definition:** The ratio of change in output to the corresponding change in input.
$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx}$$
- **Unit:** Output unit / Input unit (e.g., mV/°C for a thermocouple).
- **Sensitively important:** Higher sensitivity does **not** imply higher accuracy.

### 2.1.4 Linearity
- The degree to which the actual calibration curve conforms to a **straight line**.
- **Linearity Error (%)**:
$$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{(\theta_m)_{\text{max deviation from ideal line}}}{(\theta_{FS})} \times 100\%$$
- **Types:** Theoretical (zero-based) line and Best-Fit Straight Line (BFSL).

### 2.1.5 Hysteresis
- The maximum difference in output for the same input, depending on whether the input is **increasing** or **decreasing**.
$$\text{Hysteresis Error} = \frac{\theta_{\text{up}} - \theta_{\text{down}}}{(\theta_{FS})} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.6 Dead Zone / Dead Band
- The smallest range of input for which the output **does not change** (the instrument is unresponsive).

### 2.1.7 Resolution / Discrimination
- The **smallest measurable change** in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- Modern digital systems express this in **bits**: $\text{Resolution} = \frac{FS}{2^n - 1}$.

### 2.1.8 Drift
- The change in output over time for a constant input. Sub-classified as:
  * **Zero Drift:** Change at zero input.
  * **Span Drift / Sensitivity Drift:** Change in the slope of the calibration curve.
  * **Thermal Drift:** Caused by ambient temperature changes.

### 2.1.9 Span and Range
- **Range:** The difference between the maximum and minimum input values the instrument can measure.
- **Span:** The algebraic difference between the upper and lower range values. For a 0–100°C sensor, both range and span are 100°C, but for a 20–100°C sensor, range = 80°C.

### 2.1.10 Threshold
- The smallest input that produces a measurable output (slightly above the dead zone).

### 2.1.11 Reproducibility
- The closeness of measurements when conditions (operator, environment, instrument) are **varied** over a long period — broader than repeatability.

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Operational Logic

When the input $x(t)$ changes rapidly, the system is described by an **n-th order linear differential equation** of the form:

$$a_n \frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \dots + a_0 y = b_m \frac{d^m x}{dt^m} + \dots + b_0 x$$

For most practical transducers, a **first-order** or **second-order** approximation suffices.

### 2.2.1 First-Order System
- **Standard form:**
$$a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_0 x(t)$$
$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot x(t)$$
where $\tau = a_1 / a_0$ is the **time constant** and $K = b_0 / a_0$ is the static sensitivity.
- **Step Response:**
$$y(t) = K \cdot A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$
- **Key Time Markers:**
  * At $t = \tau$: Output reaches **63.2%** of final value.
  * At $t = 4\tau$: Output reaches **98.2%** (practical settling).
  * At $t = 5\tau$: Output reaches **99.3%** (theoretical settling).

### 2.2.2 Second-Order System
- **Standard form:**
$$\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \frac{dy}{dt} + \omega_n^2 y = K \omega_n^2 x(t)$$
where $\omega_n$ is the **undamped natural frequency** and $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**.
- **Step Response by Damping Case:**
  * $\zeta = 0$ → Sustained oscillations (undamped).
  * $0 < \zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillatory decay).
  * $\zeta = 1$ → **Critically damped** (fastest non-oscillatory response).
  * $\zeta > 1$ → **Overdamped** (sluggish, exponential).

### 2.2.3 Underdamped Step Response Metrics
For $\zeta < 1$:
- **Rise Time:** $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$
- **Peak Time:** $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
- **Maximum Overshoot:** $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
- **Percentage Overshoot:** $\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
- **Settling Time (2% band):** $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$
- **Settling Time (5% band):** $t_s \approx \dfrac{3}{\zeta \omega_n}$

### 2.2.4 Frequency Response Parameters
- **Bandwidth:** The range of frequencies over which the system response is within **−3 dB** of the mid-band value.
- **Resonant Frequency:** $\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (exists only for $0 \le \zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$).
- **Magnification factor** at resonance:
$$M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD** — The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Key Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Accuracy Error | $E_a = \dfrac{\theta_t - \theta_m}{\theta_t} \times 100$ | $\theta_t$ = true, $\theta_m$ = measured | % |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | Output change / Input change | (Output unit)/(Input unit) |
| 3 | Linearity Error | $E_L = \dfrac{d_{\max}}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100$ | $d_{\max}$ = max deviation from best-fit line | %FS |
| 4 | Hysteresis | $E_H = \dfrac{\theta_{\uparrow} - \theta_{\downarrow}}{\text{FS}} \times 100$ | Up vs. down reading difference | %FS |
| 5 | Dead Zone | $x_{\text{dead,upper}} - x_{\text{dead,lower}}$ | Range of unresponsive input | Input unit |
| 6 | Resolution (ADC) | $R = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = number of bits | Volt / LSB |
| 7 | First-Order Output | $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $A$ = step magnitude | Output unit |
| 8 | Time at 63.2% | $t = \tau$ | Time constant itself | seconds |
| 9 | % Overshoot | $\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | $\zeta$ = damping ratio | % |
| 10 | Peak Time | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | $\omega_n$ = natural frequency | seconds |
| 11 | Settling Time (2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% tolerance band | seconds |
| 12 | Rise Time | $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | 10% to 90% rise | seconds |
| 13 | Resonant Frequency | $\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ | Peak of frequency response | rad/s |
| 14 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = mass | rad/s |
| 15 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | $c$ = damping coefficient | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Flight-control accelerometers must have a **bandwidth > 50 Hz** and minimal hysteresis to avoid pilot-induced oscillations.
- **Biomedical:** ECG electrodes require a **flat frequency response from 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** to capture both baseline wander and QRS complexes.
- **Process Industry:** A pressure transmitter in a chemical reactor needs **low drift** (≤ 0.1% FS/year) for closed-loop control stability.
- **Automotive:** Knock sensors in engines are **piezoelectric** devices whose high-frequency resonance (> 20 kHz) is exploited via the second-order dynamic model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Step Response

### 3.1.1 Problem Context
A temperature transducer used in a chemical bath is modeled as a **first-order system** with a time constant of $\tau = 4$ s and static sensitivity $K = 0.5 \text{ mV/°C}$. The bath temperature is suddenly increased by $\Delta T = 20$°C (a step input). Derive the output response $y(t)$ and find the output at $t = 8$ s.

### 3.1.2 Governing Equation
The first-order transducer obeys:
$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the input function.**
A step input of magnitude $A = 20$°C at $t = 0$ is mathematically:
$$x(t) = A \cdot u(t) = 20 \cdot u(t) \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Form the forcing equation.**
Substituting $x(t) = 20$ into the governing equation:
$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot 20 = 0.5 \times 20 = 10 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Apply initial condition.**
At $t = 0$, the transducer output is zero (it has not yet sensed the change):
$$y(0) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear first-order ODE.**
This is a standard ODE. The **complementary (homogeneous) solution** is:
$$y_c(t) = C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$
The **particular (steady-state) solution** is the constant:
$$y_p(t) = 10 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Combine and apply initial condition.**
The total solution is:
$$y(t) = y_p + y_c = 10 + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$
At $t = 0$: $0 = 10 + C \Rightarrow C = -10$. Therefore:
$$y(t) = 10 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**
With $\tau = 4$ s:
$$y(t) = 10\left(1 - e^{-t/4}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at $t = 8$ s.**
$$y(8) = 10\left(1 - e^{-8/4}\right) = 10\left(1 - e^{-2}\right)$$
$$y(8) = 10\left(1 - 0.1353\right) = 10 \times 0.8647 = 8.647 \text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** At $t = 8$ s, which equals $2\tau$, the output is at $1 - e^{-2} = 86.47\%$ of the final value. This matches the universal **first-order step response table** (at $1\tau$ → 63.2%, at $2\tau$ → 86.5%, at $3\tau$ → 95.0%, at $4\tau$ → 98.2%, at $5\tau$ → 99.3%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Percentage Overshoot from Damping Ratio

### 3.2.1 Problem Context
A **second-order pressure transducer** used in a rocket engine test stand has a damping ratio of $\zeta = 0.4$ and a natural frequency of $\omega_n = 200$ rad/s. Determine (a) the percentage overshoot, (b) the peak time, and (c) the settling time (2% criterion) for a unit step pressure input.

### 3.2.2 Governing Equation
The general second-order model:
$$\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n \frac{dy}{dt} + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2 x(t)$$

For $\zeta < 1$ (underdamped), the solution takes the form:
$$y(t) = K \left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]$$
where the **damped natural frequency** is:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Solution for (a) Percentage Overshoot

**Step 1 — Locate the first peak.**
The first peak occurs when the sine argument equals $\pi/2$, i.e., at:
$$t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$t_p = \frac{\pi}{200 \times \sqrt{1-0.16}} = \frac{\pi}{200 \times 0.9165} = \frac{3.1416}{183.30} = 0.01714 \text{ s} = 17.14 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the damping envelope at the peak.**
The output at the first peak equals:
$$y(t_p) = K\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right]$$
(The minus minus from sine/cosine yields a positive exponential at first peak.)

**Step 4 — Form the % overshoot formula.**
$$\%M_p = 100 \times e^{-\zeta\pi / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\zeta = 0.4$.**
$$\frac{\zeta\pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{0.4 \times 3.1416}{\sqrt{0.84}} = \frac{1.2566}{0.9165} = 1.371$$
$$\%M_p = 100 \times e^{-1.371} = 100 \times 0.2540 = 25.40\%$$

### 3.2.4 Step-by-Step Solution for (c) Settling Time

**Step 1 — Recall the 2% settling time formula.**
$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**
$$t_s = \frac{4}{0.4 \times 200} = \frac{4}{80} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**
After 50 ms, the output stays within 2% of the final steady-state value. For a rocket engine test, this is **fast enough** to capture pressure transients during combustion start-up.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity of a Stacked Piezoelectric Transducer

### 3.3.1 Problem Context
A piezoelectric force sensor uses **$n$ stacked ceramic discs**, each of thickness $t$ and electrode area $A$, connected in series electrically. The piezoelectric charge constant is $d_{33}$ in Coulombs per Newton. Derive the sensitivity expression.

### 3.3.2 Governing Equations

The fundamental piezoelectric constitutive relation is:
$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$
where $Q$ is the generated charge (Coulombs) and $F$ is the applied force (Newtons).

**Step 1 — Each disc sensitivity.**
For a single disc, the charge generated is $Q_1 = d_{33} F$.

**Step 2 — Series connection of $n$ discs.**
In series, the **same charge** $Q$ appears across each layer, but the voltage adds. Sensitivity in charge mode remains:
$$Q_{\text{total}} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot F$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity definition.**
$$K_Q = \frac{dQ}{dF} = n \cdot d_{33} \quad \left[\frac{\text{C}}{\text{N}}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Voltage sensitivity.**
Since $V = Q/C$ and the capacitance of $n$ series layers is $C/n$ (for equal layers):
$$K_V = \frac{V}{F} = \frac{n d_{33}}{C/n} = \frac{n^2 d_{33}}{C}$$
This is why **stacking multiplies voltage sensitivity by $n^2$** — a critical design insight for KTU instrumentation questions.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Static Error Analysis (Composite Error)

A **pressure gauge** is used to measure a true pressure of 50 bar. The gauge reads 50.6 bar at 25°C. The manufacturer's spec sheet states:
- Linearity error: ±0.3% of reading
- Hysteresis error: ±0.2% of reading
- Thermal drift: ±0.05% of reading per °C (calibrated at 20°C)
- Repeatability: ±0.1% of reading

**Step 1 — Compute each error contribution at the reading of 50.6 bar.**

$$E_{\text{lin}} = 0.3\% \times 50.6 = 0.1518 \text{ bar}$$
$$E_{\text{hys}} = 0.2\% \times 50.6 = 0.1012 \text{ bar}$$
$$E_{\text{rep}} = 0.1\% \times 50.6 = 0.0506 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2 — Compute thermal drift at 25°C (5°C above calibration).**
$$E_{\text{thermal}} = 0.05\% \times 5 \times 50.6 = 0.1265 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case RSS (Root Sum of Squares) error.**
$$E_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{E_{\text{lin}}^2 + E_{\text{hys}}^2 + E_{\text{rep}}^2 + E_{\text{thermal}}^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{0.0230 + 0.0102 + 0.0026 + 0.0160} = \sqrt{0.0518} = 0.2276 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case arithmetic sum (conservative bound).**
$$E_{\text{max}} = 0.1518 + 0.1012 + 0.0506 + 0.1265 = 0.4301 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage of full scale (assume FS = 100 bar).**
$$\text{RSS \%} = \frac{0.2276}{100} \times 100 = 0.228\% \text{ FS}$$
$$\text{Worst-case \%} = \frac{0.4301}{100} \times 100 = 0.430\% \text{ FS}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Transducer Characteristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transducer Characteristics] --> B[Static Characteristics]
    A --> C[Dynamic Characteristics]
    A --> D[Environmental and Operational Characteristics]
    B --> B1[Accuracy and Precision]
    B --> B2[Sensitivity and Linearity]
    B --> B3[Hysteresis and Dead Zone]
    B --> B4[Resolution and Threshold]
    B --> B5[Drift, Span, Range]
    C --> C1[First Order Systems]
    C --> C2[Second Order Systems]
    C --> C3[Frequency Response and Bandwidth]
    C1 --> C1A[Time Constant Tau]
    C1 --> C1B[Rise Time and Settling Time]
    C2 --> C2A[Natural Frequency Wn]
    C2 --> C2B[Damping Ratio Zeta]
    C2 --> C2C[Overshoot and Peak Time]
    D --> D1[Temperature Effects]
    D --> D2[Vibration and Shock Effects]
    D --> D3[Humidity and Pressure Effects]
    B1 --> note1[Steady State Behavior]
    C1 --> note2[Time Domain Behavior]
    C3 --> note3[Frequency Domain Behavior]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — First-Order Transducer Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[Input x of t] --> SumJ[Summer Junction]
    SumJ --> Int[Integrator 1 divided by Tau s]
    Int --> Gain1[Gain K Static Sensitivity]
    Gain1 --> Output[Output y of t]
    Output --> FB[Feedback unity]
    FB --> SumJ
    style Input fill:FCE4D6
    style Output fill:C5E0B4
    style Int fill:FFE699
    style Gain1 fill:FFE699
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Second-Order System Block Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Input X s] --> Sum1[Summing Node Negative Feedback]
    Sum1 --> GainK[Gain K]
    GainK --> Int1[Integrator 1 divided by s]
    Int1 --> Y1[Intermediate signal v s]
    Y1 --> Int2[Integrator 1 divided by s]
    Int2 --> Y2[Position output]
    Y2 --> Damp[Feedback path 2 zeta Wn s]
    Damp --> Sum1
    Y2 --> Spring[Feedback path Wn squared s]
    Spring --> Sum1
    Y2 --> Out[Output Y s]
    style X fill:DEEBF7
    style Out fill:C5E0B4
    style GainK fill:FFE699
    style Int1 fill:FFE699
    style Int2 fill:FFE699
```

## 4.4 Step Response Comparison Chart — Damping Effects

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Damping Ratio] --> B[Zeta equals 0]
    A --> C[Zeta less than 1]
    A --> D[Zeta equals 1]
    A --> E[Zeta greater than 1]
    B --> B1[Undamped: pure sine oscillation]
    C --> C1[Underdamped: overshoot with decaying oscillation]
    D --> D1[Critically damped: fastest no overshoot]
    E --> E1[Overdamped: slow exponential approach]
    style B1 fill:FFC7CE
    style C1 fill:FFE699
    style D1 fill:C5E0B4
    style E1 fill:BDD7EE
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Static Error Composition Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    TrueInput[True Value theta t] --> MeasSystem[Measuring System]
    MeasSystem --> MeasOutput[Measured Value theta m]
    MeasOutput --> ErrorCalc[Error Computation Block]
    TrueInput --> ErrorCalc
    ErrorCalc --> AccError[Accuracy Error]
    ErrorCalc --> LinError[Linearity Error]
    ErrorCalc --> HysError[Hysteresis Error]
    ErrorCalc --> DriftError[Drift Error]
    AccError --> Combined[RSS or Arithmetic Sum]
    LinError --> Combined
    HysError --> Combined
    DriftError --> Combined
    Combined --> FinalError[Total Uncertainty]
    style TrueInput fill:DEEBF7
    style FinalError fill:FCE4D6
    style MeasSystem fill:FFE699
```
