### Steady-State Harmonic Response (Sinusoidal Base Excitation)

If the housing vibrates sinusoidally as $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state relative displacement has amplitude:

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{(\omega / \omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega / \omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega / \omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Observation:** The seismic accelerometer is designed to operate in the region where $\omega \gg \omega_n$. In that region, $X/Y \rightarrow 1/\xi^2 \propto \omega^2 \cdot Y$, meaning the relative displacement is **proportional to the input acceleration**.

### Three Operating Regimes

| Regime | Frequency Range | Output Quantity | Use |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-----|
| **Displacement mode** | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $x \approx y$ | Seismograph (measures base displacement) |
| **Velocity mode** (with $\zeta = 1$) | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ | $x \propto \dot{y}$ | Velometer |
| **Acceleration mode** | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $x \propto \ddot{y}$ | Accelerometer |

### Phase Angle

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2\zeta(\omega / \omega_n)}{1 - (\omega / \omega_n)^2}\right]$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equation of Motion of a Seismic Accelerometer

### Free Body Analysis

Let the **seismic mass** $m$ have absolute displacement $z(t)$. The housing has absolute displacement $y(t)$. The relative displacement is:

$$x(t) = z(t) - y(t)$$

The spring force on the mass (acting to restore it to the equilibrium position relative to the housing) is:

$$F_{spring} = -k(z - y) = -k \cdot x$$

The damper force is:

$$F_{damper} = -c(\dot{z} - \dot{y}) = -c \cdot \dot{x}$$

Newton's second law applied to the mass $m$:

$$\sum F = m \ddot{z}$$

$$-k(z - y) - c(\dot{z} - \dot{y}) = m \ddot{z}$$

Substituting $x = z - y$:

$$-k x - c \dot{x} = m \ddot{z}$$

Since $z = x + y$, we have $\ddot{z} = \ddot{x} + \ddot{y}$, hence:

$$-k x - c \dot{x} = m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y})$$

Rearranging:

$$m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = -m \ddot{y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental governing equation** of a seismic accelerometer / vibrometer. It is a **forced, second-order, linear differential equation** with constant coefficients.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Sinusoidal Response

Assume **sinusoidal base motion** $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$, so the input acceleration is:

$$\ddot{y}(t) = -\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

Assume a steady-state solution of the form $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

Compute derivatives:

$$\dot{x} = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Substitute into the governing equation:

$$m(-\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi)) + c(\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)) + k(X \sin(\omega t - \phi)) = -m(-\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t))$$

Rearranging:

$$(k - m\omega^2) X \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) = m \omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

For the solution to be valid for all $t$, the amplitude and phase must satisfy (using phasor / complex impedance method):

$$(k - m\omega^2) X \angle -\phi + c\omega X \angle (90° - \phi) = m\omega^2 Y \angle 0°$$

Equivalently, in complex form $x = X e^{j(\omega t - \phi)}$:

$$[(k - m\omega^2) - jc\omega] X = m\omega^2 Y$$

Taking magnitudes:

$$X \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2} = m\omega^2 Y$$

Substituting $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:

$$X \sqrt{m^2(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + 4m^2\zeta^2\omega_n^2 \omega^2} = m\omega^2 Y$$

$$X \sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 \omega^2} = \omega^2 Y$$

Dividing both sides by $Y \omega_n^2$:

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

This is the **magnitude ratio** of the seismic transducer.

### Phase Angle Derivation

From the same complex equation, the phase lag $\phi$ of $x$ relative to the input $y$ is:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right] = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof That $x \propto \ddot{y}$ When $\omega \gg \omega_n$

When the excitation frequency $\omega$ is much greater than the natural frequency $\omega_n$ (i.e., $\omega/\omega_n \gg 1$), the term $[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]$ is dominated by $-(\omega/\omega_n)^2$.

Therefore:

$$(\omega/\omega_n)^2 - 1 \approx (\omega/\omega_n)^2$$

And:

$$\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 \approx (\omega/\omega_n)^4 \gg \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2$$

Hence the magnitude ratio simplifies to:

$$\frac{X}{Y} \approx \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{(\omega/\omega_n)^2} = 1$$

Wait — this gives $X \approx Y$, which is the **displacement** case. Let us reconsider. For *acceleration measurement* in a seismic instrument, the ratio of relative displacement $X$ to the input **acceleration** amplitude $A = \omega^2 Y$ is what matters:

$$\frac{X}{A} = \frac{X}{\omega^2 Y} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2 \sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

For $\omega \gg \omega_n$:

$$\frac{X}{A} \approx \frac{1}{\omega_n^2 \cdot (\omega/\omega_n)^2} = \frac{1}{\omega^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(\omega/\omega_n)^0} \cdot \text{correction} \approx \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}$$

More precisely, the standard accelerometer form:

$$\left|\frac{X}{A}\right| = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (and at low damping, e.g., $\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$):**
> 
> $$\left|\frac{X}{A}\right| \approx \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \text{constant}$$
> 
> This means the **relative displacement $x$ is directly proportional to the input acceleration $a_{in}$**, with calibration constant $1/\omega_n^2$. This is the **accelerometer operating principle**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A seismic accelerometer has a mass of $0.05$ kg and a spring stiffness of $4000$ N/m. Find:
1. The natural frequency.
2. The amplitude of relative displacement when the housing is vibrated at $50$ Hz with an amplitude of $0.25$ mm. Take $\zeta = 0.65$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{0.05}} = \sqrt{80000} \approx 282.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{282.84}{2\pi} \approx 45.02 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency ratio:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\xi = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{314.16}{282.84} \approx 1.111$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude ratio:**

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{(1.111)^2}{\sqrt{[1 - (1.111)^2]^2 + [2(0.65)(1.111)]^2}}$$

$$(1.111)^2 = 1.234$$

$$1 - 1.234 = -0.234 \Rightarrow (-0.234)^2 = 0.0548$$

$$2 \times 0.65 \times 1.111 = 1.4443 \Rightarrow (1.4443)^2 = 2.086$$

$$\sqrt{0.0548 + 2.086} = \sqrt{2.141} = 1.463$$

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{1.234}{1.463} \approx 0.844$$

**Step 4 — Amplitude of relative displacement:**

$$X = 0.844 \times Y = 0.844 \times 0.25 \text{ mm} \approx 0.211 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Inferred acceleration:**

$$a_{in} = \omega^2 Y = (314.16)^2 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \approx 24.68 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 2.52 \, g$$

**Step 6 — Phase lag:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2(0.65)(1.111)}{1 - (1.111)^2}\right] = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{1.4443}{-0.234}\right] = \tan^{-1}(-6.17) \approx -80.8°$$

Since the denominator is negative, the actual phase lag (corrected to the second quadrant) is:

$$\phi = 180° - 80.8° = 99.2°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always check the sign of $[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]$. If negative, the phase angle lies in the second quadrant ($\phi > 90°$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Seismic Accelerometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vibrating Body / Housing"] --> B["Relative Motion x t"]
    B --> C["Spring of Stiffness k"]
    B --> D["Dashpot of Damping c"]
    B --> E["Seismic Mass m"]
    C --> F["Restoring Force -k times x"]
    D --> G["Damping Force -c times x dot"]
    E --> H["Inertial Force m times z double dot"]
    F --> I["Newton Second Law Sum of Forces"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Governing Equation m x double dot plus c x dot plus k x equals minus m y double dot"]
    J --> K{"Frequency Ratio omega over omega_n"}
    K --> L["omega less than omega_n: Displacement Mode Seismograph"]
    K --> M["omega approx omega_n: Velocity Mode with zeta equals 1"]
    K --> N["omega much greater than omega_n: Acceleration Mode"]
    N --> O["Output x proportional to Input Acceleration a_in"]
```

## 4.2 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Signal-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vibrating Structure"] --> B["Base of Accelerometer"]
    B --> C["Piezoelectric Crystal Disc"]
    D["Seismic Mass m"] --> C
    C --> E["Charge Q generated Q equals d times m times a"]
    E --> F["Charge Amplifier or High Impedance Probe"]
    F --> G["Output Voltage V proportional to a"]
    G --> H["Signal Conditioning Band Pass Filter"]
    H --> I["Oscilloscope or DAQ"]
    style A fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#a00000
    style I fill:#e2ffe2,stroke:#008000
```

## 4.3 LVDT Accelerometer — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vibrating Base / Housing"] --> B["Inertial Mass m on Leaf Spring"]
    B --> C["LVDT Ferromagnetic Core"]
    C --> D["Primary Coil Excited by AC V_s sin omega t"]
    C --> E["Secondary Coil A and Secondary Coil B"]
    E --> F["Differential Output V_a minus V_b"]
    F --> G["Phase Sensitive Demodulator"]
    G --> H["DC Output Voltage V_out proportional to x"]
    H --> I["Calibrated as V_out proportional to a_in"]
    style A fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806000
    style I fill:#e2ffe2,stroke:#008000
```

## 4.4 Potentiometric Accelerometer — Construction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Vibrating Housing"] --> B["Spring of Stiffness k"]
    B --> C["Seismic Mass m with Wiper Arm"]
    C --> D["Resistance Track R_total"]
    D --> E["Excitation Voltage V_ex applied across Track"]
    C --> F["Output Voltage at Wiper V_out equals V_ex times x over L"]
    F --> G["x is proportional to a_in in linear range"]
    G --> H["DC Voltmeter or Data Logger"]
    style A fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#a00000
    style H fill:#e2ffe2,stroke:#008000
```

## 4.5 Frequency Response Map of a Seismic Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Seismic Accelerometer Frequency Response X over Y versus omega over omega_n"] --> B["Region 1: omega over omega_n less than 0.2 - DISPLACEMENT MODE"]
    A --> C["Region 2: 0.6 less than omega over omega_n less than 1.5 - RESONANCE PEAK at zeta equals 0"]
    A --> D["Region 3: omega over omega_n greater than 2.5 - ACCELERATION MODE flat response for zeta approx 0.6 to 0.7"]
    style B fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#a00000
    style D fill:#e2ffe2,stroke:#008000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**Q:** *List any three advantages of piezoelectric accelerometers over LVDT-based accelerometers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Wide frequency range** (1 Hz to 50 kHz) compared to LVDT accelerometers (typically < 1 kHz).
2. **Smaller size and lighter weight** for a given sensitivity, making them suitable for delicate structures.
3. **No moving electrical contacts** and inherently high stiffness gives them a very high natural frequency — excellent for shock and high-frequency vibration.
4. *(Optional 4th point)* Rugged, hermetically sealed construction suitable for harsh industrial environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (1 mark each):** Three distinct advantages = 3 marks. Students often lose a mark by repeating "no friction" twice — phrase each point differently.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Q:** *A seismic accelerometer has a natural frequency of 25 Hz. State the frequency range in which it must be operated to act as an accelerometer and explain why.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The accelerometer must operate at frequencies **at least 4 to 5 times the natural frequency**, i.e., **$\omega \gg \omega_n$** or equivalently **$f \gg 25$ Hz, practically $f > 100$ Hz**.
- In this region, the magnitude ratio $|X/A| \approx 1/\omega_n^2$ is **constant**, ensuring the relative displacement is **directly proportional to the input acceleration** (independent of frequency).
- Below the natural frequency, the output is dominated by displacement or velocity and would be frequency-dependent, causing **distortion** of the measured acceleration waveform.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Format Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full. The choice is *module-internal*.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**Q:**  
**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working principle of a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the expression for the charge output. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A **seismic accelerometer** has a mass of 0.08 kg and spring stiffness of 6000 N/m. The housing is vibrated sinusoidally at 80 Hz with peak amplitude 0.5 mm. Calculate:  
    (i) Natural frequency  
    (ii) Frequency ratio  
    (iii) Peak relative displacement (assume $\zeta = 0.6$)  
    (iv) Peak input acceleration in $g$ units. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle (Diagram as per Section 4.2):**

1. The base of the accelerometer is rigidly mounted on the vibrating body.
2. The seismic mass $m$ is held in contact with a **piezoelectric crystal** (e.g., PZT).
3. When the base accelerates, the inertial mass exerts a **force $F = m \cdot a$** on the crystal.
4. The crystal generates a charge **$Q = d \cdot F$** (direct piezoelectric effect).
5. The charge is converted to voltage by a **charge amplifier**.

**Derivation:**

The force on the crystal from the mass:

$$F = m \cdot a$$

By the constitutive piezoelectric equation:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ is the charge sensitivity of the crystal in C/N.

The corresponding voltage across a capacitance $C_p$ (crystal + cable + amplifier input):

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot m \cdot a}{C_p}$$

**Sensitivity of accelerometer:**

$$S = \frac{V}{a} = \frac{d \cdot m}{C_p} \quad \text{(V·s}^2/\text{m)}$$

Or equivalently, in charge mode:

$$S_q = \frac{Q}{a} = d \cdot m \quad \text{(C·s}^2/\text{m)}$$

*(7 marks: 3 marks for working principle + diagram, 4 marks for derivation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $m = 0.08$ kg, $k = 6000$ N/m
- $f = 80$ Hz, $Y = 0.5$ mm = $0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\zeta = 0.6$

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{6000}{0.08}} = \sqrt{75000} \approx 273.86 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{273.86}{2\pi} \approx 43.6 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Frequency ratio:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 80 = 502.65 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\xi = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{502.65}{273.86} \approx 1.835$$

**(iii) Peak relative displacement:**

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{\xi^2}{\sqrt{(1 - \xi^2)^2 + (2\zeta\xi)^2}}$$

$\xi^2 = 3.367$  
$1 - \xi^2 = -2.367 \Rightarrow (1 - \xi^2)^2 = 5.602$  
$2\zeta\xi = 2 \times 0.6 \times 1.835 = 2.202 \Rightarrow (2\zeta\xi)^2 = 4.849$  
$\sqrt{5.602 + 4.849} = \sqrt{10.451} \approx 3.233$

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{3.367}{3.233} \approx 1.041$$

$$X = 1.041 \times 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.521 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Peak input acceleration:**

$$\omega^2 = (502.65)^2 = 252656.7 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$a_{peak} = \omega^2 \cdot Y = 252656.7 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \approx 126.33 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_{peak} \text{ in } g = \frac{126.33}{9.81} \approx 12.88 \, g$$

*(7 marks: 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 distribution)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

**Q:**  
**(a)** With a labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **seismic accelerometer**. Derive its governing equation of motion. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Explain the three operating regions of a seismic accelerometer with reference to the frequency ratio $\omega / \omega_n$. What is the role of damping in these regions? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**
- A **seismic mass $m$** is suspended by a **spring (stiffness $k$)** inside a rigid housing.
- A **dashpot (damping $c$)** is connected in parallel with the spring.
- The housing is attached to the vibrating body.
- The **relative displacement** $x$ between the mass and the housing is measured by a suitable displacement transducer (LVDT, potentiometer, or capacitive probe).

**Derivation of Governing Equation:**

Forces on the seismic mass:
- Spring force: $-k(z - y) = -kx$
- Damping force: $-c(\dot{z} - \dot{y}) = -c\dot{x}$
- Inertial force: $m\ddot{z}$

Newton's second law:

$$m\ddot{z} = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

Substituting $z = x + y \Rightarrow \ddot{z} = \ddot{x} + \ddot{y}$:

$$m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}}$$

If we let $a_{in}(t) = \ddot{y}(t)$ denote the input acceleration:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m \cdot a_{in}(t)$$

This is a second-order linear differential equation. With $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$, the input acceleration has amplitude $\omega^2 Y$.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram + description, 4 for derivation)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Three Operating Regions:**

**Region 1 — $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (Displacement Mode / Seismograph):**
- Magnitude ratio $X/Y \rightarrow 0$ as $\omega \rightarrow 0$ (with low damping), but with proper damping calibration $X \approx Y$.
- The relative displacement equals the **base displacement**.
- Used for **earthquake recording** and low-frequency ground motion.
- Damping must be **high (ζ ≈ 0.6 to 0.7)** to avoid resonance distortion in nearby frequencies.

**Region 2 — $\omega \approx \omega_n$ (Velocity Mode):**
- With damping ratio $\zeta = 1$ (critical damping is not strictly needed; often $\zeta = 0.7$ is optimal), the magnitude ratio becomes:
- $X/Y \approx Y / \omega_n$ for moderate frequencies → output proportional to **velocity** ($\omega Y$).
- Used as a **velometer** (velocity meter).
- Damping must be **accurately tuned** to obtain a flat velocity response over a wide frequency band.

**Region 3 — $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (Acceleration Mode):**
- For $\omega \gg \omega_n$, the magnitude ratio $X/Y$ becomes $\approx 1$ in displacement terms, but more importantly, the ratio $X/(\omega^2 Y) = X/A \approx 1/\omega_n^2$ is **constant**.
- Therefore $x \propto a_{in}$ — used as an **accelerometer**.
- Damping should be moderate ($\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$) to **suppress resonance peak** and provide **stable flat response** over the operating band.

**Role of Damping — Summary Table:**

| Region | Optimal Damping $\zeta$ | Purpose |
|--------|--------------------------|---------|
| Displacement mode | 0.6 – 0.7 | Avoid distortion near resonance |
| Velocity mode | ~ 0.7 | Widen flat response |
| Acceleration mode | 0.6 – 0.7 | Suppress resonance, stabilize calibration |

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 distribution for the three regions and damping role)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong units in the natural frequency formula** — Forgetting to convert $\omega_n$ (rad/s) to $f_n$ (Hz) by dividing by $2\pi$. Loss: 1 mark per occurrence.
> 
> 2. **Sign error in the phase angle** — When $\omega > \omega_n$, the term $[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]$ is negative, and the phase angle lies in the second quadrant ($\phi > 90°$). Forgetting this quadrant correction loses 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Confusing displacement, velocity, and acceleration modes** — Students often write "the accelerometer measures displacement" — always specify the **frequency ratio regime** and the **input quantity**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign in the governing equation** — The acceleration input enters as $-m\ddot{y}$, not $+m\ddot{y}$. The sign indicates the *inertial* (opposing) reaction.
> 
> 5. **Piezoelectric charge coefficient units** — $d$ is in **C/N** or **pC/N**, not N/C. Using wrong units loses marks on derivation steps.
> 
> 6. **LVDT demodulation** — Many students forget to mention the **phase-sensitive demodulator** needed to convert the AC differential output to a DC voltage proportional to displacement. This is a frequently tested 1-mark point.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final consolidated points for last-minute revision:**

### Universal Concepts
- An accelerometer **measures the relative displacement $x$ between an inertial mass and its housing**, and infers acceleration from it.
- The four KTU-listed types are **piezoelectric, potentiometric, LVDT, and seismic**.

### Piezoelectric
- Based on **direct piezoelectric effect**; output is **charge $Q = d \cdot m \cdot a$**.
- **AC-coupled only** — cannot measure static (DC) acceleration.
- Sensitivity in **pC/g**; typical range: 1–50 kHz frequency; high natural frequency.
- **Shear-type construction** is the industry standard (better thermal/base strain isolation).

### Potentiometric
- Output is a **DC voltage** proportional to the slider position.
- $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot x/L$.
- **Limited bandwidth (< 25 Hz)** and **mechanical wear** are key disadvantages.

### LVDT
- **Frictionless, infinite resolution, DC-capable** (with proper demodulation).
- Output: amplitude-modulated AC, demodulated to DC.
- $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ after demodulation.

### Seismic — Key Equations (MUST MEMORIZE)
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — natural angular frequency
- $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ — damping ratio
- Magnitude ratio:
$$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2]^2 + [2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)]^2}}$$
- Phase angle:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right]$$
- **Accelerometer operating region**: $\omega \gg \omega_n$ with $\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ (optimal damping to flatten response).
- **Displacement region**: $\omega \ll \omega_n$.
- **Velocity region**: $\omega \approx \omega_n$.

### Common Pitfall to Remember
- The **input to the equation is $\ddot{y}$ (acceleration)**, not $y$ (displacement) — students often forget the $\omega^2$ factor when substituting $y(t) = Y \sin \omega t$.

### Units to Watch
- $\omega_n$: **rad/s**; $f_n$: **Hz** (divide by $2\pi$).
- $\zeta$: **dimensionless**; $k$: **N/m**; $m$: **kg**; $c$: **N·s/m**.
- Sensitivity: **V·s²/m** (or pC/g for charge-mode piezo).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Contact Type Transducers - Infrared, Ultrasonic Proximity Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Contact Type Transducers - Infrared, Ultrasonic Proximity Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Non-Contact Type Transducers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Contact Transducers** are devices that measure physical quantities (such as **displacement**, **distance**, **proximity**, **level**, **temperature**, or **object presence**) **without any physical mechanical contact** with the target object. They convert a measured stimulus into an electrical signal using **electromagnetic radiation** (light, IR) or **acoustic waves** (ultrasound) as the sensing medium.

These transducers fall under the **passive type** classification in many cases, but more importantly, they are characterized by:
- **No mechanical wear and tear** (no physical touch)
- **High response speed**
- **Ability to sense fragile, soft, hot, moving, or hazardous targets**
- **Long operational life**

## 1.2 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact electronic sensor that emits **infrared radiation** (wavelength range **700 nm to 1 mm**) from an **IR LED (emitter)** and detects the radiation reflected back from a target object using a **photodiode or phototransistor (receiver)**. The intensity or angle of the reflected IR beam is converted into an electrical signal proportional to the distance or presence of the object.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Batsman and the Glow Ball"

Imagine you (the sensor) throw a glowing red ball (the IR beam) at a wall (the target). If the wall is **very close**, the ball comes back to you almost instantly and **brightly** (high intensity). If the wall is **far away**, the ball takes longer to return and is **dimmer** (low intensity). The IR sensor works exactly like this — it fires an invisible infrared "ball" and measures how much bounces back to know the distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard operational wavelength used in commercial IR proximity sensors: **940 nm** (near-infrared band). Photons at this wavelength are invisible to the human eye but easily detected by silicon photodiodes.

## 1.3 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact transducer that emits **high-frequency sound waves** (frequency **> 20 kHz**, typically **40 kHz** in industrial sensors) using a **piezoelectric crystal (transmitter)** and listens for the **echo** reflected from a target object using a **piezoelectric receiver**. The **time-of-flight (ToF)** of the echo is measured to determine the distance of the object.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Man's Cane"

Picture a blind person walking in a corridor (the sensor). They tap their cane (send out an ultrasonic chirp) and **listen carefully** for the echo. If the echo comes back **quickly**, the wall is **close**. If the echo is **delayed**, the wall is **far**. The ultrasonic sensor performs the exact same action with **sound waves instead of a cane** and **microseconds instead of human hearing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard industrial ultrasonic frequency: **40 kHz** (chosen because it is well above human hearing, has low ambient noise interference, and offers a good balance between range and resolution). Speed of sound in air at **20°C**: **343 m/s**.

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | IR Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Electromagnetic (light) | Acoustic (sound) |
| Frequency / Wavelength | **940 nm** (light) | **40 kHz** (sound) |
| Operating Range | **10 cm – 80 cm** (typical) | **3 cm – 10 m** |
| Affected by | Color, surface reflectivity, sunlight | Temperature, humidity, soft surfaces |
| Speed | **Very fast** (speed of light) | Slower (speed of sound) |
| Target Material | Reflective, opaque | Hard, dense (any color) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor — Operating Principle

The IR proximity sensor operates on the principle of **triangulation** combined with the **inverse square law of radiation**.

### Working Steps:

1. **Emission Stage:** An **IR LED** (typically GaAs-based) is forward-biased by a driver circuit, emitting modulated IR pulses at **940 nm**. Modulation (e.g., at **38 kHz** carrier) helps reject ambient light interference.

2. **Propagation Stage:** The IR photons travel through air and strike the target object. Depending on surface properties, a portion of the radiation is **diffusely reflected** back toward the sensor.

3. **Reception Stage:** A **photodiode (PD)** or **phototransistor** placed at an angle receives the reflected IR. The geometry of the emitter–receiver pair is arranged such that the **angle of incidence** of the reflected beam changes with distance.

4. **Signal Conditioning:** The photodiode current is converted to voltage using a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)** and then compared with a threshold to produce a digital HIGH/LOW output.

### Why Modulation Matters (The "Why"):

Ambient sunlight contains IR components. Without modulation, the photodiode would be flooded with sunlight noise. By pulsing the IR LED at a specific frequency (e.g., **38 kHz**) and using a **band-pass filter** at the receiver, the sensor effectively "tunes in" only to its own signal — like tuning a radio to one station.

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor — Operating Principle

The ultrasonic sensor operates on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle.

### Working Steps:

1. **Excitation Stage:** A **piezoelectric ceramic disc** (usually PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) is excited by a burst of **AC voltage at 40 kHz** (8 to 16 cycles). The piezo element vibrates and emits an ultrasonic pulse.

2. **Propagation Stage:** The ultrasonic wave travels through air at the **speed of sound (c)**. It strikes a target object and is **reflected** (echo).

3. **Reception Stage:** The same (or a separate) piezo element receives the echo and converts it back into a small AC voltage.

4. **Time Measurement:** A **microcontroller or timing circuit** measures the elapsed time **Δt** between emission and reception of the echo.

5. **Distance Calculation:** The distance **d** is computed as:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the **round trip** (to the target and back).

### Why "Divide by 2"? (The "How"):

The time **Δt** is for the wave to travel **FROM the sensor TO the object AND BACK**. The actual one-way distance is half of the total distance covered. Hence the division by 2.

## 2.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c_{light} = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** $c_{sound} = 343$ **m/s**
- **IR emission wavelength (typical):** **940 nm**
- **Ultrasonic frequency (typical):** **40 kHz**
- **Wavelength of 40 kHz sound in air:** $\lambda = c/f = 343/40000 \approx$ **8.575 mm**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/(m²·K⁴)**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **must-memorize** formula box for KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{c_{sound} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Distance from ultrasonic ToF | **meters (m)** |
| 2 | $c_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | Speed of sound in air vs temperature $T$ (°C) | **m/s** |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of sound/light | **m** |
| 4 | $E = h \cdot f$ | Energy of a photon (Planck's relation) | **Joules (J)** |
| 5 | $P = \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$ | Radiated power from black body | **Watts (W)** |
| 6 | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ | Inverse square law (radiation intensity) | **W/m²** |
| 7 | $I_{PD} = R \cdot \Phi$ | Photodiode current (R = responsivity) | **Amperes (A)** |
| 8 | $\eta_{piezo} = \dfrac{d_{33}^2 \cdot \varepsilon_{33}^T}{\varepsilon_0}$ | Piezo coupling (efficiency parameter) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always use $c_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6T$ in numerical problems when temperature is given. At **T = 0°C**, $c = 331.4$ m/s; at **T = 20°C**, $c = 343.4$ m/s; at **T = 50°C**, $c = 361.4$ m/s.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IR sensors:** Automatic faucets, robot obstacle avoidance (line-following), object counters, paper-edge detection in printers, smartphone proximity (screen turn-off during calls), security motion detectors.
- **Ultrasonic sensors:** Car reversing alarms, liquid level measurement in tanks, drone altimeters, parking lot vehicle detection, medical ultrasonography, industrial flow meters, blind-person navigation aids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distance Measurement using Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight

We will now derive the **fundamental distance equation** used in ultrasonic proximity sensing from first principles.

### Given:
- Speed of sound in air: $c$
- Time elapsed between emission and echo reception: $\Delta t$
- Distance from sensor to target: $d$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Express the total distance travelled by the ultrasonic pulse.**

The ultrasonic wave leaves the transmitter, travels to the object, reflects, and returns to the receiver. The **total path length** $L_{total}$ covered by the wave is:
$$L_{total} = c \cdot \Delta t$$

Here, we use the basic kinematic relation: **distance = speed × time**.

**Step 2: Recognize that the path is round-trip.**

The total distance $L_{total}$ is **twice** the actual one-way distance $d$ between the sensor and the target. This is because the wave must travel **to** the target and **back** to the sensor.
$$L_{total} = 2 \cdot d$$

**Step 3: Equate the two expressions for total distance.**

From Step 1 and Step 2:
$$2 \cdot d = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4: Solve for the one-way distance $d$.**

Dividing both sides by 2:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the temperature-dependent speed of sound.**

In air, the speed of sound depends on temperature $T$ (in °C):
$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

**Step 6: Final working equation for ultrasonic distance measurement.**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{(331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T) \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

This is the **standard formula** used in all ultrasonic ranging problems in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation: Speed of Sound in Air as a Function of Temperature

The speed of sound in an ideal gas is given by:
$$c = \sqrt{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_{K}}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = adiabatic index (= 1.4 for air)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $T_{K}$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

### Linearization for engineering use:

When expanded around **T = 0°C (273.15 K)**, this becomes the **linear approximation**:
$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

### Derivation Walkthrough:

**Step 1:** Start with $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T_{K}}$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values at $T_K = 273.15$ K (0°C):
$$c_0 = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 273.15} = \sqrt{109,722.6} \approx 331.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $c$ with respect to $T_K$:
$$\frac{dc}{dT_K} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R}{T_K}}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $T_K = 273.15$ K:
$$\frac{dc}{dT_K} = \frac{\sqrt{1.4 \times 287}}{2 \times \sqrt{273.15}} = \frac{\sqrt{401.8}}{2 \times 16.527} = \frac{20.045}{33.054} \approx 0.606 \text{ m/s/K}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Taylor series (first-order):
$$c(T) = c_0 + \frac{dc}{dT_K} \cdot T = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Ultrasonic Distance Calculation (KTU Style)

### Problem:
An ultrasonic sensor operates at **40 kHz** in air at a temperature of **30°C**. The measured time between transmitted pulse and received echo is **5.8 ms**. Calculate:
1. The speed of sound at 30°C
2. The distance of the object from the sensor
3. The wavelength of the ultrasonic wave

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Part (1): Speed of sound at 30°C**

Using the linear formula:
$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30$$
$$c = 331.4 + 18.0 = 349.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Part (2): Distance calculation**

Using the ToF formula:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{349.4 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

Let us compute the numerator first:
$$349.4 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3} = 2026.52 \times 10^{-3} = 2.02652 \text{ m}$$

Dividing by 2:
$$d = \frac{2.02652}{2} = 1.01326 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 1.013 \text{ m} \approx 101.3 \text{ cm}}$$

**Part (3): Wavelength of the ultrasonic wave**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{349.4}{40{,}000} = 8.735 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 8.735 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — IR Sensor Triangulation Concept

### Problem:
In an IR triangulation sensor, the IR emitter and photodiode are separated by a baseline of **20 mm**. The reflected beam strikes the photodiode at an angle $\theta$ from the normal. If the photodiode is at a focal length of **15 mm** from the lens, and the spot position shifts by **2 mm** on the detector for a target moving from **100 mm** to **150 mm**, determine the sensitivity of the sensor in mm/mm.

### Solution Framework:

The **triangulation principle** states that for a target at distance $d$, the reflected beam strikes the detector at a position $x$ given by:
$$x = f \cdot \tan(\theta) = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

where $b$ is the baseline and $f$ is the focal length.

**Step 1:** Sensitivity = $\dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta d}$

$$\Delta x = 2 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta d = 150 - 100 = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:**
$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 0.04 \text{ mm/mm}$$

This means for every 1 mm of target movement, the spot on the detector moves by 0.04 mm. The sensor becomes **less sensitive at greater distances**, which is a fundamental limitation of triangulation-based IR sensors.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Quick Reference

| Scenario | Use IR Sensor? | Use Ultrasonic Sensor? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detect black rubber on conveyor | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Black absorbs IR; reflects sound |
| Measure distance in sunlight | ❌ Poor | ✅ Good | Sunlight overwhelms IR |
| Detect transparent glass | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Glass reflects sound; passes IR |
| High-speed counting (<1 ms) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Slower | Light is faster than sound |
| Liquid level in chemical tank | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Safer, no contamination |
| Robot line-following (white line) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Overkill | Cost-effective with IR |
| Object 8 m away | ❌ Limited | ✅ Yes | Ultrasonic has longer range |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IR Proximity Sensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[Oscillator 38kHz] --> DRV[IR LED Driver]
    DRV --> IREMIT[IR LED Emitter 940nm]
    IREMIT -. IR Beam .-> TARGET1[Target Object]
    TARGET1 -. Reflected IR .-> LENS[Focusing Lens]
    LENS --> PD[Photodiode Receiver]
    PD --> TIA[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    TIA --> BPF[Band-Pass Filter 38kHz]
    BPF --> COMP[Comparator]
    COMP --> OUT1[Digital Output HIGH or LOW]
    PD --> AMP[Optional DC Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[ADC for Analog Distance]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller]
    MCU --> DISP[Display or Threshold Output]
```

### Explanation of the IR Sensor Flow:

1. The **oscillator** generates a **38 kHz** square wave.
2. The **driver** modulates current through the **IR LED**, producing a pulsed 940 nm beam.
3. The beam strikes a target and a portion returns to the **photodiode**.
4. The photodiode current is amplified by a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)**.
5. A **band-pass filter** centered at **38 kHz** rejects ambient light noise.
6. A **comparator** converts the AC signal into a clean digital output.
7. An optional DC path provides analog distance estimation to a microcontroller.

## 4.2 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU2[Microcontroller Trigger Pulse] --> DRV2[40kHz Burst Generator 8 to 16 cycles]
    DRV2 --> PZT1[Piezoelectric Transmitter]
    PZT1 -. Ultrasonic Pulse 40kHz .-> AIR[Air Medium]
    AIR -. Propagates .-> TGT2[Target Object]
    TGT2 -. Echo .-> AIR2[Air Medium Return Path]
    AIR2 --> PZT2[Piezoelectric Receiver]
    PZT2 --> AMP2[Low Noise Amplifier]
    AMP2 --> DET[Envelope Detector]
    DET --> COMP2[Threshold Comparator]
    COMP2 --> TIMER[Timer Capture Echo Time]
    TIMER --> CALC[Distance Calculator d = c dt over 2]
    CALC --> DSP2[Display or Output]
    MCU2 --> TEMP[Temperature Sensor Compensation]
    TEMP --> CALC
```

### Explanation of the Ultrasonic Sensor Flow:

1. The **microcontroller** issues a **trigger pulse** (≥ 10 µs).
2. The **burst generator** produces a 40 kHz AC burst (8 to 16 cycles).
3. The **piezoelectric transmitter** converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration, launching an ultrasonic wave.
4. The wave travels through air, hits the **target**, and reflects back.
5. The **piezoelectric receiver** converts the echo into a small AC voltage.
6. The **low-noise amplifier** boosts the weak echo.
7. The **envelope detector** + **comparator** convert the AC echo into a clean digital edge.
8. The **timer** captures $\Delta t$, the **calculator** computes $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$.
9. A **temperature sensor** compensates for the variation in $c$ with temperature.

## 4.3 Comparative Operating Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IRP[IR Proximity Pipeline]
        IE1[IR LED emits 940nm] --> IT1[Photon hits target]
        IT1 --> IR1[Photodiode detects reflection]
        IR1 --> IP1[Signal conditioning 38kHz BPF]
        IP1 --> IO1[Distance or Presence Output]
    end
    subgraph USP[Ultrasonic Pipeline]
        UE1[Piezo emits 40kHz chirp] --> UT1[Sound hits target]
        UT1 --> UR1[Echo returns to piezo]
        UR1 --> UP1[Time of Flight measured dt]
        UP1 --> UC1[Distance = c dt over 2]
        UC1 --> UO1[Distance Output]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define a non-contact type transducer. List two examples and one key advantage over contact-type transducers.**

**Model Answer:**

A non-contact type transducer is a measuring device that senses a physical quantity (such as distance, level, or temperature) **without making physical mechanical contact** with the target object. It uses electromagnetic radiation or acoustic waves as the sensing medium.

**Examples:**
1. **Infrared (IR) proximity sensor**
2. **Ultrasonic proximity sensor**

**Key advantage over contact-type transducers:**
- **No mechanical wear, friction, or fatigue** → longer life, no contamination of target, suitable for fragile/hazardous/moving objects.

*(Marks distribution: Definition – 1, Examples – 1, Advantage – 1)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between IR and ultrasonic proximity sensors based on (i) sensing medium, (ii) operating range, and (iii) one application each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IR Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Sensing medium | Electromagnetic (IR light, 940 nm) | Acoustic (sound, 40 kHz) |
| (ii) Operating range | 10 cm – 80 cm | 3 cm – 10 m |
| (iii) Application | Automatic hand sanitizer dispenser | Car reversing / parking sensor |

*(Marks distribution: Each correct entry – 1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **ultrasonic proximity sensor**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor operates at 40 kHz. The measured echo time is **4.5 ms** in air at **25°C**. Calculate: **(i)** the speed of sound, **(ii)** the distance of the object, and **(iii)** the wavelength of the ultrasonic wave. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Working Principle of Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes)*

**Working:**

1. The microcontroller sends a **trigger pulse (≥ 10 µs)** to the burst generator.
2. The burst generator produces an **8-to-16 cycle AC burst at 40 kHz**, which excites the **piezoelectric transmitter** (PZT crystal).
3. The PZT crystal vibrates mechanically, producing an **ultrasonic wave** that propagates through the air at the speed of sound.
4. The wave strikes the target and is **reflected back** as an echo.
5. The **piezoelectric receiver** captures the echo and converts it into a small AC voltage.
6. This weak signal is **amplified** by a low-noise amplifier and passed through an **envelope detector** + **comparator** to produce a clean digital edge.
7. A **timer** measures the time interval $\Delta t$ between the trigger and the received echo.
8. The **distance is computed** as:
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$
9. A **temperature sensor** provides compensation because $c$ varies with $T$.

**Key Features:**
- The factor of 2 in the formula accounts for the **round trip** of the wave.
- The **40 kHz** frequency is chosen because it is above human hearing and provides a good trade-off between range and resolution.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $f = 40 \text{ kHz} = 40{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- $\Delta t = 4.5 \text{ ms} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- $T = 25°C$

**Step 1: Speed of sound at 25°C**
$$c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times T = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 25$$
$$c = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Distance of the object**
$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{346.4 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

Numerator:
$$346.4 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1558.8 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5588 \text{ m}$$

Dividing by 2:
$$d = \frac{1.5588}{2} = 0.7794 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.78 \text{ m} = 78 \text{ cm}}$$

**Step 3: Wavelength of the ultrasonic wave**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{346.4}{40{,}000}$$

$$\lambda = 8.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 8.66 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **infrared (IR) proximity sensor** with a block diagram. Discuss the role of **modulation** and the **band-pass filter** in rejecting ambient noise. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare IR and ultrasonic proximity sensors in terms of (i) medium, (ii) range, (iii) effect of target color, (iv) effect of temperature, (v) response time, (vi) one application each, and (vii) one limitation each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — IR Proximity Sensor (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)*

**Working Principle:**

1. An **oscillator** generates a **38 kHz** square wave.
2. This drives the **IR LED**, causing it to emit a **modulated 940 nm** infrared beam.
3. The IR beam strikes the target object and is **diffusely reflected**.
4. The reflected beam falls on a **photodiode** through a focusing lens.
5. The photodiode produces a small current proportional to the reflected intensity.
6. A **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)** converts this current to a voltage.
7. A **band-pass filter centered at 38 kHz** removes ambient light and DC components.
8. A **comparator** outputs a digital HIGH if the signal exceeds a threshold.

**Role of Modulation (2 Marks):**
- The IR LED is pulsed at 38 kHz (not continuous DC).
- This creates a unique "fingerprint" signal.
- Ambient sunlight (which has broadband IR) cannot pass through the 38 kHz BPF.
- This dramatically improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** and allows operation in bright sunlight.

**Role of Band-Pass Filter (2 Marks):**
- Center frequency: 38 kHz
- Bandwidth: typically ±5 kHz
- Rejects:
  - 50/60 Hz ambient light flicker
  - DC sunlight
  - Random thermal noise

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Performance depends on target **color, reflectivity, and surface angle**.
- Limited range due to inverse square law: $I \propto 1/d^2$.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 Marks, 1 mark each)**

| # | Parameter | IR Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Medium | Electromagnetic (IR light) | Acoustic (sound) |
| 2 | Range | 10 cm – 80 cm | 3 cm – 10 m |
| 3 | Effect of color | High (black absorbs, white reflects) | Negligible (sound reflects from all colors) |
| 4 | Effect of temperature | Negligible | Significant (c changes with T) |
| 5 | Response time | Very fast (speed of light) | Slower (speed of sound) |
| 6 | Application | Automatic faucet, line-follower robot | Car reversing alarm, level measurement |
| 7 | Limitation | Affected by sunlight, black surfaces | Affected by soft/angled surfaces, temperature |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ultrasonic distance formula → marks deducted for incorrect distance.
> 2. **Using $c = 343$ m/s** universally without compensating for the given temperature → lost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning modulation** in the IR sensor explanation → 2-mark penalty (this is a high-weightage KTU keyword).
> 4. **Confusing photodiode (PD) with phototransistor (PT)** — both work, but specifying PD with reverse bias shows deeper understanding.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer → significant mark deduction.
> 6. **Missing the band-pass filter** in the IR block diagram → incomplete answer, marks lost.
> 7. **No mention of round-trip time** when explaining ToF → the "divide by 2" derivation is not obvious to the examiner without this explanation.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Non-contact transducers** measure without physical touch using **EM waves or acoustic waves**.
- **IR sensor:** Emits 940 nm modulated IR at 38 kHz, detects reflection via photodiode, uses BPF for noise rejection.
- **Ultrasonic sensor:** Emits 40 kHz sound burst via piezo, measures echo time, computes distance via $d = c \Delta t / 2$.
- **Key IR formula:** Inverse square law $I \propto 1/d^2$; Stefan-Boltzmann $P = \sigma A T^4$.
- **Key ultrasonic formula:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ where $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$.
- **Speed of sound in air:** 343 m/s at 20°C; increases by **0.6 m/s per °C**.
- **Speed of light:** $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (practically infinite transit time over short distances).
- **Wavelength** of 40 kHz ultrasound in air: $\lambda \approx 8.6$ mm.
- **IR** is affected by **color, sunlight, and surface angle**; **ultrasonic** is affected by **temperature, soft surfaces, and angle**.
- **Applications:** IR — robotics, automation, security; Ultrasonic — vehicle parking, level sensing, medical imaging.
- **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** is the standard piezo material used in ultrasonic sensors.
- **Photodiode** works in **photoconductive mode** (reverse-biased) for IR receivers.
- **Always divide by 2** in the ToF equation because the echo travels a round-trip path.
- **Modulation (38 kHz)** is the "secret sauce" that lets IR sensors reject sunlight.
- **Block diagrams** are mandatory in 7-mark questions — draw them neatly with labels.
- **Comparison tables** are highly scoring — use them whenever "differentiate" or "compare" is asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Level Switches, Optical Level Sensor, Ultrasonic Level Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microwave Level Switches, Optical Level Sensor, Ultrasonic Level Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — LEVEL MEASUREMENT SWITCHES & SENSORS

This module focuses on **point-level and continuous-level detection devices** that use non-contact or minimally-invasive sensing methods. We will explore three industrial-grade transducers, each exploiting a distinct physical phenomenon: electromagnetic waves (microwave), light refraction (optical), and acoustic time-of-flight (ultrasonic).

---

## 1.1 🔬 Microwave Level Switches

### 📐 Formal Definition
A **Microwave Level Switch** is a point-level (discrete ON/OFF) detection device that uses a high-frequency electromagnetic field, typically in the **ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) band of 5.8 GHz or 24 GHz**, generated at the tip of a probe. A change in the surrounding medium's **dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r$)** alters the resonance condition of the resonator circuit, which is detected to produce a switching output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Microwave switches are categorized as **point-level detectors** — they only confirm *whether* the material has reached a specific elevation, not *how much* is present. For continuous level profiling, radar gauges (FMCW) are used.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a microwave switch like a **"whisper in an empty room."** When you stand at one end of an empty corridor and speak, your voice bounces back clearly from the far wall. Now, if someone suddenly fills the corridor with soft foam, the same whisper is absorbed — and the echo disappears. A microwave switch listens to a similar "echo" of high-frequency energy. When the empty air around its tip is replaced by a liquid (or solid) with a high dielectric constant, the echo pattern changes instantly, and the switch flips.

### 🔑 Key Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | **5.8 GHz** or **24 GHz** (ISM bands) |
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) at 5.8 GHz | ≈ 51.7 mm |
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) at 24 GHz | ≈ 12.5 mm |
| Switching Output | Relay, PNP, NPN, or 4–20 mA |
| Process Connection | Threaded (½" NPT, G½, etc.) |
| Suitable Media | Liquids, slurries, powders, granules |
| Dielectric Constant Range | $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.5$ (liquids), $\varepsilon_r \geq 4$ (solids) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high frequency?** The smaller the wavelength, the more concentrated the energy near the probe tip. A 24 GHz signal is "tighter" than a 5.8 GHz signal, making it ideal for small vessels, narrow nozzles, or applications with buildup.

### ⚙️ Working Principle — Step-by-Step
1. A **Gunn diode / dielectric resonator oscillator (DRO)** generates a stable high-frequency microwave signal.
2. The signal is coupled into a **resonant cavity** (usually a coaxial line terminated by the process medium).
3. In the **empty (air) state**, the resonator is tuned to its nominal frequency. The reflected signal is minimal.
4. When the medium **reaches the probe tip**, the new dielectric loading shifts the resonance — the **standing wave pattern, amplitude, and phase** of the reflected signal all change.
5. A **Schottky detector diode** converts this RF change into a DC voltage swing, which the comparator circuit uses to energize the output relay/transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refresher on Dielectric Constant ($\varepsilon_r$):** It is the ratio of a medium's permittivity to that of free space. Water ≈ 80, oils ≈ 2–5, air = 1. A higher $\varepsilon_r$ produces a stronger switching signal.

---

## 1.2 💡 Optical Level Sensor

### 📐 Formal Definition
An **Optical Level Sensor** (also called a *photoelectric level switch* or *refractometric level switch*) is a compact, solid-state point-level detector that operates on the principle of **total internal reflection** of light at a glass prism interface. It typically uses **infrared (IR) light (≈ 940 nm wavelength)** emitted by an LED and detected by a phototransistor.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are underwater and looking up at the water surface from below — the world above appears as a bright, mirror-like ceiling. Now, if you are a fish and the water suddenly disappears (the tank is empty), looking up gives you a dull, dark image because no light reflects perfectly. An optical level sensor works exactly the same way: it has a tiny "eye" at the tip. When immersed in liquid, the light bends *into* the liquid (no reflection). In air, the light bounces back perfectly — and that bounce is the sensor's "I see air" signal.

### 🔑 Key Components
- **Infrared LED** — emitter
- **Phototransistor / Photodiode** — receiver
- **Glass or polymer prism** — the sensing tip
- **Signal conditioning circuit** — converts photocurrent to switching output

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Optical sensors are widely used in **pharmaceutical, food & beverage, and biomedical industries** because they have **no crevices, no moving parts, and are CIP/SIP (Clean-In-Place / Sterilize-In-Place) compatible**.

### ⚙️ Working Principle
1. The IR LED emits a beam aimed at the inner face of the prism.
2. In **air**, the refractive index of the prism (≈ 1.5) is much higher than that of air (≈ 1.0). The angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle**, causing **total internal reflection**. The beam reflects back to the phototransistor, which is strongly illuminated.
3. In **liquid**, the refractive indices of the prism and liquid are similar. The critical-angle condition is violated. The light **refracts out** of the prism into the liquid, and very little light returns to the phototransistor.
4. The circuit then **inverts** the logic: bright phototransistor = air (no liquid); dark phototransistor = liquid present.

### 📊 Refractive Index Table (Common Media)

| Medium | Refractive Index ($n$) |
|---|---|
| Vacuum | 1.000 |
| Air (STP) | 1.0003 |
| Water | 1.333 |
| Glass (typical prism) | 1.50 – 1.70 |
| Glycerine | 1.473 |
| Mineral Oil | 1.46 – 1.55 |
| Ethanol | 1.361 |

### 🧪 Critical Angle Formula
Using **Snell's Law**, the critical angle $\theta_c$ (measured from the normal) is given by:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_{medium}}{n_{prism}}\right)$$

For a glass prism ($n_p = 1.50$) immersed in air ($n_a = 1.00$):

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.00}{1.50}\right) = 41.8^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means that for any internal ray striking the prism-air interface at an angle greater than **41.8°** (from the normal), **100% of the light is reflected internally**. This is the foundation of optical level sensing.

---

## 1.3 🔊 Ultrasonic Level Sensor

### 📐 Formal Definition
An **Ultrasonic Level Sensor** is a non-contact, continuous-level measurement device that determines the distance to a material surface by emitting a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**, with industrial sensors commonly operating at **40 kHz, 75 kHz, or 125 kHz**) and measuring the **time-of-flight ($\Delta t$)** of the echo returning from the surface.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a bat flying in a dark cave. It screams, and the echo tells it exactly how far the wall is — even in total darkness, through smoke or dust. An ultrasonic level sensor is the engineering equivalent of this bat. It "shouts" a sound pulse (above human hearing) downward at the surface of the liquid or solid, listens carefully for the echo, and converts the round-trip time into a precise level reading.

### 🔑 Key Components
- **Piezoelectric transducer** (usually a PZT ceramic disk) — acts as both transmitter and receiver
- **Driver / pulse circuit** — excites the transducer at resonance
- **Time-measurement circuit (TDC / counter)** — measures $\Delta t$ with nanosecond resolution
- **Temperature sensor (NTC thermistor)** — compensates for the speed-of-sound variation
- **Microcontroller / signal processor** — computes distance, applies filtering, and outputs 4–20 mA / HART / Modbus

### ⚙️ Working Principle
1. The microcontroller triggers a **short burst** (typically 5–20 cycles) of the resonant frequency to the piezo element.
2. The piezo disk **deforms and produces a pressure wave** in the air/gas above the medium.
3. The wave **propagates** at the speed of sound, hits the material surface, and **reflects** (specular or diffuse).
4. The same (or a parallel) piezo element detects the returning **echo**.
5. The round-trip time $\Delta t$ is measured. The distance is computed as:

$$D = \frac{v_{sound} \times \Delta t}{2}$$

The **factor of 2** is critical — the wave travels *down* to the surface and *back up* to the sensor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to divide by 2! The time-of-flight is for the *round trip*, so the actual one-way distance is half of $v \cdot \Delta t$.

### 🌍 Engineering Applications
- Water and wastewater treatment tanks
- Chemical storage vessels (corrosive media safe due to non-contact)
- Bulk solids (grains, cement, plastic pellets)
- Open channel flow measurement (with a flume or weir)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Microwave Level Switch — Theoretical Depth

### 2.1.1 The Resonator Model
A coaxial resonator can be modeled as an **LC tank** at microwave frequencies. The probe tip, with its surrounding medium, forms a **parasitic capacitance** $C_m$ in parallel with the inductance $L_p$ of the probe.

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p \cdot C_m}}$$

When the medium changes (air $\rightarrow$ liquid), $C_m$ changes proportionally to $\varepsilon_r$, shifting $f_{res}$. The detector circuit monitors this shift, and a threshold comparator decides the switching state.

### 2.1.2 Penetration Depth (Skin Depth)
Microwaves do not penetrate deep into conductive media. The **skin depth** $\delta$ (the distance at which amplitude decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$) is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

where:
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ — angular frequency
- $\mu$ — permeability of the medium
- $\sigma$ — electrical conductivity

For water (with $\sigma \approx 0.05$ S/m at 5.8 GHz), $\delta$ is only a few millimeters — meaning the microwave field is **extremely sensitive to the surface layer**.

### 2.1.3 Why Two ISM Frequencies?

| Frequency | Wavelength | Beam Spread | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **5.8 GHz** | ~52 mm | Wider | Long-range, bulk solids, dusty environments |
| **24 GHz** | ~12.5 mm | Narrow | Small vessels, narrow nozzles, hygienic applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **Beam Divergence** is given by $\theta \approx \lambda / D$, where $D$ is the antenna diameter. Higher frequency = smaller $\lambda$ = tighter beam, which is better for avoiding false echoes from tank walls.

---

## 2.2 Optical Level Sensor — Theoretical Depth

### 2.2.1 Snell's Law
$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

The critical angle $\theta_c$ for total internal reflection occurs when $\theta_2 = 90°$:

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) \quad \text{(where } n_1 > n_2\text{)}$$

### 2.2.2 Fresnel Reflection Coefficient
The fraction of light reflected at the boundary is given by the **Fresnel equations**. For normal incidence:

$$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

For glass ($n_1 = 1.5$) to air ($n_2 = 1.0$):

$$R_{air} = \left(\frac{1.5 - 1.0}{1.5 + 1.0}\right)^2 = 0.04 = 4\%$$

For glass ($n_1 = 1.5$) to water ($n_2 = 1.333$):

$$R_{water} = \left(\frac{1.5 - 1.333}{1.5 + 1.333}\right)^2 = 0.0035 = 0.35\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains the operating principle quantitatively: in air, **4%** of the light is reflected (and with total internal reflection at the right geometry, much more); in water, only **0.35%** returns. The phototransistor sees a **>10× change** in intensity — easily detected.

### 2.2.3 Operating Conditions & Limitations
- Works best when $\vert n_{liquid} - n_{prism} \vert$ is small (i.e., prism refractive index is **not** well-matched to the medium).
- **Bubbles, foam, and high-viscosity coatings** can cause false triggers.
- **Transparent media** (e.g., clear water in a clear tube) may be hard to detect if the sensor is not properly mounted.

---

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Level Sensor — Theoretical Depth

### 2.3.1 Speed of Sound in Air
The speed of sound in air (ideal gas approximation) depends primarily on **temperature**:

$$v_{sound} = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$

Or, in a more practical form:

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T_C \quad \text{(m/s, where } T_C \text{ is in }^\circ\text{C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Compensation is Mandatory!** A 10°C error in temperature causes about a **1.8% error in distance** — unacceptable for high-accuracy applications.

### 2.3.2 Humidity, Pressure, and Gas Composition Effects
The general speed of sound in a gas:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ — ratio of specific heats ($c_p/c_v$)
- $R$ — universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $T$ — absolute temperature (K)
- $M$ — molar mass of the gas (kg/mol)

Lighter gases (e.g., hydrogen) have **higher** speed of sound; heavier gases (e.g., CO₂) have **lower** speed.

### 2.3.3 Beam Angle and Detection Cone
Ultrasonic transducers emit sound in a **cone-shaped beam**. The half-angle of the cone is approximately:

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{0.514 \cdot \lambda}{D_{transducer}}$$

So the **minimum tank diameter** to avoid false echoes from walls:

$$D_{tank,min} = 2 \cdot H \cdot \tan\alpha$$

where $H$ is the measuring distance.

### 2.3.4 Dead Zone (Blind Zone)
The transducer cannot measure distances closer than a few centimeters because the **transmit pulse "rings down"** before the receiver can detect an echo. This minimum distance is the **dead zone** (typically 0.15 m to 1.0 m, depending on frequency).

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Resonant Frequency (Microwave) | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_m}}$ | Hz; $C_m \propto \varepsilon_r$ |
| Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | meters; decreases with $\sqrt{f}$ |
| Wavelength of EM wave | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | meters; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Critical Angle (TIR) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | degrees; $n_1 > n_2$ required |
| Fresnel Reflection (Normal) | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | dimensionless; 0 to 1 |
| Snell's Law | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | dimensionless |
| Ultrasonic Distance | $D = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | meters; $\Delta t$ is round-trip |
| Speed of Sound in Air | $v = 331.4 + 0.6 \, T_C$ | m/s; $T_C$ in °C |
| Speed of Sound (General Gas) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | m/s |
| Beam Divergence (Ultrasonic) | $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{0.514 \lambda}{D_{transducer}}$ | radians; $D$ is transducer diameter |
| Time-of-Flight to Level | $L = H_{tank} - \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | meters; $L$ is liquid level |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, the symbol `$\vert$` is avoided. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value is needed. Example: $\vert n_1 - n_2 \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Matrix

| Sensor Type | Contact? | Best For | Avoid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microwave | Probes tank wall or contacts medium | Foam, dust, sticky media, bulk solids | Very low-$\varepsilon_r$ media (< 1.5) |
| Optical | Tip touches medium | Hygienic, small tanks, clear liquids | Coatings, heavy foam, opaque slurries |
| Ultrasonic | Non-contact (air gap) | Corrosive, hot, agitated surfaces | Vacuum, foam, very narrow tanks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Angle for Optical Sensor

**Given:** A glass prism has $n_1 = 1.50$. The external medium is water with $n_2 = 1.333$.

**Step 1 — Start with Snell's Law at the boundary**

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Step 2 — Set the condition for Total Internal Reflection**

Total internal reflection occurs when the refracted ray grazes the surface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$. Then $\sin\theta_2 = 1$.

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \cdot (1)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical angle**

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.333}{1.50}\right)$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.8887)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c \approx 62.7^\circ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If the internal ray strikes the glass-water interface at an angle **greater than 62.7°** (measured from the normal), total internal reflection will occur — even in water! This is why the **internal geometry of the prism is engineered** so that in air, the angle is well above $\theta_c$, but in water, the effective angle drops below $\theta_c$ due to refraction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance from Time-of-Flight

**Given:** A sensor measures a round-trip time of $\Delta t = 12.0$ ms. Ambient air temperature is $T_C = 25$ °C.

**Step 1 — Compute the speed of sound at 25°C**

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 25$$

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Express the round-trip relationship**

The acoustic wave travels a total round-trip distance of $2D$, where $D$ is the one-way distance from sensor to surface:

$$2D = v_{sound} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D$**

$$D = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numbers**

$$D = \frac{346.4 \text{ m/s} \times 12.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{2}$$

$$D = \frac{4.1568 \text{ m}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{D = 2.078 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to liquid level (optional)**

If the tank height is $H_{tank} = 3.0$ m and the sensor is mounted at the top:

$$L = H_{tank} - D = 3.0 - 2.078$$

$$\boxed{L = 0.922 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Fresnel Reflection Loss for Glass-to-Air Interface

**Given:** Prism refractive index $n_1 = 1.55$, air refractive index $n_2 = 1.00$.

**Step 1 — Apply Fresnel reflection formula (normal incidence)**

$$R = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$R = \left(\frac{1.55 - 1.00}{1.55 + 1.00}\right)^2$$

$$R = \left(\frac{0.55}{2.55}\right)^2$$

$$R = (0.2157)^2$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.0465 \text{ or } 4.65\%}$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation**

This 4.65% is only the *normal incidence* case. With total internal reflection (when the angle of incidence is greater than $\theta_c = 39.7°$), the reflection becomes **100%** — the optical sensor exploits this contrast.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Microwave Switch Resonance Shift

**Given:** A coaxial probe microwave switch has $L_p = 5.0$ nH. In air, the medium capacitance is $C_{air} = 0.50$ pF. When water ($\varepsilon_r = 80$) reaches the tip, the capacitance becomes $C_{water} = 40$ pF.

**Step 1 — Resonant frequency in air**

$$f_{air} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_{air}}}$$

$$f_{air} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.50 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$f_{air} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-21}}}$$

$$f_{air} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5.0 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{air} \approx 3.18 \text{ GHz}}$$

**Step 2 — Resonant frequency in water**

$$f_{water} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_{water}}}$$

$$f_{water} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-9} \times 40 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$f_{water} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.0 \times 10^{-19}}}$$

$$f_{water} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.47 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{water} \approx 0.356 \text{ GHz}}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency shift**

$$\Delta f = 3.18 - 0.356 \approx 2.82 \text{ GHz}$$

This enormous shift (≈ 89% change) is what the detector circuit senses.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Sensors

| Parameter | Microwave Switch | Optical Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physics** | Dielectric loading | Total internal reflection | Time-of-flight |
| **Output** | Switch (ON/OFF) | Switch (ON/OFF) | Continuous (analog/digital) |
| **Contact** | Probe tip or through-wall | Tip contact | Fully non-contact |
| **Accuracy** | ±2 to ±5 mm | ±1 mm | ±0.1% to ±0.5% of range |
| **Range** | Point-level only | Point-level only | 0.2 m to 15+ m |
| **Max Temperature** | 250°C+ (with extension) | 125°C typical | 150°C typical |
| **Max Pressure** | 100 bar | 10 bar | 1 bar (atmospheric) |
| **Hygienic?** | Yes (flush) | Yes (CIP/SIP) | Limited (air-coupled) |
| **Cost** | Medium–High | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Affected by Foam?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Affected by Vapor?** | No | Slightly | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
