# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📌 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a microwave level switch. Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A microwave level switch operates on the principle of **change in dielectric loading** of a high-frequency resonator. A Gunn oscillator generates a stable microwave signal (typically 5.8 GHz or 24 GHz) which is coupled to a coaxial probe tip. When the surrounding medium is air, the resonator operates at its nominal frequency. When a liquid or solid with a different dielectric constant reaches the probe tip, the **resonant frequency shifts** due to the change in capacitance. This shift is detected by a Schottky detector diode, and a comparator circuit switches the output relay.

**Advantages:**
1. **Unaffected by foam, dust, steam, or vapor** — these do not appreciably change the dielectric constant.
2. **Can detect through non-metallic container walls** — ideal for sealed plastic tanks.
3. No moving parts → high reliability and long life.
4. Suitable for high-temperature and high-pressure applications.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for sketch/description, 1 mark for principle, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between point-level and continuous-level measurement. Name one sensor each for these two categories.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Point-Level | Continuous-Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Detects *whether* material is present at a specific height | Measures *exact height* of the material surface |
| **Output** | Discrete (ON/OFF, relay) | Analog (4–20 mA) or digital (HART) |
| **Use** | High-level alarms, pump control | Inventory management, dosing |
| **Examples** | Float switch, **Optical sensor**, **Microwave switch** | **Ultrasonic sensor**, Radar, Capacitance probe |

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples and distinction.)*

---

## 📌 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **optical level sensor** with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for the critical angle and discuss how the refractive indices of the prism and medium affect the switching threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **ultrasonic level sensor**. Derive the relationship between the measured time-of-flight and the distance to the material surface. Discuss the role of temperature compensation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Construction:**
An optical level sensor consists of an **infrared LED (emitter)**, a **phototransistor (receiver)**, and a **glass/polymer prism** at the tip. The LED and phototransistor are mounted at fixed angles such that light from the LED strikes the inner face of the prism and is normally totally internally reflected toward the phototransistor. The whole assembly is housed in a stainless-steel body with electrical connections at the top.

**Working:**
- When the prism tip is in **air**: The refractive index of the prism ($n_1 \approx 1.5$) is much larger than that of air ($n_2 = 1.0$). Light from the LED strikes the prism-air interface at an angle greater than the **critical angle**, and **total internal reflection (TIR)** occurs. The reflected light falls on the phototransistor, which is strongly illuminated → the sensor indicates "no liquid."
- When the prism tip is immersed in **liquid** (e.g., water, $n_2 = 1.333$): The refractive indices of prism and liquid are closer. The light now **refracts out** of the prism into the liquid. Very little light returns to the phototransistor → the sensor indicates "liquid present."

**Derivation of Critical Angle:**
Starting from Snell's Law at the prism-medium interface:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

For TIR, the refracted ray grazes the interface: $\theta_2 = 90°$, so $\sin\theta_2 = 1$.

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)}$$

For glass ($n_1 = 1.5$) and water ($n_2 = 1.333$):

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.889) \approx 62.7°$$

**Effect of Refractive Indices:**
- If $n_{liquid}$ is **close to $n_{prism}$**, the critical angle is large, and the TIR condition is easily violated → the sensor reliably detects the liquid.
- If $n_{liquid}$ is **very different from $n_{prism}$** (e.g., air), TIR is strong, and the phototransistor receives a strong signal.
- Choosing a prism with $n_{prism} \approx \sqrt{2} \cdot n_{liquid}$ optimizes the switching contrast.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for discussion.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Block Diagram:**
```
Microcontroller → Driver → Piezo Transmitter (Pulse)
                                  ↓
                          Material Surface
                                  ↑ (echo)
Piezo Receiver → Amplifier → TDC → Microcontroller
                ↑ Temperature Sensor (NTC)
                          ↓
              4–20 mA / HART Output
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The microcontroller generates a trigger pulse every measurement cycle (e.g., every 1 second).
2. The driver circuit excites the **piezoelectric transducer** with a short burst of high-frequency voltage (typically 10–20 cycles of 40 kHz, 75 kHz, or 125 kHz).
3. The piezo element **converts electrical energy into mechanical vibration**, producing an ultrasonic pressure wave in the air column above the medium.
4. The wave propagates at the **speed of sound**, strikes the material surface, and **reflects**.
5. The same (or a parallel) piezo element **receives the echo** and converts it back into a small AC voltage.
6. A **Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC)** measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ with nanosecond resolution.
7. The microcontroller computes distance using $D = v \cdot \Delta t / 2$ and outputs the result as 4–20 mA, HART, or Modbus.

**Derivation of Distance Equation:**
Let $D$ = one-way distance from sensor to surface, $v$ = speed of sound, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time.
Total path length of the wave = $2D$ (down and back).

$$2D = v \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

The liquid level $L$ in a tank of total height $H$ (sensor mounted at top):

$$L = H - D = H - \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Role of Temperature Compensation:**
The speed of sound in air is given by:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \, T_C \quad \text{m/s}$$

A 10°C error produces ≈ 1.8% error in measured distance. For a 10 m range, this is a 18 cm error — unacceptable for precision. The integrated NTC thermistor measures air temperature, and the microcontroller corrects $v$ before computing $D$. Some advanced sensors also compensate for humidity.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for temperature compensation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the principle of operation of a **microwave level switch** with a neat block diagram. Explain why the dielectric constant of the medium is the key parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of a water tank of height **4.0 m**. The measured round-trip time is **22.0 ms**. The ambient temperature is **30°C**. Calculate the water level in the tank. State one assumption. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Block Diagram:**
```
Power Supply → Oscillator (Gunn / DRO) → Resonator Probe
                                              ↓
                                    Process Medium (Dielectric)
                                              ↓
                                    Schottky Detector Diode
                                              ↓
                                    Comparator → Output Relay
```

**Principle of Operation:**
A microwave level switch uses a **high-frequency electromagnetic field** generated at the tip of a probe by an oscillator (typically 5.8 GHz or 24 GHz, both in the ISM band). The probe, together with the surrounding medium, forms a **resonant circuit** whose resonant frequency depends on the medium's dielectric constant.

- In **air** ($\varepsilon_r = 1$), the resonance is at its nominal frequency.
- When a **liquid, slurry, or solid** with $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.5$ reaches the probe tip, the increased capacitance shifts the resonance — this shift is detected by a Schottky diode and converted to a switching output.

**Why Dielectric Constant is the Key Parameter:**
The capacitance of the probe tip with the surrounding medium is:

$$C_m = \varepsilon_r \cdot C_0$$

where $C_0$ is the geometric capacitance (with vacuum). Since the resonant frequency depends on $\sqrt{C_m}$, any change in $\varepsilon_r$ directly shifts $f_{res}$. Water ($\varepsilon_r = 80$) gives a capacitance 80× higher than air, producing a very strong, easily detected signal. This is why **microwave switches are insensitive to foam, dust, vapor, and turbulence** — these do not change $\varepsilon_r$ significantly.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for block diagram and principle, 2 marks for dielectric role, 2 marks for advantages and limitations.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- Tank height, $H = 4.0$ m
- Round-trip time, $\Delta t = 22.0$ ms = $22.0 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- Ambient temperature, $T_C = 30°C$

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 30°C**

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 331.4 + 18.0 = 349.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — One-way distance from sensor to water surface**

$$D = \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{349.4 \times 22.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$D = \frac{7.6868}{2} = 3.843 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Water level in the tank**

Since the sensor is mounted at the top, the **empty space above the water** = $D = 3.843$ m. Therefore:

$$L = H - D = 4.0 - 3.843$$

$$\boxed{L = 0.157 \text{ m} \approx 15.7 \text{ cm}}$$

**Assumption:** The speed of sound in the air above the water is constant at 349.4 m/s throughout the measurement path (uniform temperature, dry air, no significant humidity or pressure variations). Also, the surface is assumed to be flat and perpendicular to the sensor axis for a strong echo.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for speed of sound, 2 marks for distance, 2 marks for level, 1 mark for assumption.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ultrasonic distance formula → mark deduction of 1–2.
> 2. **Using 330 m/s for speed of sound** without temperature correction → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Snell's Law variables** ($n_1$ and $n_2$ swapped) → entire critical-angle derivation is marked wrong.
> 4. **Calling microwave sensors "continuous level"** sensors in theory questions → incorrect categorization; they are point-level switches.
> 5. **Not specifying the frequency (5.8 / 24 GHz)** in microwave questions → KTU examiners expect this.
> 6. **Mixing up Fresnel reflection (normal incidence) with TIR** — they are different physical regimes; clarify which applies.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🔑 Microwave Level Switches
- ✅ Use **high-frequency EM field (5.8 / 24 GHz)** at probe tip.
- ✅ Detect change in **dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r$)** of surrounding medium.
- ✅ Output is **discrete (ON/OFF switch)** — point-level only.
- ✅ Insensitive to **foam, dust, vapor, turbulence**.
- ✅ Can work through **non-metallic walls**.
- ✅ Key formula: $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_p C_m}}$, where $C_m \propto \varepsilon_r$.

### 🔑 Optical Level Sensors
- ✅ Based on **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of IR light in a glass/polymer prism.
- ✅ TIR occurs in **air**, breaks down in **liquid** → switching.
- ✅ Formula: $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$.
- ✅ Fresnel reflection at normal incidence: $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$.
- ✅ **Hygienic, no moving parts, CIP/SIP compatible**.
- ✅ Affected by **coatings, bubbles, foam**.

### 🔑 Ultrasonic Level Sensors
- ✅ Based on **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** of acoustic waves.
- ✅ Frequency range: **20–200 kHz** (industrial: 40, 75, 125 kHz).
- ✅ Distance: $D = \dfrac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ — **don't forget /2!**
- ✅ Speed of sound: $v = 331.4 + 0.6 \, T_C$ m/s.
- ✅ **Temperature compensation is mandatory** for accuracy.
- ✅ **Non-contact**, ideal for corrosive, hot, or agitated surfaces.
- ✅ Affected by **foam, vapor, vacuum, narrow tanks**.
- ✅ **Dead zone** (typically 0.15–1.0 m) is a measurement limitation.

### 🆚 Quick Comparison Mnemonic
> **"M-O-U" → Microwave, Optical, Ultrasonic.**
> - **M** = **M**edium's dielectric (microwave)
> - **O** = **O**ptical reflection/refraction (optical)
> - **U** = **U**ltrasonic pulse timing (ultrasonic)

### ⚠️ Key Engineering Trade-offs
- **Contact vs. non-contact** → Optical/Microwave contact; Ultrasonic non-contact.
- **Point vs. continuous** → Microwave/Optical are point-level; Ultrasonic is continuous.
- **Hygienic vs. industrial** → Optical for pharma/food; Microwave/Ultrasonic for industrial.
- **Accuracy vs. cost** → Ultrasonic gives continuous data; Optical is cheap and reliable for switching.

---

## 📚 KTU 2024 SCHEME — BLOOM'S TAXONOMY MAPPING

| Question Type | Bloom's Level | Course Outcome (CO) |
|---|---|---|
| Define Microwave / Optical / Ultrasonic sensors | **L1 – Remember** | CO1 (Fundamentals) |
| Explain working principle with diagrams | **L2 – Understand** | CO2 (Working Knowledge) |
| Derive formulas (critical angle, distance, Fresnel) | **L3 – Apply** | CO3 (Analytical Skills) |
| Compare sensors and justify selection | **L4 – Analyze** | CO4 (Design Thinking) |
| Calculate water level from time-of-flight | **L5 – Evaluate** | CO5 (Problem Solving) |

> **End of Module 3 — Microwave, Optical, and Ultrasonic Level Sensors**

topics.title: Pyrometers - Optical, Radiation Pyrometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pyrometers - Optical, Radiation Pyrometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pyrometer?

A **Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measurement instrument used to measure very high temperatures (typically above 600 °C, where conventional thermometers like thermocouples, RTDs, or liquid-in-glass thermometers fail due to physical contact limitations, material degradation, or range constraints). Pyrometers infer the temperature of a body by analyzing the **thermal radiation** emitted by the object, exploiting fundamental laws of blackbody radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> *A pyrometer is a device used for measuring the temperature of an object without making physical contact with it, by measuring the thermal radiation (visible, infrared, or total spectrum) emitted from the object's surface, governed by Planck's, Wien's, and Stefan-Boltzmann laws of blackbody radiation.*

Pyrometers are broadly classified as:
1. **Optical Pyrometers** (Monochromatic / Brightness Pyrometers) → Measure brightness at a single wavelength.
2. **Radiation Pyrometers** (Total Radiation Pyrometers) → Measure total emitted radiation over all wavelengths.
3. **Two-Color / Ratio Pyrometers** (Color Pyrometers) → Measure ratio of intensities at two wavelengths.
4. **Infrared Pyrometers** → Operate in the IR band using photodetectors.

For this module, we study **Optical** and **Radiation** Pyrometers in detail.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Beginner-Friendly)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Candle and the Sun" Rule:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a room. You can still tell whether someone holds a candle or a 100-Watt halogen lamp near your hand. Your skin senses the **intensity of heat (radiation)** falling on it. A pyrometer works exactly like that — it does **not touch** the object. Instead, it "feels" the invisible infrared/visible rays coming from the object and converts them into a temperature reading. Hotter the object → Brighter and more intense the radiation → Higher the indicated temperature.

| Object Emitting Radiation | What Pyrometer "Sees" |
|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Candle flame (~1500 K) | Faint red glow |
| Iron rod heated red (~900 K) | Dim red |
| Sun's surface (~5800 K) | Brilliant white |
| Furnace inside (~1800 K) | Bright orange/yellow |

This relationship between **color (wavelength)** and **temperature** is the fundamental basis of pyrometry.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used in Pyrometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants (Must be Memorized for KTU 2024):**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Wien's Displacement Constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$
> - **Second Radiation Constant (Planck's):** $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m·K} = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$
> - **First Radiation Constant (Planck's):** $c_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16} \; \text{W·m}^2$

---

### 1.4 Why Pyrometers? (Engineering Necessity)

- **Contact-based sensors fail** beyond ~1500 °C due to melting, oxidation, or chemical reaction.
- **Moving targets** (molten metal in tundish, rotating kilns, turbine blades) cannot be touched.
- **Hazardous environments** (nuclear reactors, blast furnaces) require remote sensing.
- **Fast response** required in industrial process control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The upper limit of measurement by optical and radiation pyrometers is theoretically **unbounded** (limited only by the source intensity and detector sensitivity). Practically, they cover **600 °C to 6000 °C** in industrial applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Theory — Blackbody Radiation

Any object above **0 K** emits electromagnetic radiation. The theoretical reference object — a **blackbody** — absorbs and re-emits all incident radiation. Its emission properties are governed by three foundational laws that the student must know verbatim.

#### 2.1.1 Planck's Law (Spectral Radiance)

The spectral radiance $L_\lambda$ of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$:

$$L_\lambda = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}$$

- At short wavelengths or high $T$, the term $(e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1) \approx e^{c_2/\lambda T}$, simplifying to **Wien's approximation**.
- This approximation is valid for $\lambda T < 3000 \; \mu\text{m·K}$ and is the basis of **optical pyrometers**.

#### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which maximum radiation occurs shifts inversely with temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** As the temperature rises, the peak emission shifts to shorter (bluer) wavelengths. This explains why iron glows red first (~900 K) and turns white (~1500 K).

#### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Radiation)

The total energy emitted per unit area per unit time across **all wavelengths**:

$$E = \sigma T^4$$

- $E$: Total emissive power (W/m²)
- $\sigma$: Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **$5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$**
- $T$: Absolute temperature (K)

This is the **bedrock of the Radiation Pyrometer**.

---

### 2.2 PART A — OPTICAL PYROMETER (Disappearing Filament Type)

#### 2.2.1 Definition

An **Optical Pyrometer** is a non-contact device that determines the temperature of a hot body by **visually comparing the monochromatic brightness** of radiation emitted by the body with that of a calibrated internal reference source (a tungsten filament lamp) at a single, narrow wavelength band (typically $\lambda = 0.65 \; \mu\text{m}$, the red region).

#### 2.2.2 Working Principle — "Disappearing Filament"

1. The hot target's image is focused onto a **tungsten filament** inside the pyrometer.
2. Current through the filament is varied using a rheostat until the filament's **brightness exactly matches** that of the target's image.
3. At the matching point, the **filament outline "disappears"** against the target's background.
4. The filament current is then a direct measure of the **brightness temperature** $T_s$.

#### 2.2.3 Constructional Details

- **Objective Lens:** Forms the image of the hot object onto the filament plane.
- **Tungsten Filament:** Calibrated reference source inside a sealed glass envelope.
- **Eye-piece / Monochromatic Filter:** Red filter (λ ≈ 0.65 μm) restricts the comparison to monochromatic brightness.
- **Rheostat + Ammeter (or calibrated dial):** Varies filament current; the dial reads temperature directly.
- **Absorbing Screens (for high range):** Used to extend the upper limit by reducing the apparent brightness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration Rule:** Optical pyrometers are calibrated against a **blackbody furnace**, since the calibration curve depends on the emissivity of the reference. Real (non-black) bodies give a **brightness temperature $T_s$**, which is always **less than** the true temperature $T$.

#### 2.2.4 Mathematical Foundation of Optical Pyrometers

Using Wien's approximation, the monochromatic intensity at wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$I_\lambda = c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/\lambda T}$$

For a non-black (real) body with spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$:

$$I_\lambda^{(\text{real})} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/\lambda T}$$

The brightness temperature $T_s$ is defined as the temperature a blackbody would have to produce the **same brightness**. Equating:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot e^{-c_2/\lambda T} = e^{-c_2/\lambda T_s}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$$

This is the **fundamental equation of the optical pyrometer** in KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 2.3 PART B — RADIATION PYROMETER (Total Radiation Type)

#### 2.3.1 Definition

A **Radiation Pyrometer** measures the **total thermal radiation** emitted by a hot body over a broad wavelength band and infers temperature using the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** ($E = \sigma T^4$). It does not rely on the eye — instead, a **thermopile** acts as the detector.

#### 2.3.2 Working Principle

1. Radiation from the hot target falls on a concave mirror (or lens).
2. The mirror focuses the **total radiation** onto a sensitive detector — typically a **thermopile** (multiple thermocouples in series to amplify the millivolt signal).
3. The thermopile generates an EMF proportional to the incident radiation.
4. Since $E \propto T^4$, the EMF is calibrated directly in terms of temperature.

#### 2.3.3 Constructional Details

- **Concave Mirror (or Plano-convex Lens):** Collects and focuses radiation from the target.
- **Aperture Diaphragm:** Restricts field of view; controls target image size.
- **Thermopile / Radiation Detector:** Series of thermocouples (often Sb-Bi or Chromel-Constantan) producing EMF in mV.
- **Cold-junction Compensation** circuit and **Indicating meter** (calibrated in °C or K).
- **Eyepiece / Sighting telescope:** Aligns the pyrometer at the target.

#### 2.3.4 Mathematical Foundation of Radiation Pyrometers

For a blackbody, the total energy is:

$$E = \sigma T^4$$

For a real body with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$E_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

The **radiation temperature $T_r$** is the temperature a blackbody would have to give the same total radiation:

$$\varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4 = \sigma T_r^4$$

$$T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of the radiation pyrometer** in KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of formulas that recur in KTU 2024 ESE (End-Semester Examination) Part B questions for Module 3.**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|----------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann total radiation | $E$: W/m², $T$: K | All blackbody radiation |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$ | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max}$: m, $T$: K | Blackbody only |
| 3 | $I_\lambda = c_1 \lambda^{-5} e^{-c_2/\lambda T}$ | Wien's approximation to Planck's law | $I_\lambda$: W/m²·m, $T$: K | $\lambda T < 3000 \; \mu\text{m·K}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_s} = \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$ | Brightness-True Temp relation (Optical) | $T, T_s$: K, $\lambda$: m | Optical Pyrometer |
| 5 | $T = T_r \cdot \varepsilon^{-1/4}$ | Radiation-True Temp relation (Radiation) | $T, T_r$: K | Radiation Pyrometer |
| 6 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Total radiation of real (gray) body | $E$: W/m² | Radiation Pyrometer |
| 7 | $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$ | Second Planck constant | — | All radiation problems |
| 8 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | — | All radiation problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Steel industry:** Continuous casting tundish temperature (1600 °C) — measured by **two-color** or **radiation pyrometers** to avoid slag interference.
> - **Cement kilns:** Burning zone (~1450 °C) — **optical pyrometers** with sighting telescopes.
> - **Glass furnaces:** Forehearth and feeder temperature — **radiation pyrometers** with thermopile detectors.
> - **Power plants:** Boiler tube surface temperature — **infrared pyrometers** (modern extension of radiation type).

---

### 2.5 Step-by-Step Problem-Solving Strategy (For KTU Numerical Type)

1. **Identify** the type of pyrometer from the problem statement (optical ⇒ $\lambda$-dependent; radiation ⇒ $T^4$-dependent).
2. **Extract** the given emissivity $\varepsilon$ or $\varepsilon_\lambda$ and reference temperature ($T_s$ or $T_r$).
3. **Select** the appropriate governing equation from the cheat sheet.
4. **Substitute** with units (convert °C → K by adding 273.15; convert μm → m).
5. **Solve** with scientific-calculator accuracy; typically results are in Kelvin.
6. **Convert** back to °C only if the question demands it.

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Brightness Temperature Relation for Optical Pyrometer

**Goal:** Starting from Planck's Law, derive the relation connecting true temperature $T$, brightness temperature $T_s$, spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$, and effective wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Write the Planck-Wien expression for a real body:**

The spectral radiant exitance (monochromatic intensity) of a real body at wavelength $\lambda$ and true temperature $T$ is given by Wien's approximation:

$$I_\lambda^{(\text{real})} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T)}$$

Here, $\varepsilon_\lambda$ accounts for the fact that real bodies are not perfect blackbodies; the term $c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T)}$ is the ideal blackbody intensity from Planck-Wien's law.

**Step 2 — Define brightness temperature $T_s$:**

By definition, the brightness temperature $T_s$ is the temperature at which a **blackbody** would emit radiation of the **same monochromatic intensity** as observed from the real body:

$$I_\lambda^{(\text{blackbody at } T_s)} = c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T_s)}$$

This is the same form as the ideal Planck-Wien law, evaluated at the brightness temperature $T_s$.

**Step 3 — Equate the two intensities (matching condition):**

At the matching point in the disappearing-filament optical pyrometer, the brightnesses are equal:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T)} = c_1 \lambda^{-5} \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T_s)}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $c_1 \lambda^{-5}$:**

The pre-exponential factor $c_1 \lambda^{-5}$ is identical on both sides and therefore cancels out:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \cdot e^{-c_2/(\lambda T)} = e^{-c_2/(\lambda T_s)}$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the emissivity term:**

Divide both sides by $e^{-c_2/(\lambda T)}$:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = e^{-c_2/(\lambda T_s)} \cdot e^{+c_2/(\lambda T)}$$

Rearranging using the property $a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}$:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = e^{\frac{c_2}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_s}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the natural logarithm on both sides:**

Taking $\ln$ of both sides yields a linear expression in reciprocal temperature:

$$\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda) = \frac{c_2}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_s}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the true temperature $T$:**

Re-arranging to express $1/T$:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for converting brightness temperature to true temperature in an optical pyrometer.

**Key observation:** Since $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$ for non-blackbodies, $\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda) < 0$, hence $1/T < 1/T_s$, implying $T > T_s$. Therefore, **true temperature is always higher than brightness temperature**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Radiation Temperature Relation for Radiation Pyrometer

**Goal:** Starting from Stefan-Boltzmann law, derive the relation between true temperature $T$, radiation temperature $T_r$, and total emissivity $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Stefan-Boltzmann law for a real (gray) body:**

A real body with total hemispherical emissivity $\varepsilon$ emits total power per unit area:

$$E_{\text{real}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4$$

The factor $\varepsilon < 1$ is a wavelength-averaged value, in contrast to the spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ used in the optical case.

**Step 2 — Define radiation temperature $T_r$:**

The radiation temperature $T_r$ is the temperature at which a **blackbody** emits the same total power as the real body at temperature $T$:

$$E_{\text{blackbody at } T_r} = \sigma T_r^4$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$\varepsilon \cdot \sigma T^4 = \sigma T_r^4$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\sigma$ on both sides:**

$$\varepsilon \cdot T^4 = T_r^4$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T$ in terms of $T_r$:**

$$T^4 = \frac{T_r^4}{\varepsilon}$$

Taking the fourth root on both sides:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for radiation pyrometers.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> *The radiation temperature of a furnace wall is measured as 1450 K. The total emissivity of the wall is 0.7. Calculate the true temperature.*

**Given:** $T_r = 1450 \; \text{K}$, $\varepsilon = 0.7$
**Find:** $T$ (true temperature)

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}} = \frac{1450}{(0.7)^{0.25}}$$

Compute $(0.7)^{0.25}$:

$$(0.7)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(0.7)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.3567)} = e^{-0.0892} = 0.9147$$

Substitute:

$$T = \frac{1450}{0.9147} = 1585.2 \; \text{K}$$

Convert to °C: $T = 1585.2 - 273.15 = 1312.05 \; °\text{C}$

**Answer:** True temperature = **1585.2 K (1312 °C)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Wien's Displacement Law (Quick Recap)

**Goal:** Differentiate Planck's spectral distribution with respect to $\lambda$ and equate to zero to find the peak.

**Step 1 — Planck's distribution:**

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{c_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\lambda$ and set to zero:**

After long algebraic manipulation (admitted in textbooks), the condition for maximum is:

$$\frac{c_2}{\lambda_{\max} T} = 5 \left(1 - e^{-c_2/(\lambda_{\max} T)}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical solution:**

Let $x = c_2/(\lambda_{\max} T)$. The transcendental equation gives $x \approx 4.9651$, hence:

$$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{c_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}}$$

**Numerical application:**

> *The peak emission from a furnace occurs at 1.7 μm. Find the furnace temperature.*

$$T = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-6}}{1.7 \times 10^{-6}} = 1704.7 \; \text{K} \approx 1431.6 \; °\text{C}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Optical vs Radiation)

| Parameter | Optical Pyrometer | Radiation Pyrometer |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Law Used** | Planck-Wien (monochromatic) | Stefan-Boltzmann (total) |
| **Wavelength Band** | Narrow (single λ, e.g. red filter 0.65 μm) | Broad (entire spectrum) |
| **Reference** | Human eye + comparison filament | Thermopile / detector |
| **Measured Quantity** | Brightness temperature $T_s$ | Radiation temperature $T_r$ |
| **Conversion to True T** | $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2}\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$ | $T = T_r \, \varepsilon^{-1/4}$ |
| **Effect of Emissivity** | Logarithmic correction | Power-law correction |
| **Range** | 700 °C – 6000 °C | 600 °C – 3000 °C |
| **Calibration Source** | Blackbody furnace (tungsten strip lamp) | Blackbody furnace |
| **Advantage** | No detector drift, eye is stable | Works in darkness, no eye needed |
| **Limitation** | Needs skilled observer; subjective | Strong dependence on $\varepsilon$ |
| **Sighting** | Telescope with red filter | Mirror + thermopile housing |

## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Optical Pyrometer (Disappearing Filament Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Target Furnace Emits Radiation] --> B[Objective Lens Forms Image of Target]
    B --> C[Image Formed on Tungsten Filament Plane]
    D[Battery Supply] --> E[Rheostat Variable Resistor]
    E --> F[Tungsten Filament Reference Source]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Red Filter at Lambda equals 0.65 micrometer]
    G --> H[Eye Piece Observer Sees Fused Image]
    H --> I{Disappearance of Filament Outline}
    I -- Yes --> J[Ammeter or Calibrated Dial Reads Brightness Temperature Ts]
    I -- No --> K[Adjust Rheostat Until Match]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Apply Correction Equation to get True Temperature T]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The hot target emits radiation → Objective lens focuses it onto the filament plane → Current-controlled tungsten filament provides a reference glow → Red filter ensures monochromatic comparison → Observer looks through eyepiece → Adjusts rheostat until the filament "disappears" → Calibrated dial reads $T_s$ → Apply the brightness-to-true-temperature correction.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Radiation Pyrometer (Total Radiation Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Hot Target Furnace Surface] --> B1[Concave Mirror Collects Total Radiation]
    B1 --> C1[Mirror Focuses Radiation on Thermopile Hot Junction]
    D1[Ambient Reference Cold Junction] --> C1
    C1 --> E1[Thermopile Generates EMF proportional to T power 4]
    E1 --> F1[Cold Junction Compensation Circuit]
    F1 --> G1[Amplifier and Linearizer]
    G1 --> H1[Calibrated Indicator reads Radiation Temperature Tr in degree C]
    H1 --> I1[Apply Correction T equals Tr divided by epsilon to the power 1 by 4]
    I1 --> J1[Display True Temperature T]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Hot target → Concave mirror collects ALL emitted radiation → Focused onto thermopile hot junction → Temperature difference with cold junction produces mV EMF → Compensated, amplified, linearized → Indicator reads $T_r$ → Correction equation yields true $T$.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of Both Pyrometers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpticalChain[Optical Pyrometer Signal Chain]
        O1[Target Image] --> O2[Objective Lens]
        O2 --> O3[Tungsten Filament at Image Plane]
        O3 --> O4[Red Filter at 0.65 micrometer]
        O4 --> O5[Human Eye Observer]
        O5 --> O6[Visual Disappearance Calibration]
    end
    subgraph RadiationChain[Radiation Pyrometer Signal Chain]
        R1[Total Radiation from Target] --> R2[Concave Mirror]
        R2 --> R3[Thermopile Detector]
        R3 --> R4[EMF Signal mV]
        R4 --> R5[Signal Conditioner]
        R5 --> R6[Indicator in degree C]
    end
    OpticalChain --> FinalEq1[Apply 1 divided by T equals 1 divided by Ts plus lambda over c2 times ln epsilon lambda]
    RadiationChain --> FinalEq2[Apply T equals Tr divided by epsilon to the power 1 by 4]
    FinalEq1 --> Result[Display True Temperature T]
    FinalEq2 --> Result
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — KTU Problem-Solving Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Given: Temperature and Emissivity Problem] --> P2{Is a specific wavelength lambda given?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Use Optical Pyrometer Equation]
    P2 -- No --> P4[Use Radiation Pyrometer Equation]
    P3 --> P5[Compute 1 over T equals 1 over Ts plus lambda over c2 times ln epsilon lambda]
    P4 --> P6[Compute T equals Tr divided by epsilon to the power 1 by 4]
    P5 --> P7[Invert reciprocal to get T in Kelvin]
    P6 --> P8[Take fourth root to get T in Kelvin]
    P7 --> P9[Convert K to degree C if asked by subtracting 273.15]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Final Answer with Units]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always carry units in your final substitution step. Convert $\mu\text{m} \to \text{m}$ (divide by $10^6$) before using $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$. This is where students most often commit silly errors.

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define an **Optical Pyrometer**. State the principle on which it works. Why is a red filter used in its construction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **optical pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument that determines the temperature of a hot body by visually comparing the **monochromatic brightness** of the radiation emitted by the body with that of a calibrated reference source (tungsten filament) at a single effective wavelength. It is also called a **disappearing filament pyrometer**.

**Principle:** The intensity of monochromatic radiation from a body is a function of its temperature as per **Wien's approximation to Planck's law**. The filament current is adjusted until its brightness matches the target's brightness, at which point the filament outline "disappears".

**Role of red filter (λ ≈ 0.65 μm):**
1. It restricts the comparison to a **single, narrow wavelength band**, making the comparison essentially monochromatic.
2. The eye has maximum sensitivity in the red region, so the match is more accurate.
3. The filter enables the **Wien approximation** ($\lambda T < 3000 \; \mu\text{m·K}$) to be valid at lower temperatures, extending the useful range.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **brightness temperature** and **true temperature**. How is the correction factor applied in an optical pyrometer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Brightness Temperature ($T_s$) | True Temperature ($T$) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Definition | Temperature of a blackbody emitting the same monochromatic intensity as the real body | The actual thermodynamic temperature of the real body |
| Measured by | Optical pyrometer directly (via dial reading) | Computed via correction |
| Numerical value | Always **less than** $T$ for $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$ | The actual value |

**Correction Equation (Optical Pyrometer):**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$$

Since emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$ for a real body, $\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda) < 0$, hence the correction term is negative, and the inverse $1/T$ is smaller than $1/T_s$. This gives $T > T_s$, the true temperature.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a disappearing filament optical pyrometer**. (7 marks)
> *(b)* The brightness temperature of a molten metal surface, as measured by an optical pyrometer set at an effective wavelength of 0.65 μm, is **1450 K**. If the spectral emissivity of the metal at this wavelength is **0.65**, determine the **true temperature** of the molten metal. Take $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$. (7 marks)

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An optical pyrometer is a non-contact temperature-measuring instrument that compares the monochromatic brightness of a hot body with that of a calibrated internal reference source.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Objective lens** — focuses image of hot target onto filament plane.
- **Tungsten reference filament** — housed in a sealed glass envelope, current controlled by a rheostat.
- **Red filter (λ = 0.65 μm)** — restricts the comparison to monochromatic red radiation.
- **Eyepiece** — for visual observation of the filament-target alignment.
- **Calibrated dial** (or ammeter) — reads brightness temperature directly.
- **Battery** — supplies current to the filament.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The pyrometer is sighted on the hot target; objective lens forms its image in the plane of the tungsten filament.
2. Current through the filament is gradually increased using the rheostat; the filament glows with increasing brightness.
3. At the matching brightness, the **filament outline vanishes** ("disappears") against the background of the target image.
4. The current at this point is read on the calibrated dial, giving the **brightness temperature** $T_s$.
5. The instrument is calibrated in °C or K using a blackbody furnace as the reference standard.
6. Correction for emissivity is applied to get the **true temperature** $T$.

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $T_s = 1450 \; \text{K}$, $\lambda = 0.65 \; \mu\text{m} = 0.65 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.65$, $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$.

**Formula:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$:**

$$\ln(0.65) = -0.4308$$

**Step 2 — Compute the correction term $\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{0.01438} = 4.520 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda) = (4.520 \times 10^{-5}) \times (-0.4308) = -1.947 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $1/T$:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1450} + (-1.947 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 6.897 \times 10^{-4} - 1.947 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 6.702 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain $T$:**

$$T = \frac{1}{6.702 \times 10^{-4}} = 1492.1 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to °C:**

$$T = 1492.1 - 273.15 = 1218.9 \; °\text{C}$$

**Final Answer:** True temperature $T \approx 1492 \; \text{K} \; (1219 \; °\text{C})$.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> *(a)* Explain the **working principle of a radiation pyrometer** with a neat block diagram. State the laws of radiation governing its operation. (7 marks)
> *(b)* A radiation pyrometer measures the radiation temperature of a furnace wall as **1700 K**. If the total emissivity of the wall is **0.8**, calculate the **true temperature** of the wall. (7 marks)

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **radiation pyrometer** is a non-contact instrument that measures the **total thermal radiation** emitted by a hot body over the entire spectrum and infers temperature using the Stefan-Boltzmann Law.

**Laws of Radiation Governing Its Operation (2 marks):**
1. **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E = \sigma T^4$ — total emitted power is proportional to the fourth power of absolute temperature.
2. **Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation** — describes the spectral distribution of emitted radiation.
3. **Kirchhoff's Law** — emissivity $\varepsilon$ equals absorptivity for a body in thermal equilibrium (used to extend the law to real bodies via $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$).

**Working Principle (with Block Diagram — 4 marks):**
1. **Radiation Collection:** The hot target emits continuous-spectrum radiation. A concave mirror (or lens) collects this radiation.
2. **Focusing:** The mirror focuses the collected radiation onto a **thermopile** (series-connected thermocouples) housed at the focal point.
3. **Detection:** The hot junction of the thermopile absorbs the radiation and heats up; the cold junction remains at ambient temperature. The temperature difference generates a **thermoelectric EMF (mV)** proportional to the absorbed power.
4. **Signal Conditioning:** The EMF is amplified, cold-junction-compensated, and linearized (since $E \propto T^4$, a fourth-root linearizer is required).
5. **Display:** A calibrated indicator displays the **radiation temperature $T_r$** directly.
6. **Correction:** The true temperature is obtained by $T = T_r \cdot \varepsilon^{-1/4}$.

**Block Diagram (Text Form):**

$$\text{Hot Target} \rightarrow \text{Concave Mirror} \rightarrow \text{Thermopile} \rightarrow \text{EMF Signal} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier + CJC} \rightarrow \text{Linearizer} \rightarrow \text{Indicator in } °\text{C}$$

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $T_r = 1700 \; \text{K}$, $\varepsilon = 0.8$

**Formula:**

$$T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $\varepsilon^{1/4}$:**

$$\varepsilon^{1/4} = (0.8)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \ln(0.8)} = e^{0.25 \times (-0.2231)} = e^{-0.0558} = 0.9457$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T$:**

$$T = \frac{1700}{0.9457} = 1797.5 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to °C:**

$$T = 1797.5 - 273.15 = 1524.4 \; °\text{C}$$

**Final Answer:** True temperature $T \approx 1797.5 \; \text{K} \; (1524 \; °\text{C})$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Callouts:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster:** Failing to convert $\lambda$ from $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$ while using $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K}$ is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss** in optical pyrometer numericals. Always convert: $1 \; \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$.
> 2. **Sign Confusion on $\ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$:** Since $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$, the logarithm is **negative**, and the correction term is **subtracted** from $1/T_s$. Some students add it by mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting the 273.15 Conversion:** KTU expects answers in Kelvin unless the question specifically states "in °C". Don't lose a mark for unit conversion.
> 4. **Confusing Brightness vs Radiation Temperature:** Optical pyrometer ⇒ brightness $T_s$ with $\lambda$-dependent correction. Radiation pyrometer ⇒ radiation $T_r$ with $\varepsilon$-dependent fourth-root correction. Many students mix the two formulas.
> 5. **Missing the Physical Justification:** When asked "why use a red filter?", KTU expects a 3-part answer: monochromaticity, eye sensitivity, and validity of Wien's approximation. Skipping the third point is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Module 3 (Pyrometers):**

- **Pyrometer** = non-contact temperature sensor for high ranges (> 600 °C). Two main types studied: **Optical** (monochromatic) and **Radiation** (total).

- **Optical Pyrometer Key Points:**
  - Uses the **disappearing filament** principle.
  - Effective wavelength $\lambda = 0.65 \; \mu\text{m}$ (red filter).
  - Measures **brightness temperature** $T_s$.
  - **Correction equation:** $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(\varepsilon_\lambda)$.
  - **Always:** $T > T_s$ for $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$.

- **Radiation Pyrometer Key Points:**
  - Based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** $E = \sigma T^4$.
  - Detector: **Thermopile** (series thermocouples).
  - Measures **radiation temperature** $T_r$.
  - **Correction equation:** $T = T_r \cdot \varepsilon^{-1/4}$.
  - **Always:** $T > T_r$ for $\varepsilon < 1$.

- **Constants (memorize verbatim for KTU):**
  - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
  - $c_2 = 0.01438 \; \text{m·K} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m·K}$
  - $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$ (Wien's displacement)
  - $c_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16} \; \text{W·m}^2$ (rarely used in KTU)

- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$.

- **Wien's Approximation validity:** $\lambda T < 3000 \; \mu\text{m·K}$.

- **Errors/Disadvantages:**
  - Optical → subjective (depends on observer's eye), not usable in darkness, requires reference calibration.
  - Radiation → strong sensitivity to $\varepsilon$ and dust/fumes (water vapor, CO₂ absorb IR), lens/mirror contamination, background radiation errors.

- **Engineering Domains of Application:**
  - Steel-making (tundish, ladle, continuous casting).
  - Cement kilns.
  - Glass melting furnaces.
  - Power plant boiler tubes.
  - Aerospace (turbine blade temperature).

- **Comparisons to Remember (commonly asked as 7-mark questions):**
  - Optical: 700–6000 °C, monochromatic, manual.
  - Radiation: 600–3000 °C, broad spectrum, automated with thermopile.

- **Quick Conversion:** $T(°\text{C}) = T(\text{K}) - 273.15$.

- **Examiner's Mantra:** Show all substitution steps, **carry units**, **convert $\mu\text{m}$ to $\text{m}$**, and **state the formula before using it**. These are the three cheapest ways to gain +1 to +2 marks in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring and Recording Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measuring and Recording Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Measuring Instruments?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **measuring instrument** is a device used to determine the magnitude of a physical quantity (current, voltage, power, energy, resistance, etc.) by converting it into a directly observable form — typically a mechanical deflection on a calibrated scale.

In instrumentation engineering, every instrument is fundamentally a **transducer + signal conditioner + display/recording device**. The *measuring part* gives an *instantaneous* indication, while the *recording part* preserves the measurement as a function of time.

## 1.2 What are Recording Instruments?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A **recording instrument** is a device that continuously measures a quantity and produces a permanent, time-stamped graphical record (trace) of that measurement on paper, magnetic tape, or digital memory. Examples include **strip-chart recorders, X–Y recorders, and magnetic tape recorders**.

The two main families are:

| Family | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Indicating Instruments** | Display instantaneous value via pointer on scale | PMMC ammeter |
| **Recording Instruments** | Produce permanent trace of quantity vs. time (or vs. another variable) | Strip-chart recorder |
| **Integrating Instruments** | Totalize quantity over a period | Energy meter (kWh meter) |
| **Null-Type Instruments** | Measure by balancing unknown against a known | Potentiometer, Wheatstone bridge |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Ward

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy (Think of a hospital):**
> - A **mercury thermometer** = **Indicating instrument** (instantaneous reading of temperature).
> - An **ECG machine** = **Recording instrument** (continuously plots heart voltage vs. time).
> - A **water meter** at home = **Integrating instrument** (totals the volume used over months).
> - A **weighing balance** where you slide weights until pointer is at zero = **Null-type instrument**.
>
> Just as a doctor *records* the ECG for later diagnosis, a control engineer *records* process variables to study trends, faults, and dynamic behavior.

## 1.4 Classification of Analog Measuring Instruments

Based on the **principle of operation**, indicating instruments are classified as:

1. **PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil)** — D'Arsonval type → DC only.
2. **Moving Iron (MI)** — Attraction & Repulsion types → AC & DC.
3. **Electrodynamometer (EDM)** → AC & DC, used as wattmeter.
4. **Electrostatic** → Voltmeters for high voltage.
5. **Induction type** → AC energy meters.
6. **Rectifier type** → AC voltmeters/ammeters using PMMC + diode bridge.
7. **Thermocouple type** → High-frequency RF measurements.

## 1.5 Physical Constants Used

- Permeability of free space, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**.
- Spring constant of phosphor-bronze control spring: typically **$K' = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ Nm/rad**.
- Standard reference temperature: **$25^{\circ}\text{C}$**.
- Standard deflection angle for full scale: $\theta_{FS} = \pi/2$ **rad** $= 90^{\circ}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 D'Arsonval / PMMC Galvanometer — Operational Principle

The PMMC instrument (invented by **Jacques d'Arsonval**, 1882) is the **workhorse** of DC measurement. Its operation follows a strict, logical sequence:

### Step-by-Step Operation

1. **Magnetic field generation:** A permanent magnet (Alnico / modern rare-earth such as NdFeB) creates a **radial, uniform magnetic flux density $B$** between the pole pieces and the soft-iron cylindrical core.
2. **Coil placement:** A lightweight rectangular coil of $N$ turns, carrying current $I$, is suspended (or pivoted) in this radial field.
3. **Deflecting torque production:** Each side of the coil of effective length $l$ and width $d$ experiences a force $F = NBIl$. The two forces are opposite, creating a **deflecting torque**:
$$T_d = 2 \cdot F \cdot \frac{d}{2} = NBIl \cdot d = NBAI$$
where $A = l \cdot d$ is the coil area.
4. **Restoring torque:** A phosphor-bronze control spring supplies a **controlling torque** $T_c = K' \theta$ proportional to angular deflection $\theta$.
5. **Damping:** Eddy currents induced in the aluminum former (or a damping former) provide **electromagnetic damping**, bringing the pointer to rest quickly without oscillation.
6. **Equilibrium:** At balance, $T_d = T_c$:
$$NBAI = K'\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{NBA}{K'} \cdot I = G \cdot I$$
where $G = NBA / K'$ is the **galvanometer constant** (rad/A).

> [!NOTE]
> Because $\theta \propto I$ **linearly**, the PMMC scale is **uniform** — a major advantage for KTU problems.

## 2.2 Extension of PMMC to Ammeter — Shunt Design

The PMMC galvanometer can carry only a few mA. To convert it into an **ammeter** of range $I$, we connect a low-resistance **shunt** $R_{sh}$ in **parallel** with the meter of resistance $R_m$.

### Why a Shunt?

When the total current $I$ enters, only $I_m$ (full-scale current of galvanometer) flows through the meter; the rest $(I - I_m)$ must flow through $R_{sh}$.

**Voltage equality across parallel combination:**

$$I_m R_m = (I - I_m) R_{sh}$$

Solving for $R_{sh}$:

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \, R_m}{I - I_m} = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}$$

where $m = I / I_m$ is the **multiplying factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU problems:** Always remember that the shunt must have a **very small resistance** (milliohms) to carry the bulk of current, and it must have a **low temperature coefficient** (manganin / constantan) to minimize temperature error.

### Ayrton Shunt (Universal Shunt)

For multi-range ammeters, an **Ayrton shunt** is used — a series of resistors tapped at intermediate points. The advantage is that **the meter is never left in an open circuit when the range is switched** (important for protecting the PMMC).

## 2.3 Extension of PMMC to Voltmeter — Multiplier Design

To convert a PMMC galvanometer (full-scale voltage $V_m = I_m R_m$) into a voltmeter of range $V$, a **high-resistance multiplier** $R_s$ is connected in **series**.

**Kirchhoff's voltage law around the loop:**

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

Solving:

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \frac{V - V_m}{I_m}$$

The **total voltmeter resistance** is $R_V = R_m + R_s$.

> [!TIP]
> **Sensitivity** of a voltmeter is expressed as $\Omega/\text{V}$. A typical good voltmeter: **$20{,}000\ \Omega/\text{V}$** (PMMC) vs. **$1000\ \Omega/\text{V}$** for moving-iron.

## 2.4 AC Measurements — Rectifier Type Instruments

PMMC cannot directly measure AC because the average torque over a full cycle is **zero**. Solution: place a **diode bridge rectifier** in front of the PMMC.

For a **sinusoidal** AC current of RMS value $I_{rms}$, the rectified average is:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{2 I_m}{\pi} = 0.637 \, I_m \quad \text{and} \quad I_{rms} = 0.707 \, I_m$$

The **form factor** $K_f = I_{rms} / I_{avg} = 1.11$ (for pure sine wave).

The PMMC reads the **average** value, but the scale is **calibrated in RMS** by multiplying the average by 1.11. Hence for non-sinusoidal waveforms, significant error arises — this is a **common exam pitfall**.

## 2.5 Wattmeter — Electrodynamometer Principle

The electrodynamometer has **two coils**:
- **Current coil (CC):** low resistance, connected in series with the load — carries load current $I$.
- **Potential coil (PC):** high resistance, connected across the load — carries current $I_p \propto V$.

The instantaneous deflecting torque is proportional to the **product of currents in the two coils**:

$$T_d(t) \propto i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) = i(t) \cdot \frac{v(t)}{R_p}$$

The **average torque** over one cycle gives the **average power**:

$$T_{avg} \propto \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t)\, i(t)\, dt = P_{avg}$$

Hence:

$$P = V I \cos\phi \quad \text{(for sinusoidal)}$$

where $\phi$ is the phase angle between $V$ and $I$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deflection of PMMC | $\theta = (NBA/K') \cdot I = G \cdot I$ | $\theta$ in rad |
| 2 | Shunt resistance (Ammeter) | $R_{sh} = R_m / (m-1)$ | $m = I/I_m$ |
| 3 | Multiplier resistance (Voltmeter) | $R_s = (V/I_m) - R_m$ | Series with meter |
| 4 | Multiplying power | $m = 1 + R_m / R_{sh}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Power in DC | $P = VI = I^2 R$ | Watts |
| 6 | Power in AC (single-phase) | $P = V I \cos\phi$ | Watts |
| 7 | Energy | $E = P \cdot t = V I t \cos\phi$ | Joules / kWh |
| 8 | RMS value (sine) | $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | — |
| 9 | Average value (sine, full-wave) | $V_{avg} = 2 V_m / \pi$ | — |
| 10 | Form factor (sine) | $K_f = V_{rms}/V_{avg} = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$ | — |
| 11 | Crest factor (sine) | $K_p = V_m / V_{rms} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ | — |
| 12 | Voltmeter sensitivity | $S = R_V / V$ in $\Omega/\text{V}$ | Higher = better |
| 13 | Series ohmmeter half-scale | $R_x = R_m + R_{int}$ (mid-scale) | Where $R_{int}$ includes battery $r$ |
| 14 | CRO deflection sensitivity | $S = y / V_y$ | cm/V |
| 15 | CRO frequency measured | $f = 1 / T = (\text{timebase})^{-1}$ | Hz |

## 2.7 Recording Instruments — Why They Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Recording instruments are essential for:
> - **Process control:** Trending of temperature, pressure, flow over shifts/days.
> - **Vibration analysis:** Capturing transient mechanical events.
> - **Fault diagnosis:** Post-mortem of electrical faults via fault recorders.
> - **Scientific experiments:** Strip-chart recorders, X–Y plotters, digital data loggers.

The two most common types are:
- **Strip-chart recorder:** quantity vs. time, written on a moving paper roll.
- **X–Y recorder:** one variable plotted against another (e.g., B–H curve of a magnetic material, stress–strain curve).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$ for PMMC Ammeter

**Given:** A PMMC galvanometer with internal resistance $R_m = 100\ \Omega$ and full-scale deflection current $I_m = 1\ \text{mA}$. Required: design an ammeter to read full scale at $I = 1\ \text{A}$.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the junction:**

The incoming current $I$ splits into $I_m$ (through meter) and $I_{sh}$ (through shunt).

$$I = I_m + I_{sh}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL across the parallel combination:**

Voltage across meter = Voltage across shunt.

$$I_m \cdot R_m = I_{sh} \cdot R_{sh}$$

**Step 3 — Express $I_{sh}$ from KCL:**

$$I_{sh} = I - I_m = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $R_{sh}$:**

$$R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_m}{I_{sh}} = \frac{(0.001)(100)}{0.999}$$

$$\boxed{R_{sh} \approx 0.1001\ \Omega}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the compact formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \\
R_{sh} &= \frac{R_m}{m-1} = \frac{100}{999} \approx 0.1001\ \Omega \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multiplier Resistance $R_s$ for PMMC Voltmeter

**Given:** Same galvanometer ($R_m = 100\ \Omega$, $I_m = 1\ \text{mA}$). Required: design a voltmeter for $V = 10\ \text{V}$ full scale.

**Step 1 — Determine full-scale meter voltage:**

$$V_m = I_m \cdot R_m = (0.001)(100) = 0.1\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the series loop:**

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \frac{10}{0.001} - 100 = 10{,}000 - 100$$

$$\boxed{R_s = 9900\ \Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Total voltmeter resistance:**

$$R_V = R_m + R_s = 100 + 9900 = 10{,}000\ \Omega = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{R_V}{V} = \frac{10{,}000}{10} = 1000\ \Omega/\text{V}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Series Ohmmeter Scale Equation (Non-Linear Scale)

A series ohmmeter consists of the PMMC meter, a **battery** of EMF $E$ with internal resistance $r$, a **zero-adjust resistor** $R_0$ in series, and the unknown $R_x$ being measured.

**Step 1 — Total loop resistance when $R_x = 0$ (short circuit, zero-ohm mark):**

Maximum current must equal $I_m$:

$$I_m = \frac{E}{r + R_0 + R_m}$$

Solve for the adjustable sum:

$$r + R_0 + R_m = \frac{E}{I_m}$$

**Step 2 — Current when unknown $R_x$ is connected:**

$$I_x = \frac{E}{r + R_0 + R_m + R_x} = \frac{E}{E/I_m + R_x} = \frac{I_m}{1 + (I_m R_x / E)}$$

**Step 3 — Deflection angle:**

$$\theta_x = G \cdot I_x = \frac{G \cdot I_m}{1 + (I_m R_x / E)} = \frac{\theta_{FS}}{1 + (R_x / R_H)}$$

where $R_H = E / I_m$ is called the **half-scale resistance** (because when $R_x = R_H$, $\theta = \theta_{FS}/2$, i.e., pointer at mid-scale).

**Step 4 — Key conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\theta_x = \frac{\theta_{FS}}{1 + (R_x / R_H)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **hyperbolic** relationship — the scale is **highly non-linear**, crowded at high $R_x$ and spread out at low $R_x$. This is why ohmmeter scales read **right-to-left** (zero on the right).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Consumed in kWh

**Given:** A household consumes a constant power $P = 2\ \text{kW}$ for $t = 5$ hours.

**Step 1 — Energy in joules:**

$$E = P \cdot t = 2000\ \text{W} \times 5 \times 3600\ \text{s}$$

$$E = 2000 \times 18{,}000 = 36 \times 10^6\ \text{J} = 36\ \text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J):**

$$E = \frac{36 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 10\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Reading on the energy meter:**

If the meter constant is $K_e = 600\ \text{rev/kWh}$ (typical induction type), number of disc revolutions:

$$N = E \times K_e = 10 \times 600 = 6000\ \text{revolutions}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Wattmeter Reading on Non-Resistive Load

**Given:** $V = 230\ \text{V (rms)}$, $I = 5\ \text{A (rms)}$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging.

**Step 1 — Real power:**

$$P = VI\cos\phi = 230 \times 5 \times 0.8 = 920\ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent power:**

$$S = VI = 230 \times 5 = 1150\ \text{VA}$$

**Step 3 — Reactive power:**

$$Q = VI\sin\phi = 1150 \times 0.6 = 690\ \text{VAR}$$

**Step 4 — Power triangle check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P^2 + Q^2 &= 920^2 + 690^2 = 846{,}400 + 476{,}100 = 1{,}322{,}500 \\
S &= \sqrt{1{,}322{,}500} = 1150\ \text{VA} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — CRO Vertical Deflection Sensitivity

**Given:** CRO with vertical deflection plates of length $L = 2\ \text{cm}$, separation $d = 0.5\ \text{cm}$, accelerating voltage $V_a = 2000\ \text{V}$, distance from plate center to screen $D = 20\ \text{cm}$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of electron entering deflection region:**

$$\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = eV_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV_a}{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical displacement at the far edge of plates:**

$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{eV_y}{md} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{v}\right)^2 = \frac{V_y \cdot L^2}{4 V_a \cdot d}$$

**Step 3 — Additional displacement after plates (until screen):**

$$y_2 = D \cdot \tan\theta \approx D \cdot \theta = D \cdot \frac{V_y \cdot L}{2 V_a \cdot d}$$

**Step 4 — Total deflection on screen:**

$$Y = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{V_y L^2}{4 V_a d} + \frac{V_y L D}{2 V_a d}$$

$$\boxed{Y = \frac{V_y L}{2 V_a d}\left(\frac{L}{2} + D\right) \approx \frac{V_y L D}{2 V_a d}\ \text{(if } D \gg L)}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting numbers (using $D \gg L$ approx):**

$$Y \approx \frac{V_y \cdot (0.02) \cdot (0.20)}{2 \cdot 2000 \cdot 0.005} = \frac{V_y \cdot 0.004}{20} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \cdot V_y\ \text{(m)}$$

Deflection sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{Y}{V_y} = 0.2\ \text{mm/V} = 0.02\ \text{cm/V}$$

This sensitivity is small — hence practical CROs use **post-deflection amplification** or larger $D$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PMMC Instrument — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Source Signal: Current I] --> nodeM[Permanent Magnet: Radial Field B]
    nodeM --> nodeC[Moving Coil: N turns area A]
    nodeC --> nodeT[Deflecting Torque Td equals NBA I]
    nodeT --> nodeSP[Control Spring: Torque Tc equals K prime theta]
    nodeSP --> nodeEQ[Equilibrium Td equals Tc]
    nodeEQ --> nodeP[Pointer deflection theta]
    nodeD[Aluminum Former: Eddy Current Damping] --> nodeEQ
    nodeSC[Calibrated Scale: Uniform Linear] --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeOP[Visual Indication on Scale]
```

## 4.2 PMMC Extension to Ammeter and Voltmeter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCAMP[DC Ammeter Circuit]
        nodeA1[Load Current I] --> nodeA2[Shunt Resistor Rsh]
        nodeA1 --> nodeA3[PMMC Galvanometer Rm Im]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA4[Common Node]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4
    end

    subgraph DCVOLT[DC Voltmeter Circuit]
        nodeV1[Voltage Source V] --> nodeV2[Multiplier Resistor Rs]
        nodeV2 --> nodeV3[PMMC Galvanometer Rm Im]
        nodeV3 --> nodeV4[Common Node]
    end

    nodeA4 -. extension .-> DCVOLT
```

## 4.3 Wattmeter — Electrodynamometer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL[Load] --> nodeCC[Current Coil: Low R, in series]
    nodeCC --> nodeRT[Return to Source]
    nodeL --> nodePC1[Potential Coil Branch]
    nodePC1 --> nodeRS[Multiplier Resistor Rp]
    nodeRS --> nodePC2[Potential Coil: High R, across load]
    nodePC2 --> nodeRT
    nodeCC -. magnetic coupling .- nodePC2
    nodeCC -. produces .- nodeF1[Force F1 proportional to I]
    nodePC2 -. produces .- nodeF2[Force F2 proportional to V over Rp]
    nodeF1 --> nodeT[Average Torque proportional to VI cos phi]
    nodeF2 --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeP[Pointer Deflection]
    nodeSP[Control Spring] --> nodeP
```

## 4.4 CRO — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeIN[Input Signal Vy t] --> nodeATT[Attenuator]
    nodeATT --> nodePRE[Vertical Pre-amplifier]
    nodePRE --> nodeDEL[Delay Line]
    nodeDEL --> nodeVAMP[Vertical Output Amplifier]
    nodeVAMP --> nodeVP[Vertical Deflection Plates Y]
    nodeVP --> nodeCRT[Cathode Ray Tube Screen]

    nodeTB[Timebase Generator: Sawtooth] --> nodeHAMP[Horizontal Amplifier]
    nodeHAMP --> nodeHP[Horizontal Deflection Plates X]
    nodeHP --> nodeCRT

    nodeCRT --> nodePHOS[Phosphor Screen: Visible Trace]
    nodeZ[Z-axis: Intensity Modulation] --> nodeCRT
```

## 4.5 Strip-Chart Recorder — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIN[Input Signal from Transducer] --> nodeAMP[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    nodeAMP --> nodeFS[Feedback Servo for Self-balancing]
    nodeFS --> nodeMOT[Servo Motor]
    nodeMOT --> nodePEN[Pen Carriage on Lead Screw]
    nodePEN --> nodePAP[Moving Paper Chart]
    nodePAP --> nodeT[Synchronous Drive Motor at constant speed]
    nodeFS -. feedback .- nodeAMP
    nodePEN --> nodeOUT[Trace of Quantity versus Time]
```

## 4.6 X–Y Recorder — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeXA[X-axis Input] --> nodeXAMP[X Amplifier]
    nodeXAMP --> nodeXSERVO[X Servo]
    nodeXSERVO --> nodeXPEN[Pen moves in X]

    nodeYA[Y-axis Input] --> nodeYAMP[Y Amplifier]
    nodeYAMP --> nodeYSERVO[Y Servo]
    nodeYSERVO --> nodeYPEN[Pen moves in Y]

    nodeXPEN --> nodePEN1[Single Pen traces X versus Y curve]
    nodeYPEN --> nodePEN1
    nodePEN1 --> nodePAP[Stationary Paper]
    nodePEN1 --> nodeOUT[Plot of Y versus X]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
