# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define the term "multiplying factor" of a shunt used with a PMMC ammeter.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **multiplying factor** $m$ of a shunt is defined as the ratio of the total current $I$ to be measured to the full-scale deflection current $I_m$ of the galvanometer:
>
> $$m = \frac{I}{I_m}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless** number, always $> 1$, and is used to compute the required shunt resistance:
> $$R_{sh} = \frac{R_m}{m-1}$$
>
> A higher $m$ corresponds to a smaller shunt resistance, meaning the meter handles a larger fraction of the total current through the shunt.

*(1 mark: Definition, 1 mark: Formula, 1 mark: Significance)*

---

### **Question 2: Why is the scale of a series ohmmeter non-linear and reversed (zero on the right)?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The series ohmmeter's deflection is given by:
> $$\theta_x = \frac{\theta_{FS}}{1 + (R_x / R_H)}$$
>
> - When $R_x = 0$ (short): $\theta = \theta_{FS}$ → pointer at **full-scale (right end)** marked **0 Ω**.
> - When $R_x \to \infty$ (open): $\theta \to 0$ → pointer at **zero (left end)** marked **∞ Ω**.
>
> Hence the **zero-ohm mark is on the right** and the scale reads **right-to-left**.
> The relationship is **hyperbolic**, not linear — the scale is **highly crowded at high resistance** and spread out near zero, hence **non-linear**.

*(1 mark: equation, 1 mark: reasoning, 1 mark: scale layout)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A (a)** Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument**. Derive the equation for the deflecting torque and discuss the sources of error. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** A PMMC galvanometer has a resistance of $200\ \Omega$ and gives full-scale deflection at $50\ \mu\text{A}$. Design a multi-range DC ammeter with ranges $0–10\ \text{mA}$ and $0–100\ \text{mA}$ using an **Ayrton shunt**. Calculate all resistor values. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for A (a):**

**1. Construction (2 marks):**

- **Permanent magnet:** Alnico / Cunife / rare-earth (NdFeB) — produces radial magnetic field between curved pole pieces.
- **Soft-iron core:** Cylindrical, fixed centrally, concentrates flux radially across the air gap.
- **Moving coil:** Lightweight rectangular coil of $N$ turns of fine copper wire, wound on an aluminum former, pivoted on jeweled bearings.
- **Control springs:** Two phosphor-bronze hair springs — provide controlling torque and lead-in currents.
- **Pointer & scale:** Lightweight aluminum pointer, knife-edge, on a uniformly graduated mirror scale.
- **Damping:** Eddy-current damping from the aluminum former; no additional damper needed.

**2. Working (2 marks):**

When current $I$ flows through the coil in the radial field $B$, the two vertical sides experience equal and opposite forces $F = BIl$ each, creating a couple.

**3. Derivation of deflecting torque (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_d &= \text{Force} \times \text{Perpendicular distance} \\
&= (BIl) \times (N \cdot d) \\
&= NBAI
\end{aligned}$$

where $A = l \times d$ is the coil area. At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c = K'\theta$, hence:

$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K'} \cdot I = G \cdot I$$

**4. Sources of error (1 mark):**

- **Temperature error:** heating of copper coil increases $R_m$.
- **Aging of magnet:** reduces $B$ over years (typically $< 1\%$/year).
- **Stray magnetic fields:** especially from nearby current-carrying conductors.
- **Spring fatigue:** alters $K'$.

---

### **Model Solution for A (b):**

**Given:** $R_m = 200\ \Omega$, $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$.

**Ayrton shunt arrangement:** The shunt is a **chain of resistors** $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots$ tapped at intermediate nodes; the meter is moved along the taps. This ensures the meter is **always protected**.

**Step 1 — Total shunt resistance (for highest range first):**

For the **100 mA range**, the entire shunt chain is in parallel with the meter.

$$R_T = \frac{R_m}{m_1 - 1}, \quad m_1 = \frac{I_1}{I_m} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 2000$$

$$R_T = \frac{200}{1999} = 0.10005\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Total shunt resistance for 10 mA range:**

$$m_2 = \frac{I_2}{I_m} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 200$$

For this range, only a **part** $R_T'$ of the chain is used:

$$R_T' = \frac{R_m}{m_2 - 1} = \frac{200}{199} = 1.005\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute individual Ayrton resistors:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= R_T' - R_T = 1.005 - 0.10005 = 0.905\ \Omega \\
R_2 &= R_T = 0.10005\ \Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

- Range 100 mA: meter sees $R_m = 200\ \Omega$ parallel to $(R_1+R_2) = 1.005\ \Omega$.
  - Equivalent = $(200 \times 1.005)/201.005 \approx 1\ \Omega$.
  - Voltage at full scale: $V = 50 \mu\text{A} \times 200 = 0.01\ \text{V}$.
  - Current through shunt: $0.01 / 1.005 \approx 9.95\ \text{mA}$. Total: $\approx 10\ \text{mA}$ for 10 mA range ✓ (proportionally 100 mA for higher range ✓).

**Final Ayrton Shunt Values:**

$$\boxed{R_1 = 0.905\ \Omega, \quad R_2 = 0.100\ \Omega}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)**. Derive the expression for vertical deflection sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Explain the construction and working of an **induction type single-phase energy meter**. Derive the equation for the number of revolutions of the aluminum disc. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for B (a):**

**1. Block diagram description (3 marks):**

Refer to Section 4.4 above. Components in order of signal flow:

1. **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT):** electron gun, deflection plates, phosphor screen.
2. **Vertical amplifier & attenuator:** scales input signal to plate-level voltage.
3. **Delay line:** ensures trigger event occurs *before* signal reaches plates (visible trigger).
4. **Time-base generator:** produces a sawtooth waveform that linearly sweeps the beam horizontally.
5. **Horizontal amplifier:** drives X-plates.
6. **Trigger & sync circuit:** synchronizes time-base with input signal.
7. **Z-axis (intensity):** modulates brightness for blanking.

**2. Working (2 marks):**

The electron beam, emitted from the heated cathode and accelerated by anodes, passes between two pairs of deflection plates. The Y-plates deflect the beam vertically in proportion to the input signal; the X-plates deflect horizontally in proportion to the time-base. The phosphor screen produces a visible trace.

**3. Derivation of deflection sensitivity (2 marks):**

Refer to **Derivation 6** in Section 3 above. The final result is:

$$Y = \frac{V_y L}{2 V_a d}\left(\frac{L}{2} + D\right)$$

Deflection sensitivity:

$$S = \frac{Y}{V_y} = \frac{L}{2 V_a d}\left(\frac{L}{2} + D\right) \quad \text{in m/V}$$

---

### **Model Solution for B (b):**

**1. Construction (2 marks):**

- **Current coil (CC):** Few turns of thick wire wound on the lower magnet; connected in series with the load.
- **Potential coil (PC):** Many turns of fine wire on the upper magnet, connected across the supply (in series with a high-resistance multiplier to make it highly inductive).
- **Aluminum disc:** Suspended in the air gap between the two electromagnets, mounted on a spindle.
- **Permanent brake magnet:** Provides braking torque proportional to disc speed.
- **Register / counter:** Geared to the spindle, displays kWh.

**2. Working principle (3 marks):**

- AC currents in CC and PC produce **two alternating fluxes** $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$, displaced in time (and space) by an angle $\psi \approx 90^{\circ}$ (made so by design of shading rings / lag coil).
- These fluxes induce **eddy currents** in the aluminum disc.
- The interaction of eddy currents with the other flux produces a **driving torque** proportional to $VI\cos\phi$ (i.e., real power).

**3. Driving torque (1 mark):**

$$T_d \propto \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\psi \propto VI\cos\phi$$

**4. Braking torque (1 mark):**

$$T_b \propto N \quad \text{(disc speed in rev/s)}$$

**5. Equation for steady-state revolutions:**

At equilibrium $T_d = T_b$:

$$N = K \cdot P = K \cdot VI\cos\phi$$

For energy $E$ over time $t$:

$$\boxed{N = K_e \cdot E}$$

where $K_e$ is the **meter constant** (typically 600 to 1200 rev/kWh).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically LOSE marks (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in formulas:** Always write $R_{sh}$ in $\Omega$, $I_m$ in A, etc. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing multiplying factor $m$ with current ratio $I/I_m$:** They are the same but state clearly $m = I/I_m$.
> 3. **Series ohmmeter zero-adjust resistor:** Many students forget that the **zero-adjust resistor** $R_0$ compensates for battery aging; the formula uses $(r + R_0 + R_m)$, not just $R_m$.
> 4. **Form factor vs Crest factor:** $K_f = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) = 1.11$ for sine; $K_p = \sqrt{2} = 1.414$ for sine. Confusing them is a 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Wattmeter connection:** When the potential coil is connected on the **load side** vs. **source side**, there is a small error — examiners expect students to mention this.
> 6. **CRO time-base:** Students often forget to mention the **sawtooth** waveform and the need for **synchronization (triggering)**.
> 7. **Energy meter $K_e$ units:** rev/kWh, not rev/Wh — losing factor of 1000.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Measuring and Recording Instruments**
>
> - ✅ **PMMC**: $\theta = (NBA/K') \cdot I$, **uniform scale**, DC only.
> - ✅ **Shunt**: $R_{sh} = R_m / (m-1)$, very low resistance, manganin.
> - ✅ **Multiplier**: $R_s = (V/I_m) - R_m$, very high resistance.
> - ✅ **Voltmeter sensitivity**: expressed in $\Omega/\text{V}$ — higher is better.
> - ✅ **Ayrton shunt**: meter never open-circuited during range change.
> - ✅ **MI instrument**: AC + DC, **non-uniform scale**, square-law deflection.
> - ✅ **Electrodynamometer wattmeter**: $P = VI\cos\phi$, average torque principle.
> - ✅ **Rectifier type**: form factor 1.11 used to convert avg → rms for **sine only**.
> - ✅ **Series ohmmeter**: scale reversed (0 on right), hyperbolic scale, half-scale = $E/I_m$.
> - ✅ **Energy meter**: $N = K_e \cdot E$ rev; driving torque $\propto VI\cos\phi$.
> - ✅ **CRO deflection sensitivity**: $S \approx LD/(2V_a d)$ in m/V; deflection $\propto V_y$.
> - ✅ **Strip-chart recorder**: self-balancing potentiometric, fixed paper speed.
> - ✅ **X–Y recorder**: two independent servos, stationary paper, plots $Y$ vs $X$.
> - ✅ **Magnetic tape recorder**: frequency-modulated or direct recording for instrumentation signals.
> - ✅ **Display devices**: LED (bright, fast), LCD (low power), CRT (analog display in CRO).
> - ✅ **Always state units and SI symbols** in derivations for full marks.
> - ✅ **Cross-verify final numerical answers** by plugging back into the original equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working and Torque Equation of PMMC, PMMI, Electrodynamometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working and Torque Equation of PMMC, PMMI, Electrodynamometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Focus:** This topic covers the three foundational electromechanical analog indicating instruments that form the backbone of every measurement laboratory — the **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC)**, **Permanent Magnet Moving Iron (PMMI)**, and **Electrodynamometer**. Mastery of their working principles and torque equations is mandatory for the ESE and is a high-frequency 14-mark question.

### 1.1 Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** The *Permanent Magnet Moving Coil* (PMMC) instrument, also known as the **D'Arsonval movement** or **Weston movement**, is a direct-acting analog indicating instrument in which a rectangular moving coil, suspended (or pivoted) in the uniform radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet, carries the current to be measured. The interaction between the coil current and the magnetic field produces a deflecting torque proportional to the current, balanced by a control torque from spiral springs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Mini Electric Motor" View):**
> Imagine you are holding a small rectangular wire loop between the poles of a strong horseshoe magnet. When you pass a current through the loop, it tries to rotate (just like a tiny DC motor). Now imagine the magnet is shaped with curved pole pieces so that the wire always "sees" the magnetic field lines coming straight at it (radial field) — the loop's sides always experience force perpendicular to them. Springy hairs resist the rotation, and a needle attached to the loop points to a calibrated scale. The stronger the current, the more the loop rotates, and the further the needle moves. That is essentially a PMMC instrument.

**Key Physical Constants / Standards (in bold):**
- Permanent magnet material: **Alnico** (Aluminium-Nickel-Cobalt alloy) or modern rare-earth variants.
- Flux density in the air gap: typically **0.1 to 0.5 Wb/m²** (≈ 1 to 5 kGauss).
- Full-scale current: typically **1 mA to 50 mA** for basic movement.
- Spring control constant: typically **0.2 × 10⁻⁶ to 1 × 10⁻⁶ N·m/rad**.

---

### 1.2 Permanent Magnet Moving Iron (PMMI) Instrument

**Formal Definition:** The *Permanent Magnet Moving Iron* (PMMI) instrument is one in which a piece of soft iron (or a system of iron vanes) moves under the influence of the magnetic field produced by a fixed current-carrying coil. The deflecting torque is a function of the square of the current, making the instrument inherently suitable for both **AC and DC** measurements with a single scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Magnetism Attracts" View):**
> Picture a paperclip hanging near an electromagnet. When you switch the electromagnet ON, the paperclip is pulled toward it. The harder the magnet pulls, the further the paperclip moves. Replace the paperclip with a soft iron vane inside a hollow coil, attach a needle to it, and provide a counter-spring. The needle deflection depends on how strongly the current magnetizes the iron — which goes up as the square of the current. That is the essence of a moving-iron instrument.

**Two Variants (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Attraction Type (Single Iron):** A single soft-iron vane is pulled *into* the coil when energized.
- **Repulsion Type (Double Iron):** Two parallel soft-iron vanes — one fixed, one movable — are magnetized with the **same polarity** and therefore *repel* each other.

---

### 1.3 Electrodynamometer Instrument

**Formal Definition:** The *Electrodynamometer* (or *Dynamometer*) is a precision instrument in which the deflecting torque is produced by the **electromagnetic interaction between two coils** — a **fixed coil** (which carries one current) and a **moving coil** (which carries another current), with **no permanent magnet** involved. The instrument can function as an ammeter, voltmeter, or (most importantly) a **wattmeter**, because its deflection depends on the product of the two coil currents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Two-Magnet Conversation" View):**
> Imagine two bar electromagnets on a table. One is bolted down (fixed coil), the other is suspended by a thread with a pointer (moving coil). When you energize *both* electromagnets, they try to align themselves like compass needles — N-S to S-N. The torque trying to twist the moving coil is proportional to how strong each electromagnet is — i.e., to the **product of the two currents**. Add a spring to pull it back, and you have an electrodynamometer. If one coil carries the load current and the other carries a current proportional to voltage, the deflection becomes proportional to **Power**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The electrodynamometer is **NOT** the same as PMMC! PMMC uses a *permanent* magnet; the electrodynamometer uses *two electromagnetic* coils. Confusing the two is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PMMC — Operating Theory

**Construction (Logical Steps):**
1. **Permanent Magnet (M):** Provides a strong, stable, and uniform magnetic field. Made of Alnico, magnetized once and never re-energized by the circuit current.
2. **Pole Pieces (P):** Soft-iron extensions shaped to produce a **cylindrical (radial) air gap** so that flux lines cut the moving coil sides *always perpendicularly* → this guarantees a **linear scale**.
3. **Moving Coil (C):** A rectangular coil of fine insulated copper wire wound on a thin aluminum former. The former also acts as a **damping eddy-current disc**.
4. **Control Springs (S):** Two phosphor-bronze spiral springs provide both **controlling torque** and **current leads** to the coil.
5. **Pointer & Scale:** Lightweight aluminum pointer rigidly attached to the coil spindle, moving over a mirror-backed scale to eliminate parallax.

**Operating Logic (Why and How):**

- **Step 1 — Deflecting Torque Generation:** When current $I$ flows through the coil of $N$ turns, each side of the coil (length $l$) experiences a force $F = BIl \cdot N$ in the radial magnetic field of density $B$.
- **Step 2 — Force × Radius = Torque:** The two forces act on opposite sides of the coil at radius $r = d/2$ (where $d$ is the breadth). The net deflecting torque is:
  $$T_d = 2 \times (NBIl) \times r = NBIA$$
  where $A = l \times d$ is the **effective coil area**.
- **Step 3 — Spring Control:** The spiral springs provide a linear restoring torque:
  $$T_c = K \theta$$
  where $K$ is the spring constant (N·m/rad).
- **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** At steady state, $T_d = T_c$, giving the famous PMMC governing equation:
  $$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I}$$
  Since $NBA/K$ is a **constant** for a given instrument, the deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional** to the current $I$ → **uniform / linear scale**.
- **Step 5 — Eddy-Current Damping:** When the aluminum former rotates in the magnetic field, eddy currents induced in it oppose the motion (Lenz's law), providing smooth, critical, or near-critical damping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Because the deflecting torque is proportional to $I$ (and not $I^2$), a PMMC instrument is fundamentally a **DC instrument** (or a true-RMS instrument only with a rectifier front-end for AC). Connecting a PMMC directly to AC without a rectifier will simply show a vibration around zero.

---

### 2.2 PMMI — Operating Theory

**Construction (Logical Steps):**
1. **Fixed Coil (FC):** A few turns of heavy wire wound on a rectangular bobbin — carries the current to be measured. In low-current (repulsion) designs, the moving iron is *inside* the coil.
2. **Moving Iron Vane(s):** Soft-iron, carefully shaped and *annealed* to minimize residual magnetism (hysteresis loss).
3. **Control Spring:** Provides restoring torque $T_c = K\theta$.
4. **Air-Chamber Damping:** A vane moving in a sealed air chamber produces **pneumatic (air-friction) damping** — the only practical damping for moving-iron (eddy damping is useless because the field is weak and unsteady).
5. **Pointer & Scale:** Calibrated based on $I^2$ relationship → **non-uniform (compressed) scale**.

**Operating Logic — Attraction Type:**

- **Step 1 — Coil Magnetization:** Current $I$ in the fixed coil produces flux $\phi \propto I$. The flux density in the coil's air gap is $B \propto I$.
- **Step 2 — Iron Attraction:** The soft-iron vane is magnetized by this field. The force of attraction is $F \propto B^2 \propto I^2$.
- **Step 3 — Torque Generation:** A small increment of work $dW = F \cdot dr$ produces a torque $T_d = F \cdot r \propto I^2$.
- **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** $T_d = T_c \Rightarrow \theta \propto I^2$.

**Operating Logic — Repulsion Type:**

- **Step 1 — Mutual Magnetization:** Both iron vanes (one fixed, one movable) are magnetized by the same coil current. By Lenz/Laplace reasoning, the *adjacent* ends of the two vanes acquire the **same polarity** (or opposite — depending on geometry) and repel.
- **Step 2 — Repulsion Force:** The repulsive force $F \propto I^2$ is generally *more uniform* than the attraction type, giving a **more linear** scale on the lower part of the range.
- **Step 3 — Equilibrium:** $T_d = T_c \Rightarrow \theta \propto I^2$ again.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Because the deflecting torque is proportional to $I^2$, the PMMI gives the **same RMS reading for AC and DC**. This is why moving-iron instruments are the most common **AC panel meters** in industry. The square-law also makes the scale **non-linear (crowded at the low end)**, a classic viva question.

---

### 2.3 Electrodynamometer — Operating Theory

**Construction (Logical Steps):**
1. **Fixed Coil (FC):** Two sections of heavy wire, precisely spaced, that produce a nearly uniform magnetic field in the central air gap. Often split into two halves so the moving coil fits *between* them.
2. **Moving Coil (MC):** A light rectangular coil of fine wire, mounted on a non-metallic (or weakly conducting) former, pivoted inside the fixed coils.
3. **Spiral Control Springs (S1, S2):** Provide restoring torque and current leads to the moving coil.
3. **Air-Chamber Damping:** Since the field is *air-cored* (not a permanent magnet), eddy damping is essentially zero → **air-friction damping** is used.
4. **Pointer & Scale:** Calibrated to read the relevant quantity (A, V, or W).

**Operating Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Two Magnetic Fields:** Let the fixed coil carry $I_1$ and the moving coil carry $I_2$. Both currents produce magnetic fields; their mutual interaction creates a force.
- **Step 2 — Deflecting Torque from Energy Balance:** The instantaneous torque is related to the rate of change of mutual inductance $M$ between the coils with respect to angular displacement $\theta$:
  $$T_d = I_1 I_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$
- **Step 3 — DC Case:** Both $I_1$ and $I_2$ are constant → the torque is constant in time:
  $$T_d^{DC} = I_1 I_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$
- **Step 4 — AC Case (Sinusoidal Currents):** Let $i_1 = I_{1m}\sin(\omega t)$ and $i_2 = I_{2m}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. The instantaneous torque is time-varying, but the *moving system* cannot follow 50 Hz. It responds to the **average** torque:
  $$\overline{T_d} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$
  where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are now **RMS** values.
- **Step 5 — Equilibrium:** $T_d = T_c = K\theta$, giving:
  $$\theta = \dfrac{I_1 I_2}{K} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta} \quad \text{(DC)}$$
  $$\theta = \dfrac{I_1 I_2 \cos\phi}{K} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta} \quad \text{(AC)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** When used as a **wattmeter**, $I_1$ is the *load current* and $I_2$ is the *current proportional to load voltage*. Then $I_1 I_2 \cos\phi = V I \cos\phi = P$ (true power), and the pointer reads watts directly. This is the cornerstone of AC power measurement.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Rule for ESE:** Memorize the first three rows; derive the rest on demand.

| **Instrument** | **Deflecting Torque $T_d$** | **Control Torque $T_c$** | **Equilibrium Deflection $\theta$** | **Scale Nature** | **Damping** | **Best Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PMMC** | $NBIA$ | $K\theta$ | $\dfrac{NBA}{K}\,I$ | **Linear** (uniform) | **Eddy-current** (Al former) | DC ammeter/voltmeter; AC with rectifier |
| **PMMI (Attraction)** | $K_a I^2$ | $K\theta$ | $\dfrac{K_a}{K}\,I^2$ | **Square-law** (compressed low end) | **Air-friction** | AC ammeter/voltmeter (panel meter) |
| **PMMI (Repulsion)** | $K_r I^2$ | $K\theta$ | $\dfrac{K_r}{K}\,I^2$ | **Square-law** (better than attraction) | **Air-friction** | AC ammeter/voltmeter (panel meter) |
| **Electrodynamometer (DC)** | $I_1 I_2\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $K\theta$ | $\dfrac{I_1 I_2}{K}\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | Depends on $dM/d\theta$ | **Air-friction** | DC ammeter/voltmeter/wattmeter |
| **Electrodynamometer (AC)** | $I_1 I_2 \cos\phi\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | $K\theta$ | $\dfrac{I_1 I_2 \cos\phi}{K}\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | Same as DC | **Air-friction** | AC wattmeter (true power) |
| **PMMC (Full PMMC Eq.)** | $T_d = \frac{NBA}{R_m+R_s}\cdot V$ (as voltmeter) | $K\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K(R_m+R_s)}\,V$ | Linear | Eddy-current | DC voltmeter via series multiplier |

**Where the symbols mean:**
- $N$ = number of turns in moving coil; $B$ = flux density in air gap; $A$ = effective coil area; $I$ = current; $K$ = spring constant.
- $M$ = mutual inductance between fixed and moving coils; $\phi$ = phase angle between the two coil currents.
- $K_a, K_r$ = instrument constants for attraction and repulsion type moving iron.
- $R_m$ = moving coil resistance; $R_s$ = series (multiplier) resistance.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PMMC:** Embedded in every digital multimeter (after a current-to-voltage conversion stage), oscilloscope analog sections, panel meters in DC control panels (railway dashboards, battery banks, solar PV systems), and as the **null detector** in Wheatstone and Kelvin bridges.
- **PMMI:** The standard **AC switchboard meter** in substations, motor control centers (MCCs), and generator panels — anywhere a robust, inexpensive, frequency-independent RMS indication is required.
- **Electrodynamometer:** The **reference standard for AC power measurement** (precision wattmeters in calibration labs), and historically the only AC instrument available before the iron-vane and rectifier types. Today, used in laboratory wattmeters and as transfer standards.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Deflecting Torque of PMMC ($T_d = NBIA$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation appears in nearly every December and June ESE paper. Writing $T_d = BIAl$ instead of $NBIA$ is the most common error — students forget that the coil has **$N$ turns** and the force must be summed.

**Setup:** Consider a rectangular coil of $N$ turns, each of side lengths $l$ (length, along the air gap axis) and $d$ (breadth, along the rotation axis). The coil is suspended in a radial magnetic field of uniform density $B$ produced by a permanent magnet.

**Step 1 — Force on a single current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field:**

For a single straight conductor of effective length $l$ carrying current $I$ in a magnetic field of density $B$, the magnitude of the force experienced is given by the Laplace force law:
$$F_1 = BIl$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the number of turns $N$:**

Each of the $N$ turns contributes one such force, so the total force on one side of the coil is:
$$F = NBIl$$

**Step 3 — Identify the moment arm:**

The force acts tangentially on the side of length $l$, and its line of action is at a perpendicular distance equal to half the coil breadth, i.e., $r = d/2$ from the axis of rotation. The torque is force times perpendicular distance:
$$T_d = F \times r = NBIl \times \dfrac{d}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the two active sides:**

In a rectangular coil, *two* opposite sides (the two sides of length $l$) carry the current in opposite directions. Both experience equal and opposite forces, but these forces form a **couple** that produces a torque in the **same rotational sense**. Therefore, we must double the torque from a single side:
$$T_d = 2 \times NBIl \times \dfrac{d}{2} = NBIl \cdot d$$

**Step 5 — Group the geometric terms into the coil area $A$:**

Define the *effective coil area* as:
$$A = l \cdot d$$
Substituting:
$$\boxed{T_d = NBIA}$$

**Step 6 — Final form of the PMMC governing equation:**

At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$:
$$NBIA = K\theta$$
$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K}\,I}$$

Since $N, B, A, K$ are all constants for a given instrument, the deflection is **directly proportional** to the current. The instrument has a **uniform scale**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Deflecting Torque of PMMI (Repulsion Type, $T_d \propto I^2$)

**Setup:** Two soft-iron vanes — one fixed to the coil body ($V_f$), one movable and attached to the spindle ($V_m$) — are placed inside a coil carrying current $I$.

**Step 1 — Magnetic field due to the fixed coil:**

By Ampere's law, the magnetic field $B$ inside the coil is proportional to the current:
$$B \propto I \quad\Longrightarrow\quad B = k_1 I$$
where $k_1$ is a constant depending on the coil geometry and permeability of the medium.

**Step 2 — Magnetization of the iron vanes:**

Each iron vane is placed in this field and gets magnetized. The induced magnetic moment of each vane is proportional to the field $B$ (assuming operation in the linear region of the magnetization curve):
$$m_f \propto B \quad \text{and} \quad m_m \propto B$$
so that:
$$m_f = k_2 B = k_1 k_2 I$$
$$m_m = k_2 B = k_1 k_2 I$$
with the same proportionality constant $k_2$ for both vanes (same material).

**Step 3 — Force of repulsion between two magnetized vanes:**

The magnetic force between two magnetized bodies with parallel dipoles separated by distance $r$ is, by Coulomb's magnetic analogue:
$$F \propto \dfrac{m_f \, m_m}{r^2}$$
Substituting the expressions for the moments:
$$F \propto \dfrac{(k_1 k_2 I)(k_1 k_2 I)}{r^2} = \dfrac{k_1^2 k_2^2}{r^2}\, I^2$$
Hence:
$$F = K_1 I^2$$
where $K_1$ is a geometric/magnetic constant for the instrument.

**Step 4 — Deflecting torque:**

If the moment arm of the force about the spindle is $x$ (a function of the deflection angle $\theta$, i.e., $x = x(\theta)$), the deflecting torque is:
$$T_d = F \cdot x(\theta) = K_1 I^2 x(\theta)$$

**Step 5 — Final form (general repulsion-type equation):**

Absorbing the function $x(\theta)$ into a generalized instrument constant (which is weakly dependent on $\theta$ for small displacements):
$$\boxed{T_d = K_d I^2}$$
where $K_d$ is a weak function of $\theta$. The dominant dependence is **on the square of the current**.

**Step 6 — Equilibrium:**

$$K_d I^2 = K \theta$$
$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{K_d}{K}\,I^2}$$
This is the **square-law** response, hence a **non-uniform scale**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Deflecting Torque of Electrodynamometer ($T_d = I_1 I_2\,dM/d\theta$)

**Setup:** A fixed coil $F$ carries current $I_1$ and a moving coil $M$ carries current $I_2$. Let $M(\theta)$ be the mutual inductance between the two coils as a function of the angle $\theta$ through which the moving coil has been deflected.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the system:**

The total magnetic energy $W$ of a two-coil system carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ is:
$$W = \dfrac{1}{2}L_1 I_1^2 + \dfrac{1}{2}L_2 I_2^2 + M(\theta) I_1 I_2$$
where $L_1, L_2$ are the self-inductances of the two coils.

**Step 2 — Energy change due to rotation:**

If the moving coil rotates by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ at constant currents (the *force* interpretation, applicable to galvanometric systems), the change in field energy is:
$$dW = I_1 I_2 \, \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}\,d\theta$$
(since $I_1, I_2$ are held constant and $L_1, L_2$ do not depend on $\theta$ for an air-core system).

**Step 3 — Equate to mechanical work:**

The mechanical work done by the deflecting torque $T_d$ in producing the rotation $d\theta$ is:
$$dW_{\text{mech}} = T_d\, d\theta$$
By the principle of virtual work for conservative magnetic systems at constant current:
$$T_d\, d\theta = I_1 I_2\, \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}\, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $d\theta$ and obtain the DC torque:**

$$\boxed{T_d = I_1 I_2\, \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}}$$

**Step 5 — AC case (time-averaged):**

For sinusoidal currents, $i_1 = \sqrt{2}\,I_1 \sin(\omega t)$ and $i_2 = \sqrt{2}\,I_2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, the instantaneous torque is:
$$T_d(t) = 2 I_1 I_2 \sin(\omega t) \sin(\omega t - \phi)\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$
Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:
$$T_d(t) = I_1 I_2 \left[\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\right] \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$

**Step 6 — Average over one cycle:**

The time-average of $\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ over a complete cycle is zero. Therefore:
$$\boxed{\overline{T_d} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \, \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}}$$
where $I_1, I_2$ are now **RMS** values.

**Step 7 — Equilibrium and final reading:**

At equilibrium, $\overline{T_d} = T_c = K\theta$:
$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{I_1 I_2 \cos\phi}{K}\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Power-Meter Identification:** In a wattmeter connection, the fixed (current) coil carries the load current $I_L = I_1$, and the moving (potential) coil carries a current $I_2 \propto V$. The current $I_2$ is in phase with $V$ (the potential coil is highly resistive). Hence $\cos\phi = \cos\phi_V$ where $\phi_V$ is the angle between $V$ and $I_L$ — which is the **load power-factor angle**. The product $V I_L \cos\phi$ is exactly the **real power** $P$. Therefore the deflection is directly proportional to true power — the gold standard of AC power measurement.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Quick Reference)

| **Parameter** | **PMMC** | **PMMI** | **Electrodynamometer** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driving field source | Permanent magnet | Fixed coil current | Two coil currents |
| $T_d$ dependence on $I$ | $T_d \propto I$ | $T_d \propto I^2$ | $T_d \propto I_1 I_2$ |
| Scale | **Linear** | **Square-law (non-linear)** | Depends on $dM/d\theta$ |
| Suitable for AC? | Only with rectifier | Yes (RMS) | Yes (RMS or watts) |
| Damping method | Eddy-current (Al former) | Air-friction | Air-friction |
| Accuracy class | High (0.1 – 1.0) | Moderate (1.0 – 2.5) | Moderate to high (0.2 – 1.0) |
| Power consumption | Very low | Moderate | High (fixed coil) |
| Cost | Low | Lowest | Highest |
| Frequency limit | DC only (or audio with rectifier) | Up to ~1 kHz | Up to ~1 kHz (air-core) |
| KTU typical use | DC ammeter, voltmeter, ohmmeter | AC switchboard meter | Wattmeter, AC/DC standards |
