## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working of a PMMC instrument. Why is its scale linear?*

**Model Answer:**

A Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument, also called the D'Arsonval movement, consists of a permanent magnet (typically Alnico), shaped soft-iron pole pieces, a rectangular moving coil of $N$ turns wound on an aluminum former, two spiral phosphor-bronze control springs, a pointer, and a calibrated scale.

When the current $I$ to be measured is passed through the moving coil via the control springs, each vertical side of the coil experiences a force $F = BIlN$ in the radial magnetic field of flux density $B$. The two forces, acting on opposite sides, form a couple whose moment is $T_d = NBIA$, where $A$ is the effective coil area.

The radial design of the pole pieces ensures that the flux lines always cut the coil sides perpendicularly, regardless of coil position. Hence $B$ is effectively constant over the operating range. The control spring provides a restoring torque $T_c = K\theta$. At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$, giving $\theta = (NBA/K) \cdot I$. Since $N, B, A, K$ are all constants, $\theta \propto I$, which is the condition for a **uniformly divided (linear) scale**. Eddy-current damping is provided by the aluminum former rotating in the magnetic field.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write $T_d = BIl$ (forgetting the factor $N$ and the breadth $d$). Always write $T_d = NBIA$ in full, with $A = l \times d$. Marks are deducted for sloppy notation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is a moving-iron instrument suitable for both AC and DC measurements? Mention its damping method.*

**Model Answer:**

In a moving-iron (PMMI) instrument, the deflecting torque is produced by the magnetization of one or more soft-iron vanes placed inside a fixed coil carrying the current $I$. The force (or repulsion) between the magnetized vanes is proportional to the **square of the current**, $T_d \propto I^2$.

Because the torque depends on $I^2$, when a sinusoidal AC current is applied, the time-averaged torque is proportional to the **mean-square value** of the current. The pointer, being heavily damped and incapable of following the 50 Hz fluctuations, settles at a position proportional to the RMS value. The same instrument therefore gives the **same RMS reading for an AC current as for a DC current of equal magnitude**. This is why PMMI instruments are universally used as **AC/DC switchboard meters** with a single scale.

The damping method used is **air-friction damping** (pneumatic damping), since eddy-current damping is impractical in the weak, time-varying field of the moving-iron mechanism.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common wrong answer is "the iron moves for both AC and DC." That is too vague. You must state that the **square-law dependence** makes the indication RMS-equivalent. Examiners award 1 of 3 marks for this point.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of a Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) instrument. Derive the equation for the deflecting torque and show that the scale is linear.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An PMMC instrument has the following data: number of turns $N = 100$, flux density $B = 0.15\ \text{Wb/m}^2$, coil area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, spring constant $K = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m/rad}$. If the full-scale current is $5\ \text{mA}$, determine (i) the deflecting torque at full scale and (ii) the full-scale deflection in degrees.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The PMMC instrument consists of:
1. **Permanent Magnet** — A high-coercivity Alnico magnet provides a strong, stable, and uniform magnetic field.
2. **Pole Pieces** — Soft-iron extensions shaped to produce a cylindrical (radial) air gap.
3. **Moving Coil** — A rectangular coil of fine insulated copper wire wound on a thin, light aluminum former, pivoted in jewel bearings.
4. **Control Springs** — Two spiral phosphor-bronze springs provide both the controlling torque and the current leads.
5. **Pointer and Scale** — A lightweight aluminum pointer attached to the spindle, moving over a mirror-backed scale.

**Working:** The current $I$ is led into the moving coil via the springs. The coil sits in the radial air gap where the flux density $B$ is uniform and always perpendicular to the coil sides. The Laplace force on each vertical side of the coil is $F = NBIl$. The two opposite forces form a couple:
$$T_d = F \cdot d = NBIl \cdot d = NBIA$$
where $A = l \cdot d$ is the effective coil area.

The springs provide a linear restoring torque $T_c = K\theta$. At equilibrium:
$$NBIA = K\theta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K}\,I$$
Since $NBA/K$ is a constant, $\theta \propto I$ — hence the scale is **linear**. Damping is by eddy currents induced in the aluminum former, and the aluminum former construction is also why PMMC instruments have very low moment of inertia and high sensitivity.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 100$
- $B = 0.15\ \text{Wb/m}^2$
- $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$
- $K = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m/rad}$
- $I_{FS} = 5\ \text{mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$

**(i) Deflecting torque at full scale:**

Using the formula $T_d = NBIA$:
$$T_{d,FS} = 100 \times 0.15 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$T_{d,FS} = 100 \times 0.15 \times 5 \times 4 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$T_{d,FS} = 300 \times 10^{-7} = 3 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{N·m}$$

**(ii) Full-scale deflection in degrees:**

Using $\theta_{FS} = \dfrac{NBA}{K}\,I_{FS}$:
$$\theta_{FS} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 60\ \text{rad}$$
Converting to degrees:
$$\theta_{FS} = 60 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = \dfrac{10800}{\pi} \approx 3437.7^\circ$$

This huge value is impractical — in real instruments, the coil area, turns, and spring constant are carefully chosen so that full-scale deflection is around 90° to 120°.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1] — Alternative to Question A**

**(a)** *With the help of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of an electrodynamometer type wattmeter. Derive the expression for the deflecting torque in the AC case and show that the deflection is proportional to the true power consumed by the load.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *In an electrodynamometer wattmeter, the fixed coil carries the load current of $10\ \text{A}$ (RMS) and the moving coil (with a series multiplier of $5\ \text{k}\Omega$) is connected across a $200\ \text{V}$ (RMS) supply. The power factor of the load is $0.8$ lagging. The mutual inductance varies as $M(\theta) = (10 + 5\theta)\ \mu\text{H}$, and the spring constant is $K = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m/rad}$. Determine (i) the deflecting torque in $\mu\text{N·m}$ and (ii) the steady-state deflection in degrees.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction, Working, and Derivation (7 marks):**

The electrodynamometer wattmeter consists of:
1. **Fixed Coil (FC)** — Two precisely spaced sections of heavy wire, connected in series, that carry the **load current** $I_L$ and produce a magnetic field in the air gap.
2. **Moving Coil (MC)** — A light rectangular coil of fine wire suspended between the fixed coils, carrying a current $I_P$ proportional to the load voltage.
3. **Spiral Control Springs** — Provide restoring torque $T_c = K\theta$ and current leads to the moving coil.
4. **Pointer and Scale** — Calibrated directly in watts.
5. **Air-Chamber Damping** — Because the field is air-cored, eddy damping is absent; air friction is used.

**Working (AC Case):**

Let the load voltage be $v = \sqrt{2}\,V\sin(\omega t)$ and the load current be $i_L = \sqrt{2}\,I_L\sin(\omega t - \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the load power-factor angle. The current in the moving coil is $i_P = v/R_m$ (purely resistive multiplier), so:
$$i_P = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,V}{R_m}\sin(\omega t)$$

The instantaneous torque is:
$$T_d(t) = i_L i_P\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta} = \dfrac{2 V I_L}{R_m}\sin(\omega t)\sin(\omega t - \phi)\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$

Using the identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:
$$T_d(t) = \dfrac{V I_L}{R_m}\left[\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\right]\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$

The moving system cannot follow the $2\omega$ ripple; it responds to the **average**:
$$\overline{T_d} = \dfrac{V I_L \cos\phi}{R_m}\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta} = \dfrac{P}{R_m}\,\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$

where $P = V I_L \cos\phi$ is the **true (real) power** consumed by the load. Since $\overline{T_d} \propto P$, the deflection $\theta = \overline{T_d}/K$ is **directly proportional to the true power**, which is exactly why the instrument is called a wattmeter.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $I_L = 10\ \text{A}$ (RMS)
- $V = 200\ \text{V}$ (RMS)
- $R_m = 5\ \text{k}\Omega = 5 \times 10^3\ \Omega$
- Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.8$
- $M(\theta) = (10 + 5\theta)\ \mu\text{H}$ where $\theta$ is in radians and $M$ in $\mu\text{H}$
- $K = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m/rad}$

**Step 1 — Compute $dM/d\theta$:**
$$\dfrac{dM}{d\theta} = 5\ \mu\text{H/rad} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the current in the moving coil (RMS):**
$$I_P = \dfrac{V}{R_m} = \dfrac{200}{5 \times 10^3} = 0.04\ \text{A} = 40\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average deflecting torque (AC formula):**
$$\overline{T_d} = I_L \cdot I_P \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$$
$$\overline{T_d} = 10 \times 0.04 \times 0.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\overline{T_d} = 0.32 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{N·m}$$
$$\overline{T_d} = 1.6\ \mu\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the steady-state deflection (in radians):**
$$\theta = \dfrac{\overline{T_d}}{K} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.2\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degrees:**
$$\theta = 3.2 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = \dfrac{576}{\pi} \approx 183.35^\circ$$

This large deflection suggests that either $dM/d\theta$ would be reduced (e.g., $M(\theta) = (10 + 0.1\theta)\ \mu\text{H}$ in a practical instrument) or $K$ would be increased, to obtain a full-scale deflection around 90°–120°.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Electrodynamometer:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\cos\phi$** in the AC case: A common 2-mark error. Always write $\cos\phi$ in the AC torque expression.
> 2. **Confusing $I_P$ with $V$:** Many students write $\overline{T_d} = V I_L \cos\phi$ directly. While dimensionally suggestive, the correct form is $I_L I_P \cos\phi$, where $I_P = V/R_m$.
> 3. **Mixing up RMS and peak:** If a question gives peak values, you must divide by $\sqrt{2}$ to get RMS before using the AC torque formula. The AC formula with $\cos\phi$ uses **RMS** values throughout.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This 3 Times Before the ESE.**

- **PMMC** deflecting torque: $T_d = NBIA$. Final form: $\theta = (NBA/K)\cdot I$. **Linear scale, eddy damping, DC only.**
- **PMMC construction keywords for sketch:** Permanent magnet → Pole pieces (curved) → Radial air gap → Moving coil on **aluminum former** → Spiral springs (2) → Pointer → Mirror scale.
- **PMMI** deflecting torque: $T_d = K_d I^2$. **Square-law scale, air damping, AC+DC, RMS reading.**
- **PMMI types:** *Attraction* (single vane pulled in) vs. *Repulsion* (two vanes, same polarity ends repel). Repulsion type has a more uniform scale.
- **PMMI construction keywords for sketch:** Fixed coil (rectangular bobbin) → Soft iron vanes (fixed + moving) → Spiral spring → **Air chamber** for damping → Compressed scale.
- **Electrodynamometer** DC torque: $T_d = I_1 I_2 (dM/d\theta)$. AC torque: $\overline{T_d} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi\,(dM/d\theta)$. Damping: air friction.
- **Wattmeter identity:** When $I_1 = I_L$ (load) and $I_2 \propto V$, then $\theta \propto V I_L \cos\phi = P_{\text{real}}$.
- **Damping comparison:** PMMC → **Eddy current**; PMMI → **Air friction**; Electrodynamometer → **Air friction** (no eddy in air-core).
- **Scale comparison:** PMMC → **Linear**; PMMI → **Square-law (non-linear)**; Electrodynamometer wattmeter → **Linear in $P$** (because $P$ itself goes as $V I \cos\phi$).
- **Frequency limit:** PMMC = DC; PMMI ≈ 1 kHz; Electrodynamometer ≈ 1 kHz (air-core).
- **KTU must-derive equations (worth 5–7 marks each):** $T_d = NBIA$ for PMMC; $T_d \propto I^2$ for PMMI; $T_d = I_1 I_2 (dM/d\theta)$ for dynamometer DC and the AC form with $\cos\phi$.
- **Standard KTU answer length for 7-mark derivations:** Include (1) labelled block diagram, (2) assumption list, (3) stepwise force/torque derivation, (4) final boxed result, (5) one-sentence practical interpretation.
- **Most-asked numerical pattern:** Given $N, B, A, K, I_{FS}$ → find $T_{d,FS}$ and $\theta_{FS}$ in degrees. For wattmeter: given $I_L, V, R_m, \cos\phi, M(\theta), K$ → find $\overline{T_d}$ and $\theta$.
- **Golden rule:** If a numerical answer is **physically unreasonable** (e.g., full-scale angle of 3437°), mention it and explain the design trade-off. Examiners reward engineering judgment with **1 bonus mark**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Oscilloscope, Digital Storage Oscilloscopes, Wave Analyzers, Instrument Transformers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sampling Oscilloscope, Digital Storage Oscilloscopes, Wave Analyzers, Instrument Transformers # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Sampling Oscilloscope > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **Sampling Oscilloscope** is a specialized real-time-replacement instrument that captures a periodic high-frequency (or fast-rising) repetitive waveform by taking *discrete amplitude samples* of the signal at successively delayed time intervals across many repetitions, and then reconstructs the complete waveform on a lower-frequency CRT/display for observation. **Key Operating Principle:** It overcomes the bandwidth limitation of conventional oscilloscopes (typically limited to a few hundred MHz) by using **equivalent-time sampling**, enabling observation of signals up to **50 GHz** and beyond. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Stroboscopic Flashlight"** > Imagine you want to photograph a spinning fan blade. Your camera shutter is too slow to "freeze" the motion. Instead, you snap a flash photo once per revolution, but you *delay* the flash by 1° more each time. After 360 such photographs (snapped over 360 revolutions), you assemble them into one composite image — and the fan blade looks perfectly still. The **Sampling Oscilloscope does exactly the same thing with electrical signals**: it triggers a tiny time delay (Δt) later on each successive trigger, samples the signal at that point, and assembles thousands of dots into a complete waveform trace. **Two Sub-Modes of Sampling:** - **Equivalent-Time Sampling (Sequential):** Trigger event delays by Δt each cycle → builds one dot per trigger. - **Equivalent-Time Sampling (Random):** Sample points taken randomly with respect to trigger → requires a separate time-reference circuit. ## 1.2 Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)** is an oscilloscope that uses an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** to digitize the input waveform and stores the resulting binary data in a **digital memory (RAM)**. The stored samples are then reconstructed on the display and may be post-processed mathematically. **Distinguishing Features:** - **Real-time sampling** (captures one full sweep in a single trigger event). - Stores waveform data permanently (until cleared or overwritten). - Supports pre-trigger viewing (can show what happened *before* the trigger). - Allows mathematical operations (FFT, averaging, integration, differentiation). - Supports connectivity: **USB, LAN (Ethernet), GPIB**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Voice Recorder"** > A regular oscilloscope is like watching a live sports match through binoculars — you see it only as it happens. A DSO is like recording the match on a DVR: the ADC digitizes the match, the hard drive (RAM) stores it, and you can pause, rewind, slow it down, zoom in, or even measure each player's run. Engineers can revisit the same captured event thousands of times. ## 1.3 Wave Analyzers > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** A **Wave Analyzer** is an instrument designed to measure the **amplitude (and sometimes phase)** of individual frequency components present in a complex (non-sinusoidal) periodic waveform. It is essentially a tunable, narrow-band voltmeter that sweeps through a frequency spectrum. **Two Principal Variants (KTU Mandatory):** - **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer (HWA):** Uses a superheterodyne receiver principle with a local oscillator. Most common industrial type. - **Harmonic Distortion Analyzer:** A special-purpose wave analyzer that reads **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** directly as a percentage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Audio Equalizer Inspector"** > Imagine a music track with vocals, guitar, and drums playing simultaneously. A regular voltmeter would only tell you the *total loudness*. A wave analyzer is like a frequency-tunable stethoscope: you set it to 440 Hz, and it tells you exactly how much the singer's "A note" contributes. Set it to 1 kHz, and it tells you the guitar's contribution. It separates the composite signal into its harmonic ingredients. ## 1.4 Instrument Transformers > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** **Instrument Transformers** are precision transformer devices used in **AC power measurement and protection circuits** to step down high voltages and high currents to standardized, safe, low-level signals (typically **5 A** for current and **110 V** for voltage) that can be safely measured by standard meters, relays, and protection equipment. **Two Categories:** - **Current Transformer (CT):** Steps down high primary current → standardized secondary (5 A or 1 A). - **Potential Transformer (PT) / Voltage Transformer (VT):** Steps down high primary voltage → standardized secondary (110 V or 100/√3 V). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Power-System Measuring Cup"** > A 400 kV transmission line carries currents of thousands of amps — you cannot connect a normal ammeter to it (it would explode!). A **CT** is like installing a *precision flow-restrictor*: it gives a small, proportional current that a regular ammeter can safely read. A **PT** does the same for voltage. Together, they let engineers measure "elephant-scale" power with "mouse-scale" meters. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Sampling Oscilloscope — Operating Theory **Operating Block Sequence:** 1. **Input Signal** (repetitive, high frequency $f_s$) enters the sampling gate. 2. **Trigger Circuit** detects each repetition of the input signal. 3. **Time-Base Generator** produces a **fast-rising ramp** (step delay Δt per cycle). 4. **Sampling Pulse Generator** outputs an extremely narrow pulse (gate pulse) whose position in time is shifted by Δt on each successive trigger. 5. **Sampling Gate** (a diode bridge or FET switch) opens momentarily, capturing one amplitude sample from the input. 6. **Hold Capacitor** stores the sample voltage until the next sample arrives. 7. **Vertical Amplifier** stretches the slow-changing held voltage so it can be displayed on a standard CRT. 8. **CRT Display** plots the dots — appearing as a continuous reconstructed waveform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalent-Time Sampling Formula:** If the input signal period is $T$ and the delay step is $\Delta t$, the *effective time per displayed dot* on the screen is $\Delta t$, even though the actual sampling rate is much lower. The screen sweep is therefore **slower than reality by a factor of $T/\Delta t$**. **Types of Sampling Oscilloscopes:** | Type | Description | Use Case | |------|-------------|----------| | **Real-Time Sampling** | Captures entire waveform in one trigger | Single-shot events | | **Equivalent-Time Sequential** | 1 dot/trigger, delay steps uniformly | Repetitive signals (most common) | | **Equivalent-Time Random** | Samples taken randomly w.r.t trigger | Pre-trigger display, faster acquisition | ## 2.2 Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) — Operating Theory **Core Architecture (KTU Mandatory Block Diagram):** 1. **Attenuator / Input Amplifier** — scales the signal to ADC range. 2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — samples at a high rate (sample rate $f_s$). 3. **Acquisition Memory (RAM)** — stores digitized samples (typically 1 Mpt to 1 Gpt). 4. **Time Base & Trigger Circuit** — controls sampling instants and capture events. 5. **Microprocessor / DSP** — processes samples (averaging, FFT, math). 6. **Display Memory & DAC** — reconstructs the waveform on LCD/display. 7. **Display (LCD/Color TFT)** — shows waveform with measurements. **Key Specifications (KTU expects students to know):** - **Sample Rate ($f_s$):** Number of ADC conversions per second (Sa/s). Modern DSOs: 1–100 GSa/s. - **Bandwidth (BW):** Maximum input frequency the scope can measure accurately (–3 dB point). - **Record Length:** Total memory depth (samples stored per acquisition). - **Vertical Resolution:** Typically **8 bits** (256 levels); high-end DSOs use 12 or 14 bits. - **Rise Time:** $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ (KTU favorite relationship). > [!NOTE] > **Nyquist Criterion (Critical for DSO Design):** To faithfully reconstruct a signal of highest frequency $f_{max}$, the sample rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. In practice, engineers use $f_s \geq 5 f_{max}$ for good fidelity. ## 2.3 Wave Analyzers — Operating Theory ### 2.3.1 Heterodyne Wave Analyzer (HWA) **Block Diagram Flow:** 1. **Input Signal** (complex waveform) enters a wideband amplifier. 2. **Local Oscillator (LO)** — a tunable RF oscillator with high spectral purity. 3. **Mixer** — multiplies input with LO, producing sum and difference frequencies. 4. **IF Amplifier (Intermediate Frequency, typically 455 kHz)** — narrow-band, high-Q crystal/LC filter passes only the IF band. 5. **Detector (Envelope / RMS)** — extracts amplitude. 6. **Meter / Display** — indicates amplitude at the *selected* frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heterodyne Principle:** When input signal $f_{in}$ mixes with LO frequency $f_{LO}$, the IF stage accepts only the difference: $f_{IF} = \vert f_{in} - f_{LO} \vert$. By tuning $f_{LO}$, the analyzer "looks at" different input frequencies one at a time, even though the IF filter is fixed-tuned. **Heterodyne Wave Analyzer Formula:** $$f_{in} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$$ - The IF stage is fixed at 455 kHz. - The LO is tuned so that $f_{in} - f_{LO} = f_{IF}$ (or $f_{LO} - f_{in} = f_{IF}$). ### 2.3.2 Harmonic Distortion Analyzer Measures the **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** of a waveform: $$THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ Where: - $V_1$ = RMS amplitude of the **fundamental** (1st harmonic) - $V_2, V_3, \ldots$ = RMS amplitudes of 2nd, 3rd, … nth harmonics ## 2.4 Instrument Transformers — Theory ### 2.4.1 Current Transformer (CT) **Construction:** A **toroidal (ring) core** of silicon steel or **mumetal** (high permeability, low loss) wound with a **secondary of many turns** ($N_s$). The **primary** is either a single conductor passing through the toroid ($N_p = 1$) or a few turns. **Ideal CT Transformation Ratio:** $$\dfrac{I_p}{I_s} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$ **Actual CT Ratio (with errors):** $$I_p = N_s \cdot \dfrac{I_s}{N_p} \quad (\text{Ideal})$$ > [!NOTE] > **CT Polarity Convention:** The **dot-marked terminals** of primary and secondary have the *same instantaneous polarity*. CTs are almost always used with the secondary **always short-circuited** (or connected to a low-impedance burden) — opening the secondary of a CT is dangerous (it develops very high voltages that can destroy insulation and endanger life). ### 2.4.2 Potential Transformer (PT) **Construction:** Identical in construction to a small power transformer, with a high-turn primary connected across the high-voltage line and a low-turn secondary. **Ideal PT Transformation Ratio:** $$\dfrac{V_p}{V_s} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}$$ **Burden:** The total impedance (in VA) connected across the secondary. Expressed as **apparent power rating** (e.g., 50 VA, 100 VA). ### 2.4.3 Ratio Error and Phase Angle Error (CT/PT) | Error | Formula | Meaning | |-------|---------|---------| | **Ratio Error** | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$ | Deviation of actual ratio from nominal | | **Phase Error** | $\delta$ (in minutes of arc) | Phase shift between primary and secondary phasors | Where $K_n$ is the **nominal (rated) transformation ratio**. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Formula | Unit | Remarks | |---------|---------|------|---------| | DSO Rise Time vs Bandwidth | $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | seconds | For 10–90% rise | | Nyquist Sampling Limit | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | $f_s$ = sample rate | | Effective Sampling Interval (Sampling Scope) | $\Delta t_{eff} = \Delta t$ per dot | seconds | Real-time span: $N \cdot \Delta t$ | | CT Ideal Ratio | $I_p / I_s = N_s / N_p$ | ratio | Dimensionless | | PT Ideal Ratio | $V_p / V_s = N_p / N_s$ | ratio | Dimensionless | | Ratio Error (CT/PT) | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$ | % | Negative if $I_p > K_n I_s$ | | Total Harmonic Distortion | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | % | $V_1$ = fundamental | | Heterodyne Tuning | $f_{in} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$ | Hz | IF = 455 kHz typical | | Sampling Pulse Width | $\tau_p \leq 1/(10 f_{max})$ | seconds | Aperture time of gate | | CT Secondary Voltage | $V_s = I_s \cdot Z_b$ | V | $Z_b$ = burden impedance | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of these systems:** > - **Sampling Oscilloscope** → Verifying fast edges in digital communication (PCIe, SerDes, microwave radar pulses). > - **DSO** → Power electronics debugging, embedded firmware verification, transient capture. > - **Wave Analyzers** → Audio engineering, power-quality audits, EMI compliance testing. > - **CT/PT** → Substation metering, protective relaying, energy billing in power utilities. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between DSO Rise Time and Bandwidth **Statement to Prove:** $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ (10%–90% rise time for a single-pole RC response). **Starting Point:** A practical oscilloscope vertical amplifier is modeled as a **first-order low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c = BW$. The transfer function is: $$H(f) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j(f / BW)}$$ **Step 1 — Step Response of First-Order System:** For a step input of amplitude $V_0$, the output is: $$v_{out}(t) = V_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ where the time constant is $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \cdot BW}$. **Step 2 — 10% Level Time:** Set $v_{out}/V_0 = 0.10$: $$0.10 = 1 - e^{-t_1/\tau} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-t_1/\tau} = 0.90 \;\Rightarrow\; t_1 = -\tau \ln(0.90)$$ $$t_1 = -\tau (-0.10536) = 0.10536\,\tau$$ **Step 3 — 90% Level Time:** Set $v_{out}/V_0 = 0.90$: $$0.90 = 1 - e^{-t_2/\tau} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-t_2/\tau} = 0.10 \;\Rightarrow\; t_2 = -\tau \ln(0.10)$$ $$t_2 = -\tau (-2.3026) = 2.3026\,\tau$$ **Step 4 — Rise Time:** $$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = (2.3026 - 0.10536)\,\tau = 2.1972\,\tau$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\tau = 1/(2\pi BW)$:** $$t_r = \dfrac{2.1972}{2\pi \cdot BW} = \dfrac{2.1972}{6.2832 \cdot BW} = \dfrac{0.3497}{BW}$$ $$\boxed{\,t_r \approx \dfrac{0.35}{BW}\,}$$ **Engineering Insight:** This relationship is universally applied to compare *scope bandwidth* and *measured signal rise time*. The *system rise time* of scope + signal is obtained by the **square-root sum of squares (SRSS)** formula: $$t_{r,system} = \sqrt{t_{r,scope}^2 + t_{r,signal}^2}$$ To measure a signal accurately, choose $t_{r,scope} \leq \dfrac{1}{5} t_{r,signal}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) — Step by Step **Statement:** For a periodic non-sinusoidal waveform, the *effective* (RMS) value is related to its harmonic components by Parseval's theorem: $$V_{rms}^2 = V_1^2 + V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2$$ **Step 1:** Let the fundamental be $V_1$ and the RMS sum of all higher harmonics be $V_h$: $$V_h^2 = \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2 \;\Rightarrow\; V_h = \sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}$$ **Step 2:** By definition of THD (ratio of harmonic RMS content to fundamental RMS): $$THD = \dfrac{V_h}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1}$$ **Step 3:** Expressed as a percentage: $$THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ **Worked Example:** A signal has fundamental $V_1 = 100$ V, 2nd harmonic $V_2 = 5$ V, 3rd harmonic $V_3 = 3$ V, 4th harmonic $V_4 = 2$ V. Calculate THD. **Solution:** $$V_h = \sqrt{5^2 + 3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9 + 4} = \sqrt{38} = 6.164 \text{ V}$$ $$THD = \dfrac{6.164}{100} \times 100\% = 6.16\%$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Current Transformer Ratio and Turns **Problem:** A CT has 1 primary turn and 200 secondary turns. The secondary ammeter reads 4.5 A. Find the primary current. **Step 1 — Apply Ideal CT Equation:** $$\dfrac{I_p}{I_s} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$\dfrac{I_p}{4.5} = \dfrac{200}{1}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $I_p$:** $$I_p = 4.5 \times 200 = 900 \text{ A}$$ **Step 4 — Nominal Ratio:** $$K_n = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = \dfrac{200}{1} = 200:5 = 40:1$$ (Industry convention: expressed as *rated primary* : *rated secondary*, e.g., 800:5.) --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Sampling Oscilloscope Effective Time Resolution **Problem:** A sampling oscilloscope uses an equivalent-time sequential sampling mode. The input signal has period $T = 10$ ns (frequency = 100 MHz). The delay increment per sample is $\Delta t = 10$ ps. If 1000 samples are taken to reconstruct the waveform, calculate (a) the equivalent time window displayed, and (b) the effective screen sweep time. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Total time window displayed:** $$T_{window} = N \cdot \Delta t = 1000 \times 10 \text{ ps} = 10 \text{ ns}$$ (This equals the input period — confirming the waveform covers exactly one cycle.) **Step 2 — Real time taken to acquire:** $$T_{acquire} = N \cdot T = 1000 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ \mu s}$$ **Step 3 — Time expansion factor:** $$\text{Expansion} = \dfrac{T_{acquire}}{T_{window}} = \dfrac{10\,\mu s}{10\,ns} = 1000$$ The scope effectively "slows down" the signal by a factor of 1000, so the CRT (which can only display low frequencies) can faithfully show a 100 MHz signal. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sampling Scope vs DSO | Parameter | Sampling Oscilloscope | Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) | |-----------|----------------------|-----------------------------------| | **Sampling method** | Equivalent-time (one dot per trigger) | Real-time (entire sweep per trigger) | | **Maximum frequency** | Up to ~50 GHz | Typically ≤ 5 GHz (high-end: 100 GHz) | | **Single-shot capture** | Not possible | Yes | | **Pre-trigger view** | Limited | Fully supported | | **Storage** | Analog hold capacitor | Digital RAM (millions of points) | | **Display** | Analog CRT | LCD / TFT digital | | **Best suited for** | Microwave, radar, fiber-optic | General-purpose, embedded, power | | **Cost** | High | Moderate to high | --- ## 3.6 Derivation: CT Secondary Voltage (Safety/Burden) **Statement:** The voltage developed across a CT secondary is: $$V_s = I_s \cdot Z_b$$ where $Z_b$ is the **burden** (ammeter + lead resistance). **Step 1 — Energy Conservation (Ideal Transformer):** Primary VA = Secondary VA: $$V_p I_p = V_s I_s$$ **Step 2 — Substitute CT Ratio:** $$\dfrac{I_s}{I_p} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_p = I_s \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_s$:** $$V_s = I_s \cdot Z_b$$ **Worked Numerical:** A CT with $N_p = 1$, $N_s = 400$ has secondary current $I_s = 5$ A through a burden of $Z_b = 0.6\,\Omega$. Find $V_s$ and $I_p$. $$V_s = 5 \times 0.6 = 3 \text{ V}$$ $$I_p = 5 \times 400 = 2000 \text{ A} = 2 \text{ kA}$$ > [!WARNING] > If the secondary of this CT is opened (no burden connected), $Z_b \to \infty$, and since magnetic flux must still be balanced, the core saturates and a dangerously high voltage (potentially kV-level) develops across the open-circuited secondary. This is why CT secondaries must *never* be open-circuited under load. --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Sampling Oscilloscope — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR node1[INPUT SIGNAL
Repetitive High Frequency] --> node2[Trigger Circuit] node1 --> node3[Sampling Gate] node2 --> node4[Time Base Generator
Ramp Generator] node4 --> node5[Sampling Pulse
Generator] node5 --> node3 node3 --> node6[Hold Capacitor
Memory Element] node6 --> node7[Vertical Amplifier
Stretching Amplifier] node7 --> node8[CRT Display
Y Deflection Plates] node4 --> node9[Horizontal Amplifier] node9 --> node10[CRT Display
X Deflection Plates] node3 --> node11[Staircase Generator
Memory] node11 --> node9 node2 --> node12[Time Base Reset] node12 --> node4 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The *trigger* fires on each cycle of the input. - The *time-base* issues a fast ramp whose voltage steps up by Δt on each trigger. - The *sampling pulse* fires at the moment the ramp voltage crosses a threshold. - The *sampling gate* is opened, capturing a vertical voltage sample. - The sample is held in a capacitor and *stretched* by the vertical amplifier. - A *staircase generator* provides the horizontal stepping signal that paints dots left-to-right on the CRT. --- ## 4.2 Digital Storage Oscilloscope — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL
from Probe] --> nodeB[Input Attenuator
and Preamplifier] nodeB --> nodeC[Analog to Digital
Converter ADC] nodeC --> nodeD[Acquisition
Memory RAM] nodeD --> nodeE[Microprocessor
and DSP] nodeE --> nodeF[Display
Memory] nodeF --> nodeG[Digital to Analog
Converter DAC] nodeG --> nodeH[LCD Color
Display Panel] nodeE --> nodeI[Control Panel
Knobs and Buttons] nodeJ[Time Base and
Trigger Circuit] --> nodeC nodeJ --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeK[Communication Ports
USB LAN GPIB] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The signal is conditioned by the attenuator/preamp, then digitized by the ADC. - Digitized samples are stored in RAM. - The microprocessor performs math (FFT, averaging, measurements). - Stored data is read out and displayed on the LCD. --- ## 4.3 Heterodyne Wave Analyzer — Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeP[Input Signal
Complex Waveform] --> nodeQ[Wideband
Input Amplifier] nodeQ --> nodeR[Mixer
Non Linear Device] nodeS[Local Oscillator
Tunable RF Source] --> nodeR nodeR --> nodeT[IF Amplifier
455 kHz Crystal Filter] nodeT --> nodeU[Detector
Envelope or RMS] nodeU --> nodeV[Indicating
Meter or Display] nodeW[Frequency
Dial] --> nodeS ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The input is amplified and fed to a mixer. - A tunable local oscillator (set by the frequency dial) is also fed to the mixer. - The mixer output contains many frequencies; the IF amplifier selects only the band around 455 kHz. - The detector produces a DC voltage proportional to the amplitude of the input component whose frequency satisfies $f_{in} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$. - The meter displays the amplitude. --- ## 4.4 Current Transformer and Potential Transformer — Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph nodeCT[Current Transformer CT] nodeCT1[High Voltage Line
Primary Conductor Np=1] --> nodeCT2[Toroidal Core
Silicon Steel Mumetal] nodeCT2 --> nodeCT3[Secondary Winding
Ns many turns] nodeCT3 --> nodeCT4[Ammeter
Burden Impedance Zb] nodeCT4 --> nodeCT5[Safety Short
Link if Meter Removed] end subgraph nodePT[Potential Transformer PT] nodePT1[High Voltage Bus
Primary Np many turns] --> nodePT2[Laminated Core
Silicon Steel] nodePT2 --> nodePT3[Secondary Ns
few turns] nodePT3 --> nodePT4[Voltmeter
Burden Zb] end ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **CT:** The primary is the line itself passing *through* the toroid (so $N_p = 1$). - **PT:** The primary is a multi-turn HV winding across the bus; secondary is a low-voltage winding. --- ## 4.5 Functional Signal-Flow Summary (Master Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD nodeT1[Physical Signal
High Frequency or
High Voltage or
High Current or
Complex Spectrum] --> nodeT2{Measurement
Instrument} nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Sampling Scope
High BW Repetitive] nodeT2 --> nodeT4[DSO
General Purpose Digital] nodeT2 --> nodeT5[Wave Analyzer
Frequency Selective] nodeT2 --> nodeT6[Instrument Transformer
Step Down to Safe Level] nodeT3 --> nodeT7[Displayed and
Stored Waveform] nodeT4 --> nodeT7 nodeT5 --> nodeT8[Amplitude vs
Frequency Plot] nodeT6 --> nodeT9[Scaled Safe
5A or 110V Signal] nodeT9 --> nodeT10[Standard Meter
or Relay] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the principle of operation of a sampling oscilloscope. How does it overcome the bandwidth limitation of a real-time oscilloscope?* **Model Answer (3 marks — structured KTU style):** A **sampling oscilloscope** exploits the repetitive nature of a high-frequency signal to construct its waveform from a sequence of *time-staggered* amplitude samples (3 points expected, here condensed). - The signal is applied to a sampling gate that is opened by an extremely narrow pulse (aperture time < 50 ps). - The pulse position is delayed by a small step Δt on each successive trigger (typically 1 ps – 10 ps). - A hold capacitor stores each sample; a vertical amplifier stretches the slow-changing held voltage. - A staircase generator steps the horizontal display by one dot per trigger. - After N triggers, the dots reproduce the original waveform. **Bandwidth Extension:** Because only **one sample per trigger** is needed, the equivalent-time sampling method effectively expands the time axis by a factor of $T/\Delta t$, where $T$ is the signal period. Thus, a CRT with only 100 MHz bandwidth can display signals up to 50 GHz, since the displayed "time per dot" remains slow. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD). A signal has fundamental 120 V RMS and harmonics of 6 V, 3 V, 2 V, 1 V. Calculate the THD.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** THD is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (above the fundamental) to the RMS value of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage. **Formula:** $$THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$ **Calculation:** $$V_h = \sqrt{6^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{36+9+4+1} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07 \text{ V}$$ $$THD = \dfrac{7.07}{120} \times 100\% = 5.89\%$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., two alternatives, one to be attempted). Each question typically splits as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Solutions must be structured, diagram-supported, and use proper KTU terminology. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Digital Storage Oscilloscope. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages over an analog CRO.* **Sub-part (a) — Block Diagram & Working (7 marks):** A DSO consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Input attenuator / preamplifier** — adjusts signal level to ADC input range. 2. **ADC** — samples the conditioned signal at a high rate ($f_s$). 3. **Acquisition memory (RAM)** — stores digitized samples. 4. **Time base & trigger circuit** — synchronizes sampling with the signal. 5. **Microprocessor / DSP** — performs post-acquisition math. 6. **Display memory & DAC** — reconstructs the waveform. 7. **LCD/Display** — visual output. **Working:** The trigger circuit detects a reference event on the input. The time base commands the ADC to start sampling. The ADC converts each instantaneous voltage into a binary number, which is stored in RAM. After acquisition, the stored binary data is read out, processed, and reconstructed on the display as a continuous waveform by connecting the dots. **Sub-part (b) — Advantages and Disadvantages (7 marks):** **Advantages over analog CRO:** - Permanent storage of waveforms. - Pre-trigger viewing. - Mathematical operations (FFT, averaging, integration, differentiation). - Higher measurement accuracy (cursor-based digital readouts). - Automated measurements (frequency, RMS, period, duty cycle). - Connectivity (USB/LAN/GPIB for remote operation). - Bright, sharp, persistence-free LCD display. **Disadvantages:** - Higher cost. - Limited by ADC sample rate and vertical resolution (typically 8 bits). - Aliasing if Nyquist criterion is violated. - Display refresh may miss very narrow glitches unless peak-detect mode is used. - Cannot display very high frequencies like a real-time analog CRO optimized for bandwidth. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]** *Explain the construction and working of a Current Transformer. Derive the expression for ratio error. Why must the secondary of a CT never be open-circuited?* **Sub-part (a) — Construction, Working, and Safety (7 marks):** **Construction:** A CT consists of a **toroidal core** of high-permeability, low-loss silicon steel (or mumetal for high accuracy) on which is wound a **secondary winding of many turns** ($N_s$ typically 100–1000). The **primary** is the line conductor itself passing through the centre of the toroid, so $N_p = 1$ (or a few turns for very low currents). **Working:** Based on transformer action, the primary current $I_p$ establishes an alternating magnetomotive force (mmf) $N_p I_p$. The secondary mmf $N_s I_s$ opposes it (Lenz's law). In the ideal case, $N_p I_p = N_s I_s$, giving: $$\dfrac{I_p}{I_s} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$ In practice, an exciting (magnetizing) current $I_e$ is needed to maintain the core flux, causing a small ratio error. **Why Secondary Must Never Be Open-Circuited (KTU favorite — high-yield):** When the secondary is closed (normal operation), the opposing secondary mmf keeps the net core flux small. If the secondary is opened, the demagnetizing effect of $N_s I_s$ vanishes, the entire primary mmf $N_p I_p$ becomes magnetizing, driving the core deep into saturation. The flux swings to a high value at a high rate $d\Phi/dt$, inducing a very large voltage across the open secondary (often 1–10 kV peak). This voltage: - Endangers personnel. - May puncture insulation. - Causes severe overheating of the core (hysteresis loss $\propto f B_{max}^2$). **Safety rule:** Always short-circuit the secondary before disconnecting the burden. **Sub-part (b) — Ratio Error Derivation (7 marks):** Let $I_e$ = exciting (magnetizing) current. By phasor mmf balance: $$N_p \vec{I_p} = N_s \vec{I_s} + N_p \vec{I_e}$$ Solving for $I_p$: $$I_p = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} I_s + I_e$$ The **nominal ratio** is $K_n = I_{p,\text{rated}}/I_{s,\text{rated}} = N_s/N_p$ (ideal). **Ratio Error Definition:** $$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$$ **Substitute $I_p$:** $$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - (K_n I_s + I_e)}{K_n I_s + I_e} \times 100\% = \dfrac{-I_e}{K_n I_s + I_e} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_r \approx -\dfrac{I_e}{K_n I_s} \times 100\% \quad (\text{negative sign indicates } I_p > K_n I_s)\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Many students **forget the negative sign** in the ratio-error formula and lose 2 marks. The sign is conventionally negative for a CT. > - When asked to "explain why CT secondary should not be open-circuited," students often give a *vague* answer like "it gives shock." You must mention **core saturation** and the $d\Phi/dt$ induced high voltage, both of which carry marks. > - In CT vs PT comparison questions, remember: **CT secondary is always short-circuited, PT secondary is never open-circuited** (PT behaves like a normal transformer — open-circuiting is safe; short-circuiting destroys it). --- ## 5.3 KTU Practice Numerical: Ratio Error of CT **Problem:** A CT has $N_p = 1$, $N_s = 200$. The rated secondary current is 5 A. The actual secondary current measured is 4.9 A when the primary carries 1000 A. Calculate the ratio error. **Step 1 — Nominal Ratio:** $$K_n = \dfrac{200}{1} = 200:5$$ **Step 2 — Apply Ratio Error Formula:** $$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$$ $$= \dfrac{200 \times 4.9 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\% = \dfrac{980 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\% = -2\%$$ $$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_r = -2\%\,}$$ The negative sign indicates $I_p > K_n I_s$, i.e., the actual current is slightly higher than indicated. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU Points):** ### Sampling Oscilloscope - Uses **equivalent-time sampling** to extend bandwidth beyond CRT limits. - Each trigger contributes **one dot** to the trace. - Delay step Δt can be as small as **1 ps**; effective bandwidth up to **50 GHz**. - Suitable only for **repetitive** signals — *cannot capture single-shot events*. - Key blocks: **Sampling Gate → Hold Capacitor → Stretcher → CRT**. ### Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) - Uses **ADC + RAM** for digital acquisition. - Sample rate must satisfy **Nyquist**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (in practice, $\geq 5 f_{max}$). - **Rise time vs Bandwidth:** $t_r = 0.35/BW$ (10–90%). - Enables **pre-trigger** view, **math operations** (FFT, integration, averaging), and **automated measurements**. - Typical vertical resolution: **8 bits** (256 levels). - Has communication ports: **USB, LAN, GPIB**. ### Wave Analyzers - **Heterodyne type** uses a tunable local oscillator and a fixed IF (455 kHz). - Tuning equation: $f_{in} = f_{LO} \pm f_{IF}$. - **THD** formula: $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$. - Wave analyzers are essentially **tunable narrow-band voltmeters** for harmonic analysis. ### Instrument Transformers - **CT** (Current Transformer): $I_p/I_s = N_s/N_p$; primary usually $N_p = 1$ (line through toroid). - **PT** (Potential Transformer): $V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s$; both windings isolated from the line. - **CT secondary MUST NEVER be open-circuited** (lethal kV-level voltages develop due to core saturation). - **PT secondary must never be short-circuited** (PT is essentially a small power transformer). - **Ratio Error:** $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$ (negative for CT, positive or negative for PT depending on design). - **Standard Secondary Ratings:** CT = 5 A or 1 A; PT = 110 V or 100/√3 V. - **Burden:** Impedance connected across the secondary; expressed in VA. ### Examiner-Approved Mnemonics - **CT: "Current Transformer → keep Closed"** (Never open secondary). - **PT: "Potential Transformer → keep open"** (Never short secondary). - **DSO memory depth × sample rate = capture time**: $T = M/f_s$. - **Sampling scope slow-down factor:** $T_{real}/T_{display} = N \cdot T / (N \cdot \Delta t) = T/\Delta t$. ---

topics.title: Current Transformer, Potential Transformer, True RMS Meter, Q-Meter, Strip Chart Recorder, XY Plotter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET303 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Current Transformer, Potential Transformer, True RMS Meter, Q-Meter, Strip Chart Recorder, XY Plotter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INSTRUMENTATION TRANSFORMERS, RMS METERS, Q-METER & GRAPHICAL RECORDERS

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Current Transformer (CT)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Current Transformer is a *measurement-type instrument transformer* used in conjunction with AC ammeters, wattmeters, energy meters, and protective relays. It is specifically designed to produce a secondary current that is precisely proportional to the primary current being measured, with a known phase relationship, while providing galvanic isolation between the high-voltage primary circuit and the low-voltage secondary measuring circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> A CT works on the **principle of mutual induction** (Faraday's Law), exactly like a power transformer, but is operated under *near-short-circuit secondary conditions* rather than near-no-load conditions. The primary has very few turns (often just a single bus-bar pass), while the secondary has many turns wound on a closed, high-permeability silicon-steel or nickel-iron core.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Funnel" of Currents):**
Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline carrying 1000 litres/sec, but you only have a small measuring cup. You cannot stick the cup directly into the pipe. Instead, you install a *funnel-divider* — a CT — that *mirrors* a small, safe, proportional flow (say 5 A) into a parallel measurement channel. The original pipeline (primary) doesn't even "know" it's being measured, because the CT draws negligible power. The water analogy is perfect: large current → small proportional current, with **electrical isolation** (no water leaks back into your cup).

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Standard Secondary Current:** **5 A** (older systems) or **1 A** (modern IEC 61869 standard).
- **Standard Primary Currents:** 10 A, 50 A, 100 A, 400 A, 800 A, 1600 A, up to 50 kA.
- **Standard Burden:** 2.5 VA, 5 VA, 10 VA, 15 VA, 30 VA (CT specification).
- **Accuracy Class:** 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3, 5 (metering); 5P, 10P, PS (protection).

---

### 1.2 Potential Transformer (PT) / Voltage Transformer (VT)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Potential Transformer (PT), also called a Voltage Transformer (VT), is an *instrument transformer* used to step down high voltages to safe, measurable, standardized low voltages that can be fed directly to voltmeters, wattmeters, energy meters, and protective relays, while providing accurate magnitude and phase information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> A PT operates on the **principle of mutual induction**, exactly like a step-down power transformer, but is designed for *measurement accuracy* over a wide range of secondary burdens, with extremely low ratio and phase errors. The primary is connected across the high-voltage line; the secondary delivers a standardized **110 V** or **110/√3 V** (line-to-neutral) for metering and protection.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Acoustic Megaphone-in-Reverse"):**
Imagine someone shouting at 1000 dB in a stadium. You cannot put your ear next to them. Instead, you use a *microphone-attenuator* — a PT — that *silently listens* and produces a tiny, faithful whisper (110 V) in your headphones, perfectly proportional to the original shout, with no time delay. The shout continues unchanged; the listener (voltmeter) hears only a small, exact replica.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Standard Secondary Voltage:** **110 V** (line-to-line) or **110/√3 V ≈ 63.5 V** (line-to-neutral).
- **Standard Primary Voltages:** 11 kV, 33 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV.
- **Standard Burden:** 25 VA, 50 VA, 100 VA, 200 VA.
- **Accuracy Class:** 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3 (metering); 3P, 6P (protection).

---

### 1.3 True RMS Meter

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A True RMS Meter is an *electronic measuring instrument* that accurately measures the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of any periodic or non-periodic, sinusoidal or non-sinusoidal AC waveform, irrespective of its waveshape, crest factor, or harmonic content. Unlike average-responding or peak-responding meters, a true RMS meter computes the mathematically exact heating-equivalent value of the input signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why True RMS is Needed (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> Many practical waveforms (SCR-fired heater outputs, PWM inverter drives, square waves from UPS systems, distorted supplies with harmonics) have a **Form Factor (FF) ≠ 1.11** (the FF of a pure sine wave). Standard moving-iron or rectifier-type meters calibrated for sine waves give **erroneous readings** on such waveforms. True RMS meters solve this by directly computing:
> $$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Equivalent Heater" Test):**
Imagine two AC sources. Source A is a pure sine wave; Source B is a chopped PWM wave. A standard cheap voltmeter might say B is "230 V" because it only looks at the peak. But a heater connected to B would glow dimmer than one connected to A — because B is genuinely doing *less heating work*. A True RMS meter measures exactly what the *heater experiences*, regardless of the waveform's shape. It asks: *"What DC voltage would produce the same heating effect?"* That DC equivalent is the True RMS.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Crest Factor (CF) Tolerance:** Up to **3.0** or **5.0** (for high-quality meters).
- **Bandwidth:** **20 Hz to 100 kHz** (typical) or up to **1 MHz** (precision).
- **Basic Accuracy:** **±0.05% to ±0.5%** of reading.

---

### 1.4 Q-Meter

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Q-Meter (Quality-factor Meter) is a *high-frequency AC bridge-based instrument* designed to measure the **Quality Factor (Q)** of inductors and capacitors, as well as their self-resonant frequency, effective resistance, and inductance/capacitance values, at the *actual operating frequency* of the component under test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> A Q-Meter works on the **principle of series resonance**. The test component (inductor) is connected in series with a known, low-loss variable capacitor, and is excited by a stable, calibrated RF signal generator. At resonance, the voltage across the capacitor (or inductor) becomes **Q times the input voltage**, which is measured by a high-impedance electronic voltmeter. The meter is *directly calibrated in Q*.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Swing Resonance"):**
Imagine pushing a child on a swing at exactly the swing's natural rhythm. Each push adds a *little* energy, but the swing rises very *high* because energy accumulates. The "swing-height-to-push-strength" ratio is exactly the **Q-factor**. A high-Q swing (like a pendulum in a vacuum) rises enormously; a low-Q swing (a swing in muddy water) barely moves. The Q-meter's "push" is the small input voltage; the "swing height" is the measured capacitor voltage; the *ratio* is the Q.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Frequency Range:** **1 kHz to 50 MHz** (typical laboratory Q-meters).
- **Q Measurement Range:** **5 to 1000** (typical), up to **5000** (precision).
- **Inductance Range:** **0.1 µH to 1 H** (with external standards).
- **Capacitance Range:** **30 pF to 500 pF** (internal standard).

---

### 1.5 Strip Chart Recorder

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Strip Chart Recorder is an *analog graphical recording instrument* that produces a continuous, permanent, time-based trace of one or more input signal variations on a long, narrow strip of paper that is fed past a writing element (pen) at a constant, selectable speed. It is a *single-channel (or few-channel) time-domain* recorder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> A strip chart recorder combines three subsystems: (1) a **signal-conditioning** and **amplifier** section that drives the galvanometer, (2) a **chart-drive mechanism** (synchronous motor + gear train) that pulls the paper at speeds like 1, 5, 25, 100 mm/s or 1, 5, 25 mm/min, and (3) a **writing system** (ink pen, thermal stylus, or pressure stylus) attached to a D'Arsonval galvanometer movement. The pen deflects vertically in proportion to the input signal; the paper moves horizontally with time, producing a continuous *y(t)* trace.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "ECG of a Machine"):**
Just as an ECG machine records a patient's heartbeat on a long, moving paper roll — time on the X-axis, voltage on the Y-axis — a strip chart recorder does the same for any slowly varying process variable: temperature in a furnace, pressure in a pipeline, level in a tank, vibration of a turbine. The paper "remembers" what your eyes missed.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Chart Speeds:** **1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100 mm/s** and **mm/min**.
- **Chart Widths:** **100 mm, 120 mm, 250 mm** (roll-fed).
- **Frequency Response:** DC to **10 Hz** (pen recorders), DC to **100 Hz** (thermal/galvanometric).
- **Number of Channels:** **1, 2, 6, 8, 12** (multi-pen).

---

### 1.6 XY Plotter / XY Recorder

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An XY Plotter (or XY Recorder) is a *two-dimensional graphical recording instrument* in which the writing element (pen) is positioned on a *flat sheet of paper* by two independent servomotor-driven axes — one driven by the X input signal, the other by the Y input signal — and *time is NOT one of the axes*. It directly plots **Y as a function of X**, i.e., **Y = f(X)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Principle (KTU High-Yield Fact):**
> An XY plotter uses **two independent servomotor-feedback systems** (one for X-axis, one for Y-axis). Each axis consists of a DC servomotor, a position-feedback potentiometer, and an error amplifier. The input signal drives the error amplifier; the motor rotates until the feedback voltage equals the input, locking the pen carriage at the commanded position. The pen is lowered onto the paper, and the trace Y(X) is produced.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Robotic Hand on Paper"):**
Imagine a robotic pen held by two motors — one motor moves it left/right (X), the other moves it front/back (Y). You give the X-motor a command "go to position 5 cm"; you give the Y-motor "go to position 3 cm"; the pen sits at (5, 3). Now vary X slowly and Y slowly, and the pen *draws the function* Y = f(X) on a stationary paper. No time axis — pure *graph-plotter*.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in bold):**
- **Plotting Area:** A3 (297 × 420 mm) or A4 (210 × 297 mm) or 250 × 250 mm.
- **Slewing Speed:** **1000 mm/s** to **2000 mm/s** (modern plotters).
- **Resolution:** **0.1 mm** or better.
- **Accuracy:** **±0.1% to ±0.3%** of full scale.
- **Input:** ±1 V, ±5 V, ±10 V, 4–20 mA, with selectable range cards.

---

## COMPARATIVE SUMMARY TABLE — AT A GLANCE

| Instrument | Quantity Measured | Output Type | Active Principle | KTU Tag |
|------------|-------------------|-------------|------------------|---------|
| **CT** | AC Current | Proportional current (5 A/1 A) | Mutual induction | Instrumentation Transformer |
| **PT** | AC Voltage | Proportional voltage (110 V) | Mutual induction | Instrumentation Transformer |
| **True RMS Meter** | True RMS of any waveform | DC equivalent | Squaring + averaging + square-root | Signal Analyzer |
| **Q-Meter** | Quality factor Q of L/C | Direct Q reading | Series resonance | AC Bridge |
| **Strip Chart Recorder** | Signal vs Time | Time-domain trace | Galvanometer + moving paper | Time Recorder |
| **XY Plotter** | Signal Y vs Signal X | Function plot Y(X) | Dual servomotor positioning | Function Recorder |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Current Transformer — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Construction & Working

A Current Transformer is a *wound-type* or *bar-type* (window-type) transformer. In practice, the primary of a CT is often **the line conductor itself** passing once through the core window. The secondary has many turns (say, **Ns = 200** for a 1000:5 CT).

- The CT is a **step-down current transformer**: Primary current `Ip` is large; secondary current `Is` is small and standardized.
- The **secondary must NEVER be open-circuited** while primary is energized. An open secondary forces the entire primary MMF (`Np·Ip`) to magnetize the core, producing dangerously high voltages (kV) across the secondary and causing core saturation, overheating, and insulation failure.
- The CT operates in a state of **almost short-circuited secondary**, so the net flux in the core is very small, and the core is designed for low flux density.

### 2.1.2 Phasor Diagram (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

Let:
- `Ip` = primary current (actual line current)
- `Is` = secondary current (measured)
- `Np, Ns` = primary and secondary turns
- `Im` = magnetizing component of no-load current
- `I0` = no-load (excitation) current
- `θ` = phase angle of `I0` w.r.t. flux `Φ`

From the **ampere-turn balance** of an ideal transformer with core loss and magnetizing branches:

$$N_p I_p = N_s I_s + N_p I_0$$

Where `Np·I0` is the *magnetizing ampere-turns* required to produce core flux `Φ`. In a real CT, `I0` is non-zero and leads to both **ratio error** and **phase error**.

### 2.1.3 Errors in CT (KTU High-Yield)

- **Ratio Error:** Defined as the deviation of the actual transformation ratio from the nominal (marked) ratio.
  $$\text{Ratio Error} = \frac{K_n I_s - I_p}{I_p} \times 100\%$$
  Where `Kn = Ns/Np` is the *nominal ratio*. For an ideal CT, `Is = Ip / Kn`, so error = 0.

- **Phase Angle Error (`β`):** The angular difference (in minutes) between the *primary current phasor* and the *reversed secondary current phasor*. For protection CTs (class 5P, 10P), phase error must be small; for metering CTs (class 0.5, 0.2), both errors must be small over the burden range.

- **Burden:** The total impedance (in ohms) of the secondary circuit, expressed in VA. Standard burdens: 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 30 VA at rated `Is` (5 A or 1 A). A larger burden increases the secondary voltage, which increases `I0`, which *worsens* both errors.

### 2.1.4 Why CT Secondary Should Not Be Open

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Open-Circuit Hazard:**
> Under normal operation, `Ns·Is ≈ Np·Ip`, and the core flux is small (since secondary MMF almost cancels primary MMF). If the secondary is opened, `Is = 0`, and the primary MMF `Np·Ip` is unopposed. This huge MMF drives the core deep into **saturation**, causing:
> 1. **High flux → high secondary EMF** (`Es = 4.44 f Ns Φmax`), potentially **kV levels**, dangerous to personnel.
> 2. **Hysteresis and eddy-current heating** → core damage, insulation failure.
> 3. **Severe ratio and phase errors** if the circuit is later closed.

> Therefore, **before disconnecting a CT secondary, it MUST be short-circuited** using the provided shorting link or test block.

---

## 2.2 Potential Transformer — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Construction & Working

A Potential Transformer is essentially a *small, high-accuracy, step-down power transformer* with very precise turns ratio. The primary is connected across the high-voltage line; the secondary delivers a standardized **110 V** (or **110/√3 V**) for measurement instruments.

- The PT is designed to operate with its secondary connected to a **high-impedance voltmeter** — i.e., essentially at *no-load* (or very small burden).
- The primary has many turns of fine wire (for high voltage); the secondary has few turns of thicker wire.
- For voltages > **33 kV**, PTs are often *capacitor voltage transformers (CVTs)*, which use a capacitive voltage divider and an intermediate inductive section. For EHV, CVTs are economically the only option.

### 2.2.2 Equivalent Circuit of PT (KTU Standard)

The PT is reduced to its primary side as a *single-phase transformer* with:
- `R1, X1` = primary winding resistance and leakage reactance
- `R2', X2'` = secondary resistance and reactance referred to primary
- `Rm, Xm` = core-loss resistance and magnetizing reactance (shunt branch)
- `Zb' = (Ns/Np)² Zb` = burden referred to primary

### 2.2.3 Errors in PT (KTU High-Yield)

- **Ratio Error:**
  $$\text{Ratio Error} = \frac{K_n V_s - V_p}{V_p} \times 100\%$$
  Where `Kn = Np/Ns` is the nominal PT ratio.

- **Phase Angle Error:** The angular difference between primary voltage phasor and reversed secondary voltage phasor.

### 2.2.4 Reduction of Errors in PT

1. **Use of high-permeability, low-loss silicon-steel or Mumetal core** to reduce magnetizing current.
2. **Reduction of leakage reactance** by using a *shell-type* construction with interleaved windings.
3. **Careful control of turns ratio** — extra turns are wound on the secondary and tapped for fine adjustment.
4. **Use of a low-burden voltmeter** (high-impedance).
5. **Burden compensation winding** (in some designs) — a small auxiliary winding that injects a corrective voltage.

---

## 2.3 True RMS Meter — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Foundation

The RMS value of a periodic signal `v(t)` of period `T` is defined as the *DC equivalent that produces the same heating in a resistor*:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}$$

For a pure sine wave: `Vrms = Vpeak / √2 = 0.7071 Vpeak`.
For a square wave: `Vrms = Vpeak`.
For a triangular wave: `Vrms = Vpeak / √3`.
For a sine wave with half-wave rectification: `Vrms = Vpeak / 2`.

### 2.3.2 Methods of True RMS Measurement

| Method | Principle | Application |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Electrothermal** | Heater + thermocouple; output ∝ `V²` | Lab standards (true RMS, very accurate) |
| **Analog Multiplier + Filter** | Squaring IC (e.g., AD534) + low-pass filter + sqrt | General-purpose DMMs |
| **Log-Antilog** | `log V² = 2 log V`; uses transistor V-I characteristics | Older DMMs |
| **Direct ADC + Digital Computation** | High-speed ADC samples `v(t)`, computes `Σv²/N`, takes sqrt | Modern DMMs, sampling |
| **Sampling + DSP** | Sample-and-hold + A/D + DSP-based computation | Precision bench meters |

### 2.3.3 Form Factor & Crest Factor (KTU Important)

- **Form Factor (FF):** `FF = Vrms / Vavg` (rectified average). For sine wave, **FF = 1.11**.
- **Crest Factor (CF):** `CF = Vpeak / Vrms`. For sine wave, **CF = √2 ≈ 1.414**.
- For SCR-fired waveforms, CF can be **3 to 5**, and average-responding meters are *dangerously wrong*.

---

## 2.4 Q-Meter — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 Working Principle (Detailed)

The Q-meter is a **series-resonance circuit** consisting of:
- A **low-impedance, variable-frequency signal generator** `Vs` (with very small internal resistance, ideally `R = 0`)
- A **small fixed series resistor** `R` (typically 0.04 Ω) used as a *reference* for current sensing
- A **precision, low-loss variable capacitor** `C` (the tuning capacitor, 30 pF to 500 pF)
- The **inductor under test** `Lx` (unknown)

At **resonance**, the series inductive reactance cancels the series capacitive reactance:
$$\omega L_x = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

The circuit current is:
$$I = \frac{V_s}{R} \quad \text{(purely resistive, since X cancels)}$$

The voltage across the tuning capacitor is:
$$V_c = I \cdot X_c = \frac{V_s}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

The **Quality Factor** of the test inductor is, by definition:
$$Q_x = \frac{\omega L_x}{R_{L,x}} = \frac{1}{\omega C R_{L,x}}$$

But in the Q-meter, since the *only resistance in the loop* (assuming `Vs` source has zero internal resistance and `C` is lossless) is the effective series resistance of the inductor `RL,x`, the voltage magnification at resonance gives:
$$Q_x = \frac{V_c}{V_s} = \frac{1}{\omega C \cdot R_{L,x}} = \frac{\omega L_x}{R_{L,x}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula**. The Q-meter's voltmeter reads `Vc`, and since `Vs` is fixed (e.g., 10 mV to 1 V adjustable), the meter is *directly calibrated in Q*.

### 2.4.2 Measurement Procedure (KTU Lab Manual Standard)

1. Set the signal generator to a desired test frequency `f`.
2. Connect the unknown inductor `Lx` to the test terminals.
3. Vary the tuning capacitor `C` until the voltmeter reads **maximum** — this is the **resonance condition**.
4. Read the **Q** directly from the meter (calibrated in Q).
5. Read the **C** from the calibrated dial; compute `Lx` from:
   $$L_x = \frac{1}{(2\pi f)^2 C}$$
6. (Optional) Read the **effective resistance** of `Lx` from:
   $$R_{L,x} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C Q}$$

### 2.4.3 Sources of Error in Q-Meter

1. **Distributed capacitance of the test inductor** — significant above 1 MHz.
2. **Self-resonance of the test inductor** — gives spurious readings.
3. **Losses in the tuning capacitor** — usually negligible (air or mica dielectric).
4. **Residual resistance of the signal source** — appears in series with `Lx`.

---

## 2.5 Strip Chart Recorder — Deep Theory

### 2.5.1 Major Subsystems (KTU Block-Diagram Question)

A strip chart recorder consists of:

1. **Input Signal Conditioner:** Attenuator + amplifier; provides range selection (mV, V, mA).
2. **Galvanometer (D'Arsonval Movement):** Converts the amplified DC signal into a pen deflection. Limited to **~10 Hz** frequency response.
3. **Pen-and-Ink Writing System:** Capillary pen or felt-tip pen attached to the galvanometer.
4. **Chart-Drive Mechanism:** Synchronous AC motor + multi-step gear train. Provides selectable chart speeds.
5. **Paper Transport:** Roll-feed mechanism with paper-tension springs.
6. **Reference Markers:** Time marks (every 1 s, 1 min) printed on the chart edge.

### 2.5.2 Frequency Limitation & Solutions

A pen recorder has limited frequency response (10 Hz) because the pen has mass. To extend frequency, the *galvanometric* or *ultraviolet* recorder is used:
- **Ultraviolet (UV) Recorder:** Uses a small mirror on a galvanometer reflecting a UV beam onto photosensitive paper. Frequency response up to **5 kHz**.
- **Thermal Array Recorder:** A row of fixed thermal styli, no moving pen. Frequency up to **1 kHz**, but not strip-chart format.

---

## 2.6 XY Plotter — Deep Theory

### 2.6.1 Major Subsystems

1. **X-Axis Servo System:** Input X → error amplifier → DC servomotor → leadscrew → pen carriage moves in X.
2. **Y-Axis Servo System:** Identical to X, but for Y movement.
3. **Pen-Lift Solenoid:** Lifts the pen off paper; lowers for plotting.
4. **Chart Bed:** A flat, ruled graph paper (A3/A4) clamped on a flat platen.
5. **Reference Grid:** Internal reference for `0, ±10 V` scaling.

### 2.6.2 Self-Balancing Potentiometric Action

Each axis of an XY plotter is a **null-type self-balancing potentiometer**:
- The input signal `Vi` is compared with the wiper voltage of a precision feedback potentiometer `Vfb`.
- The error `(Vi - Vfb)` is amplified and drives the servomotor.
- The motor moves the wiper (and the pen) until `Vfb = Vi`, so error = 0.
- This **null-balance principle** gives high linearity and high accuracy (±0.1%).

### 2.6.3 Difference Between Strip Chart and XY Plotter

| Feature | Strip Chart Recorder | XY Plotter |
|---------|---------------------|-----------|
| **X-Axis** | **Time** (fixed) | **Independent signal** (variable) |
| **Y-Axis** | Input signal | Input signal |
| **Paper** | Long roll (strip) | Flat sheet (A3/A4) |
| **Trace** | Y(t) | Y(X) |
| **Drives** | One galvanometer + chart motor | Two servo systems |
| **Use** | Continuous monitoring | B-H curves, diode V-I, etc. |

---

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | CT nominal ratio | `Kn = Ns / Np` | — | Step-down CT |
| 2 | CT actual ratio | `Kr = Ip / Is` | — | Measured at burden |
| 3 | CT ratio error | `εr = (Kn·Is − Ip) / Ip × 100%` | % | KTU 14-mark favourite |
| 4 | CT secondary EMF | `Es = 4.44 f Ns Φmax` | V | Used in open-circuit analysis |
| 5 | PT nominal ratio | `Kn = Np / Ns` | — | Step-down PT |
| 6 | PT ratio error | `εr = (Kn·Vs − Vp) / Vp × 100%` | % | — |
| 7 | Standard secondary current | `Is = 5 A` or `1 A` | A | IEC 61869 |
| 8 | Standard secondary voltage | `Vs = 110 V` or `110/√3 V` | V | IEC 61869 |
| 9 | RMS of any waveform | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\,dt}$ | V | KTU 14-mark derivation |
| 10 | RMS of sine wave | `Vrms = Vpeak / √2` | V | — |
| 11 | Form factor | `FF = Vrms / Vavg` | — | Sine = 1.11 |
| 12 | Crest factor | `CF = Vpeak / Vrms` | — | Sine = 1.414 |
| 13 | Q-factor (inductor) | `Q = ωL / RL` | — | KTU core formula |
| 14 | Q-factor (capacitor) | `Q = 1 / (ωRC)` | — | Equivalent |
| 15 | Q at resonance (Q-meter) | `Q = Vc / Vs` | — | Q-meter reading |
| 16 | Resonance frequency | `ωo = 1 / √(LC)` | rad/s | — |
| 17 | Test inductance from Q-meter | `Lx = 1 / (2πf)² C` | H | KTU lab |
| 18 | Effective resistance of Lx | `RL,x = 1 / (2πf C Q)` | Ω | KTU lab |
| 19 | Burden (general) | `B = Is² · Zb` | VA | CT/PT |
| 20 | Strip chart pen deflection | `y = k · Vi` | mm | Linear range |
| 21 | XY plotter self-balance | `Vi = Vfb` at null | V | Steady state |
| 22 | CT open-circuit secondary voltage | `Es = (Ip / Kn) · Zb` (very high if Zb → ∞) | V | Hazard analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Safety Note:**
> In the table, the `√2` and `√3` symbols are written inline; in the rendered exam, use the bar form `$\sqrt{2}$` to prevent markdown mis-parsing. Also, write `Vrms` (not `V|rms|`) — never use the vertical bar inside a markdown table.
